description: roster/switchboard/api/router.py get_ingestion_fanout: when async_query returns [] (empty list, no data from Prometheus), the guard 'if results and not (isinstance(results[0], dict) and 'error' in results[0])' is False (because results is falsy), so execution falls through to 'logger.warning(..., results[0]["error"])' which triggers IndexError: list index out of range. Fix: add an explicit empty-list guard before accessing results[0] in the warning.

description: PR #836 review finding: _check_duplicate issues one SELECT per row before each INSERT. For 1000-row imports this is 1000 SELECT + up to 1000 INSERT round-trips. acceptance_criteria:
1. Every session row has a non-null UUID7 request_id
2. Connector-sourced sessions also have ingestion_event_id FK referencing shared.ingestion_events
3. Internally-triggered sessions (tick/schedule/trigger) have request_id but ingestion_event_id=NULL
4. GET /api/ingestion/events/{requestId}/sessions returns cross-butler lineage
5. GET /api/ingestion/events/{requestId}/rollup returns token/cost/butler topology
6. /traces and /traces/:traceId redirect to /ingestion?tab=timeline
7. Timeline tab on /ingestion shows ingestion events with expandable session lineage
8. g-then-r keybinding removed from dashboard
description:
Every connector ingestion event already receives a UUID7 request_id minted by the Switchboard, and sessions already carry it — but it is a bare UUID with no backing table. There is no record of what the event actually was, so we cannot retroactively answer: what was the source of this session? How many tokens did this single inbound event consume across all butlers? Which butlers were invoked and at what cost?

This epic anchors request_id to a first-class shared.ingestion_events table and promotes it to a non-null invariant on every session (connector-sourced or internal). The dashboard /traces page is retired in favour of a unified Timeline tab under /ingestion, keyed on request_id rather than trace_id.

Spec: openspec/changes/connector-ingestion-request-id/
Design decisions: two columns on sessions (request_id always non-null, ingestion_event_id nullable FK); same UUID7 used as both message_inbox.id and shared.ingestion_events.id; spawner mints for internal sessions; lineage via API fan-out.
title: Connector ingestion request ID: unified event provenance

description:
Deep-dive review: compare the original spec/requirements (openspec/changes/connector-ingestion-request-id/) against the implementation delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

Workflow:
1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Audit the codebase changes delivered by each sibling bead.
3. Produce a checklist mapping every spec requirement to its implementing bead.
4. For any requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
   c. Do NOT create another reconciliation bead.
5. Keep this reconciliation bead open (or blocked) until all newly created gap beads are closed.
6. Re-run the requirement-to-bead checklist and close this bead only when all requirements show full coverage.

description:
Three Alembic migrations are needed:

1. Create shared.ingestion_events table with columns: id (UUID7 PK), received_at (timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()), source_channel, source_provider, source_endpoint_identity, source_sender_identity (nullable), source_thread_identity (nullable), external_event_id, dedupe_key (UNIQUE), dedupe_strategy, ingestion_tier, policy_tier, triage_decision (nullable), triage_target (nullable).

2. Add ingestion_event_id UUID REFERENCES shared.ingestion_events(id) (nullable) to all butler sessions tables.

3. Set sessions.request_id NOT NULL — backfill any existing NULL rows with gen_random_uuid() before applying the constraint.

Run in order; each is a separate Alembic revision for clean rollback.
title: Migrate shared.ingestion_events table and sessions FK columns

acceptance_criteria:
1. Accepting a new ingest envelope creates a row in both message_inbox and shared.ingestion_events with the same UUID7 id
2. Duplicate submission (same dedupe_key) does NOT create a second shared.ingestion_events row
3. Returned request_id equals shared.ingestion_events.id
4. The dedup transaction is not materially slower (both inserts are single-row writes in the same lock window)
description:
In pipeline.py, inside the advisory-lock dedup transaction (_handle_ingest_accept), after INSERT INTO message_inbox, also insert a row into shared.ingestion_events using the same UUID7.

Key invariants:
- Both inserts happen atomically inside the same transaction and advisory lock window
- On duplicate detection (inserted=false), skip the shared.ingestion_events insert (existing row already present)
- The Switchboard's DB pool already has access to the shared schema — no new pool needed
- Fields to populate from the envelope: source_channel, source_provider, source_endpoint_identity, source_sender_identity, source_thread_identity (from external_thread_id), external_event_id, dedupe_key, dedupe_strategy, ingestion_tier, policy_tier, triage_decision, triage_target
title: Write ingestion_events row in Switchboard pipeline dedup transaction

description:
Create src/butlers/core/ingestion_events.py with four query functions:

ingestion_event_get(pool, event_id) -> dict | None
  SELECT * FROM shared.ingestion_events WHERE id = 

ingestion_events_list(pool, limit=20, offset=0, source_channel=None) -> list[dict]
  ORDER BY received_at DESC; optional WHERE source_channel =  filter

ingestion_event_sessions(pool, request_id) -> list[dict]
  Fan-out across all butler schemas: for each butler, SELECT sessions WHERE request_id = .
  Returns rows with butler_name, id, trigger_source, started_at, completed_at, success, input_tokens, output_tokens, cost, trace_id. Use existing DatabaseManager fan-out pattern (matches cross-butler session list).

ingestion_event_rollup(pool, request_id) -> dict
  Aggregates from the fan-out sessions result: total_sessions, total_input_tokens, total_output_tokens, total_cost (sum cost->>'total_usd' from JSONB), by_butler breakdown.
  Use the cost JSONB field on sessions (already computed by spawner) as the cost source.
title: Implement ingestion_events query module (get, list, lineage, rollup)

description: In overview.py net_worth_history(), the carry-forward loop for missing periods scans backwards through all prior periods for each missing account+period combination. This is O(accounts*months^2). A forward-pass approach maintaining per-account last_seen state would be O(accounts*months). Identified during PR #796 review (Copilot feedback). Original: roster/finance/tools/overview.py, carry-forward logic starting around line 373.
title: Optimize net_worth_history carry-forward from O(accounts*months^2) to O(accounts*months)

description:
The switchboard butler's notifications table migration exists (roster/switchboard/migrations/003_add_notifications_table.py) but the table is absent when the switchboard daemon hasn't been started.

Observed in logs/20260307_153655/uvicorn/dashboard.log when no butler daemons were running:
  UndefinedTableError: relation "notifications" does not exist
  Unhandled exception on GET /api/notifications

Root cause: butler daemons run Alembic migrations on startup. When the dashboard starts without daemons, all butler schemas are empty and the dashboard returns 500 on every API call that touches those tables.

This bead tracks whether the dashboard should degrade gracefully (return 503/empty results) rather than throwing unhandled 500s when the switchboard schema is not yet initialized.
title: Dashboard returns 500 on /api/notifications when switchboard daemon not running

description: The memory-discovery-catalog spec requires shared.memory_catalog to have columns: title (nullable), predicate (nullable), scope (nullable), valid_at (TIMESTAMPTZ nullable), invalid_at (TIMESTAMPTZ nullable), confidence (DOUBLE PRECISION nullable), importance (DOUBLE PRECISION nullable), retention_class (TEXT nullable), sensitivity (TEXT nullable), object_entity_id (UUID nullable FK to shared.entities). The core_023 migration only created a simplified schema (summary, embedding, search_vector, memory_type, source provenance, entity_id). Additionally, the catalog upsert in storage.py writes only summary/embedding/search_vector but not predicate, scope, confidence, importance, sensitivity, or object_entity_id. Needs: (1) a follow-on migration to add the missing columns, (2) _upsert_catalog() updated to write the additional fields from the source fact/rule row.
title: Expand shared.memory_catalog schema to include spec-required columns (title, predicate, scope, valid_at, confidence, etc.)

description:
The connector-filtered-events spec requires that 'the partition for the event's received_at month SHALL exist or be auto-created'. The core_027 migration creates current-month and next-month partitions at migration time, but there is no ongoing mechanism to create partitions for subsequent months.

The FilteredEventBuffer.flush() does NOT call connectors_filtered_events_ensure_partition() before inserting. If a month passes without the function being called, inserts will fail with 'no partition of relation filtered_events found for row'.

The switchboard already has this solved for message_inbox (calls switchboard_message_inbox_ensure_partition on every insert) and sw_030 also proactively creates next month. The connectors.filtered_events table needs the same treatment.

Options:
1. Call SELECT connectors_filtered_events_ensure_partition(now()) inside FilteredEventBuffer.flush() before the batch INSERT (similar to switchboard ingest.py pattern). Note: ensure_partition uses DDL (CREATE TABLE) which must run on an autocommit connection, not inside a transaction.
2. Create a scheduled connector task that calls the ensure_partition function monthly (e.g., at connector startup or on a timer).
3. Update connectors_filtered_events_ensure_partition() to proactively create next month's partition too (same pattern as sw_030 did for switchboard tables).

Option 3 is the simplest and most resilient -- pair it with a connector startup call to ensure_partition.

Reference: openspec/changes/add-connectors-schema/specs/connector-filtered-events/spec.md (Monthly partitioning scenario)
title: Add ongoing partition maintenance for connectors.filtered_events

description:
Extend the model routing layer with three changes: (1) resolve_model returns catalog_entry_id, (2) check_token_quota function, (3) record_token_usage function. All changes are in src/butlers/core/model_routing.py with corresponding test updates.

## What

### resolve_model 4-tuple (tasks 2.1-2.5)
- Add `mc.id` to `_RESOLVE_SQL` SELECT list
- Change return type from `tuple[str, str, list[str]]` to `tuple[str, str, list[str], UUID]`
- Update spawner `_run_session()` to unpack 4-element tuple (propagate catalog_entry_id but don't wire enforcement yet — that's a separate bead)
- Update `DiscretionDispatcher.call()` to unpack 4-element tuple
- Update all existing resolve_model tests to expect 4-tuple

### check_token_quota (tasks 3.1-3.4)
- Add `QuotaStatus` dataclass: `allowed`, `usage_24h`, `limit_24h`, `usage_30d`, `limit_30d`
- Implement CTE-based single-query for both windows, respecting independent `reset_24h_at`/`reset_30d_at`
- Fast path: no limits row → return `QuotaStatus(allowed=True, usage=0, limits=None)` without querying ledger
- Fail-open: DB errors → return allowed=True, log warning
- Unit tests: no limits (fast path), within limits, 24h exceeded, 30d exceeded, one unlimited + other exceeded, reset markers, DB error fail-open

### record_token_usage (tasks 4.1, 4.4)
- Simple INSERT into ledger, best-effort with try/except (never blocks caller)
- Unit tests: success path, missing partition handled gracefully, no-op when catalog_entry_id is None

## OpenSpec References
- **Spec**: `openspec/changes/catalog-tier-token-limits/specs/model-catalog/spec.md`
  - MODIFIED Requirement: "Model Resolution" — scenarios for 4-tuple return type, especially "Return type includes catalog_entry_id"
- **Spec**: `openspec/changes/catalog-tier-token-limits/specs/catalog-token-limits/spec.md`
  - Requirement: "Pre-Spawn Quota Check" — all 8 scenarios (QuotaStatus, no limits fast path, within limits, exceeded, fail-open)
  - Requirement: "Post-Spawn Ledger Recording" — scenarios for best-effort recording, no-op cases
- **Design**: `openspec/changes/catalog-tier-token-limits/design.md`
  - Decision D4 — resolve_model 4-tuple rationale and migration notes
  - Decision D5 — check_token_quota with full CTE SQL and QuotaStatus definition
  - Decision D6 — record_token_usage as best-effort INSERT

## Acceptance Criteria
1. resolve_model() returns `tuple[str, str, list[str], UUID]` with the catalog entry's id
2. Both callers (spawner, discretion dispatcher) updated to unpack 4 elements
3. All existing resolve_model tests pass with 4-tuple expectations
4. check_token_quota returns QuotaStatus with correct allowed/usage/limit values for all scenarios
5. check_token_quota skips ledger query when no limits row exists (fast path)
6. check_token_quota returns allowed=True on any DB error (fail-open)
7. record_token_usage inserts to ledger, catches and logs errors without propagating
8. Verify implementation matches all scenarios in cited spec sections
title: Implement resolve_model 4-tuple, quota check, and ledger recording functions

description:
Deep-dive review: compare the original spec/requirements from the OpenSpec change against the implementation delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

## Workflow
1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Re-read all OpenSpec artifacts: `openspec/changes/transaction-csv-extraction/{proposal,design,specs/**,tasks}.md`
3. Audit the codebase changes delivered by each sibling bead.
4. Produce a checklist mapping every spec requirement to its implementing bead/code.
5. For any requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
6. If gap beads were created in step 5, create a follow-up reconciliation bead (gen-2) that depends on ALL the new gap beads.
7. Keep this bead open until all newly created gap beads are closed.
8. Re-run the requirement-to-bead checklist and close this bead only when all requirements show full coverage.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Every requirement in all 3 spec files has a corresponding implementation in the codebase
2. Any gaps found result in new child beads created under the same epic
3. If gaps were found, a follow-up reconciliation bead (gen-2) was created
4. Reconciliation summary is recorded in the close reason
notes:

description:
Data sanitization pipeline that scrubs PII, credentials, user content, and
environment-specific paths from error context before PR creation. Includes a
mandatory validation pass that blocks PR creation if residual sensitive patterns
are detected. This is the hard safety gate for a public repository.

## Work Items (tasks.md §5)
1. Implement src/butlers/core/healing/anonymizer.py — anonymize(text, repo_root)
   and validate_anonymized(text)
2. Credential redaction: API keys, DB URLs, JWT tokens, Telegram/Bearer patterns
3. PII scrubbing: emails (case-insensitive), phone numbers, IPv4/IPv6 (preserve
   localhost/loopback)
4. Path normalization: absolute→repo-relative, non-repo→[REDACTED-PATH]
5. Environment scrubbing: hostnames, env var values
6. Validation pass: residual pattern scan, return (bool, list[str]) with type,
   offset, anonymized surrounding context
7. False positive guards: code identifiers, version strings, git SHAs
8. Tests for each redaction category, validation, false positives, case sensitivity

## Covers
- openspec/changes/butler-self-healing/specs/healing-anonymizer/spec.md — ALL requirements:
  → Credential Redaction, PII Scrubbing, Path Normalization, User Content Exclusion,
    Environment Scrubbing, Validation Pass, False Positive Handling,
    Anonymizer Function Signature
- openspec/changes/butler-self-healing/design.md §7 (Anonymizer)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. anonymize() scrubs all credential patterns (API keys, DB URLs, JWTs, Bearer tokens)
2. PII scrubbing is case-insensitive for emails; localhost/loopback IPs preserved
3. Absolute paths normalized to repo-relative; non-repo paths redacted
4. validate_anonymized() detects residual sensitive patterns with type + offset detail
5. False positive guards: code identifiers, version strings, git SHAs not scrubbed
6. Session prompt/output content never included in anonymized output
7. Verify implementation matches all scenarios in healing-anonymizer/spec.md
title: Implement healing anonymizer with PII scrubbing and validation

description:
Upgrade predicate search from simple prefix+substring to three-signal hybrid retrieval with RRF fusion. Also adds usage_count tracking.

**Schema migration:**
- CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm
- Add search_vector tsvector column with auto-update trigger: setweight(to_tsvector('english', name), 'A') || setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(description, '')), 'B')
- Add description_embedding vector(384) column
- Add usage_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0 and last_used_at TIMESTAMPTZ columns
- Create GIN index on name using gin_trgm_ops, GIN index on search_vector
- Backfill search_vector for existing predicates; generate description_embedding for non-NULL descriptions

**Enrich seed descriptions:**
- Update seeded predicates with synonyms and related concepts for semantic discoverability

**Replace predicate_search() implementation:**
- Signal 1: Trigram fuzzy on name (pg_trgm similarity > 0.3)
- Signal 2: Full-text on search_vector (ts_rank)
- Signal 3: Semantic on description_embedding (cosine similarity)
- Fuse via RRF: score = SUM(1 / (60 + rank_i))
- Results include score field, ordered by fused score DESC

**Usage tracking:**
- Increment usage_count and last_used_at in store_fact() after successful write
- Use usage_count as a tiebreaker signal in search ranking

Spec: openspec/changes/predicate-registry-enforcement/specs/predicate-enforcement/spec.md — 'Predicate registry description and search indexes', 'Predicate search MCP tool with hybrid retrieval'
Design: openspec/changes/predicate-registry-enforcement/design.md — D6
Tasks: 7.1–7.8 from tasks.md
title: Upgrade predicate search to hybrid retrieval with RRF fusion

description:
PR #889 (bu-26ju temporal-intelligence schema migrations) has 3 critical merge blockers that must be resolved before merge:

1. **core_013 revision ID collision**: core_013_scheduled_tasks_deadline_columns.py uses revision='core_013', but core_013_event_chains.py already on main also uses revision='core_013'. Alembic will refuse to run upgrade with two heads.

2. **Duplicate event_chains table**: core_014_event_chains.py creates event_chains table, but core_013_event_chains.py on main already creates it (different DDL approach). The PR's version uses search_path + inline UNIQUE constraint; main's version loops _BUTLER_SCHEMAS with explicit schema-qualified DDL + per-role GRANTs + separate UNIQUE index.

3. **Duplicate seasonal_periods table**: core_015_seasonal_periods.py is functionally identical to core_041_seasonal_periods.py already on main (same DDL, same index names, same constraint names).

Resolution options to evaluate:
- If the intention is to supersede core_013_event_chains.py and core_041_seasonal_periods.py with search_path-based approach, those files must be removed/replaced before this PR lands.
- If those files are canonical, PR #889 should drop core_014_event_chains.py and core_015_seasonal_periods.py entirely, and only add core_013_scheduled_tasks_deadline_columns.py renumbered to avoid the collision.
- Coordinate with the team to agree on the migration numbering strategy (sequential vs sparse).
title: Resolve migration numbering conflicts for PR #889 (bu-26ju)

description:
## What

Rewrite connector tests from 51 files / 2,284 tests (in tests/connectors/) PLUS
root-level connector files (~331 tests) to ~150 tests focused on envelope output
contracts and error handling, not mock wiring.

## Why

Connector tests are the second-largest bloat area. The top offenders:
- test_google_drive_connector.py: 221 tests, 357 asserts (tests/connectors/)
- test_gmail_connector.py: 207 tests, 353 asserts (tests/ ROOT — must be relocated)
- test_telegram_user_client.py: 163 tests, 258 asserts (tests/connectors/)
- test_spotify_client.py: 150 tests, 168 asserts (tests/connectors/)

Root-level connector files to claim and relocate:
- tests/test_gmail_connector.py (207 tests)
- tests/test_whatsapp_user_client.py (75 tests)
- tests/test_google_calendar_connector.py (49 tests)

These files mock ALL external API calls and then test that mocked responses
produce expected outputs. This proves mock wiring, not connector behavior.

## Strategy

Per connector, keep exactly:
1. **One happy-path test** proving correct ingest.v1 envelope output shape (RFC 0003)
2. **One error-path test** proving graceful degradation (retries, backoff, error envelope)
3. **One config/discovery test** proving multi-account setup works
4. **Edge cases** only where the connector has connector-specific logic (e.g.,
   Google Drive change type detection, Gmail thread affinity)

**Connector-as-transport invariant** (vision.md Rule 7): Verify connectors do NOT
contain classification, routing, or domain logic. Only envelope normalization.

Delete:
- Tests asserting internal function return values
- Tests asserting mock call counts/args (2,810 mock-wiring assertions project-wide)
- Tests asserting error message strings
- Duplicate tests for the same behavior with different inputs (use parametrize)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Connector tests reduced to ≤150 tests across ≤15 files
2. Each connector has happy-path, error-path, and config tests
3. All tests validate envelope output shape (ingest.v1 schema from RFC 0003)
4. No tests assert mock call counts or internal function return values
5. Root-level connector files relocated into tests/connectors/
6. Full test suite still passes
title: Condense connector tests from 2,284 to ~150

close_reason: Non-overdue zero-context filter bugfix merged to main via direct merge
description:
Spec requirement: 'WHEN a contact is not overdue, THEN it MUST have urgency 0.0 AND MUST only appear in suggestions if they have a non-zero context bonus' (dunbar-tier-scoring/spec.md, Requirement: Unified urgency ranking, Scenario: Non-overdue contacts excluded from urgency ranking).

Current implementation: compute_urgency() includes all eligible contacts in the result list, even non-overdue contacts with zero context bonus (urgency=0.0). The spec requires these contacts to be filtered OUT of the results entirely.

Impact: Minor behavioral gap — callers like the reconnect-planner skill implicitly skip 0.0 urgency items when sorting/selecting top-N, but the API surface (compute_urgency return value) does not enforce the exclusion contract. A future consumer could unintentionally include 0-urgency contacts.

Fix: Add a filter step in compute_urgency() before the final sort: exclude entries where days_overdue == 0 and context_bonus == 0.

File: roster/relationship/tools/dunbar.py, compute_urgency() function, after context bonus calculation.
title: compute_urgency: non-overdue zero-context contacts should be filtered from results

description:
## What

`_extract_body_from_payload()` in `src/butlers/connectors/gmail.py` (line 1981) recurses into `parts` for any multipart type, but does not account for the structure of `multipart/signed` emails. In S/MIME-signed messages, the MIME tree looks like:

```
multipart/signed
├── multipart/alternative        ← actual body (text/plain + text/html)
│   ├── text/plain
│   └── text/html
└── application/pkcs7-signature  ← detached signature
```

The current code *should* recurse into the first part and find `text/plain`. However, if Gmail presents the signature part first, or if the signed wrapper uses `application/pkcs7-mime` (opaque S/MIME where the body is *inside* the PKCS envelope), the body extraction will fail and return `"(no body)"`.

## Why

Banks, government agencies, insurance companies, and enterprise senders commonly use S/MIME-signed email. These are exactly the high-value transactional messages (statements, policy documents, tax notices) that the finance and general butlers need to ingest correctly.

## Context

- **File**: `src/butlers/connectors/gmail.py`, method `_extract_body_from_payload()` (lines 1981-2002)
- **Two S/MIME variants**:
  1. `multipart/signed` (detached signature) — body is a sibling part; current recursion may work if ordering is favorable, but is not guaranteed.
  2. `application/pkcs7-mime` (opaque/enveloped) — body is wrapped inside the PKCS blob; extraction is impossible without decoding the PKCS structure (out of scope, but should be detected and logged).
- **No tests exist** for either S/MIME variant.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. For `multipart/signed` emails with detached signatures: body text is extracted from the content part (first child), ignoring the signature part (`application/pkcs7-signature`, `application/pgp-signature`).
2. For `application/pkcs7-mime` (opaque S/MIME): body extraction returns a descriptive fallback like `"(S/MIME encrypted body — cannot extract)"` instead of the generic `"(no body)"`, and a warning is logged.
3. Existing non-signed email behavior is unchanged.
4. New tests cover both S/MIME variants.

## Design

1. In the multipart recursion loop, skip parts with MIME types matching `application/pkcs7-signature` or `application/pgp-signature` — these are signature blobs, not body content.
2. When the top-level MIME type is `application/pkcs7-mime` (opaque S/MIME), return a descriptive fallback string and log a warning, since decoding requires crypto libraries outside our scope.
3. No new dependencies needed — this is just smarter part filtering in the existing recursion.

## Notes

- The detached-signature fix is simple (filter out signature parts). The opaque S/MIME case is a graceful degradation — we cannot extract the body without `cryptography` or `openssl`, but we can at least surface a clear signal instead of `"(no body)"`.
- Gmail may pre-process S/MIME in some cases; verify with real S/MIME test messages whether Gmail API already unwraps the signed content.
title: Fix Gmail body extraction for multipart/signed (S/MIME) emails

PostgreSQL will raise an error if 'shared' schema does not exist when a connection is established with that search_path.

Fix: change 'shared,public' to 'public' in both connectors, or use schema_search_path() with the appropriate schema env var (similar to how gmail.py uses BUTLER_SHARED_DB_SCHEMA defaulting to 'public').

Files to change:
- src/butlers/connectors/telegram_bot.py:1164
- src/butlers/connectors/discord_user.py:1309

Tests to update: tests/connectors/ for both connectors if search_path is asserted.

Acceptance criteria:
1. telegram_bot.py no longer uses hardcoded 'shared,public' search_path
2. discord_user.py no longer uses hardcoded 'shared,public' search_path
3. Both connectors resolve credentials correctly when shared schema doesn't exist
4. No 'shared' in any search_path in production connector code
title: Fix hardcoded search_path 'shared,public' in telegram_bot and discord_user connectors

close_reason: Merged to main. Conversation decomposition pipeline implemented with signal extraction, fan-out routing, and 22 tests.
description:
Implement the conversation decomposition step in pipeline.process() that triggers when
control.payload_type=="conversation_history". Uses signal-extraction (direct LLM API call)
to decompose conversation batches into per-butler conceptual messages with cherry-picked
excerpts, then fans out via route() to multiple butlers.

**What changes:**
1. payload_type detection in pipeline.process(): after triage decision handling, check for
   "conversation_history" and branch into _decompose_conversation()
2. _decompose_conversation() method: load signal-extraction prompt template + butler schemas
   from roster/switchboard/.agents/skills/signal-extraction/, invoke LLM API directly, parse
   JSON array response
3. Cherry-pick excerpt assembly: for each signal, select relevant messages from
   payload.raw.conversation_history, build {signal_type, target_butler, tool_name, tool_args,
   excerpts[], confidence}
4. Fan-out routing: iterate conceptual messages, call route() per target butler, accumulate
   dispatch_outcomes
5. Empty decomposition: when extraction returns [], set decomposition_output to
   {"signals":[],"reason":"no_signals_extracted"}, lifecycle_state to "decomposed_empty",
   emit butlers.pipeline.decomposition_empty metric. NO LLM classification invoked.
6. Store full extraction result + metadata (model, latency_ms, token_usage) in
   decomposition_output JSONB on message_inbox

**Covers:**
- openspec/changes/batch-conversation-connectors/specs/conversation-decomposition/spec.md: ALL requirements
- openspec/changes/batch-conversation-connectors/specs/module-pipeline/spec.md: ALL requirements

**Key files:**
- src/butlers/modules/pipeline.py (pipeline.process() ~L1053-1400, classification branch)
- roster/switchboard/tools/routing/route.py (route() function)
- roster/switchboard/.agents/skills/signal-extraction/SKILL.md (prompt contract)
- roster/switchboard/tools/ingestion/ingest.py (decomposition_output field reference)
title: Add conversation decomposition branch to switchboard pipeline

close_reason: PR #706 merged after PR review. Dunbar scoring engine implementation complete.
description:
Implement the foundational scoring algorithms for Dunbar tier assignment.

Covers: openspec/changes/dunbar-tier-scoring/specs/dunbar-tier-scoring/spec.md
  - Requirement: Decay score computation from interaction history
  - Requirement: Scoring eligibility — entity-contact linkage
  - Requirement: Rank-based Dunbar tier assignment
  - Requirement: Tier-aware cadence thresholds
  - Requirement: Unified urgency ranking for reach-out suggestions

Implementation (per design.md D1-D6):
1. compute_dunbar_scores(pool) — query all listed contacts' interaction facts,
   compute exponential decay scores (lambda=ln(2)/30, sum active interaction facts)
2. get_tier_ranking(scores, overrides) — sort by score, apply rank-based tier
   assignment (5/15/50/150/500/1500), apply hysteresis (2-rank buffer downward),
   merge manual overrides
3. Eligibility filtering: exclude listed=false, exclude entities without linked
   contact, return null for ineligible
4. Tier constants: cadence map (5→14d, 15→21d, 50→45d, 150→120d, 500→270d),
   weight map (5→5.0, 15→3.0, 50→2.0, 150→1.0, 500→0.5)
5. compute_urgency() — (days_overdue/cadence)*tier_weight + context_bonus,
   with stay_in_touch_days override, tier 1500 exclusion
6. Context bonus queries: +2.0 upcoming date 14d, +1.0 pending gift, +0.5 positive note

Place functions in roster/relationship/tools/ (new module or extend existing).
Unit tests for all scoring paths.
title: Implement core Dunbar scoring engine and urgency ranking

Contact-level channel identifiers stay on contact_info.
title: Move owner secrets from contact_info to entity_info

description:
Create Alembic migration adding shared.entity_info table parallel to contact_info
but FK'd to entities instead of contacts.

Schema:
  id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
  entity_id   UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES shared.entities(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
  type        VARCHAR NOT NULL
  value       TEXT NOT NULL
  label       VARCHAR
  is_primary  BOOLEAN DEFAULT false
  secured     BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
  UNIQUE (entity_id, type)  -- one value per type per entity

Key differences from contact_info:
- FK to entities (not contacts)
- UNIQUE on (entity_id, type) not (type, value) — entity credentials are
  identity-scoped, not globally unique channel identifiers
- No parent_id column (entity secrets don't nest)

Files to modify:
- New: alembic/versions/core/core_0XX_entity_info.py
title: Create shared.entity_info table

description:
TelegramContactsProvider.full_sync() and incremental_sync() call client.get_contacts() (telegram_provider.py:151, :189) but Telethon 1.42.0's TelegramClient has no such method. The contacts sync poll loop catches the AttributeError and retries every 900s, permanently failing.

The correct Telethon API is the raw request: client(GetContactsRequest(hash=0)) which returns a Contacts object with a .users list.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Start the relationship butler with contacts module + Telegram provider enabled
2. Wait for the contacts sync poll cycle (or trigger manually)
3. Observe: 'TelegramClient' object has no attribute 'get_contacts'

Expected: Contacts sync completes and populates shared.contacts
Actual: AttributeError on every cycle, sync never succeeds
title: Fix TelegramContactsProvider using non-existent get_contacts() method

description:
Update pricing configuration and loader support for GPT-5.4's context-specific rates.

Requested rates:
- GPT-5.4 (<272K context): input $2.50/M, cached input $0.25/M, output $15.00/M
- GPT-5.4 (>272K context): input $5.00/M, cached input $0.50/M, output $22.50/M

Current pricing loading in src/butlers/api/pricing.py assumes a flat per-model input/output shape and does not represent context tiers or cached-input pricing. We need a compatible config/data-model update so pricing data can express this accurately without breaking existing flat-priced models.
title: Add GPT-5.4 context-tier pricing configuration support

close_reason: All three Telegram credentials (telegram_api_id, telegram_api_hash, telegram_user_session) set on owner entity via dashboard. Connector successfully connects and is live.
description:
The telegram user-client connector (src/butlers/connectors/telegram_user_client.py) crashes at startup because the owner entity_info is missing required Telegram credentials:

  07:37:02 [warning] Telegram user-client connector: missing credential types in owner entity_info: ['TELEGRAM_API_HASH', 'TELEGRAM_API_ID', 'TELEGRAM_USER_SESSION']
  RuntimeError: Telegram user-client connector: could not resolve credentials from owner entity_info. Configure telegram_api_id, telegram_api_hash, and telegram_user_session on the owner entity via the dashboard.

Observed in logs/20260307_153655/connectors/telegram_user_client.log.

This is a configuration/setup task, not a code bug. The connector correctly validates credentials on startup and reports clearly what's missing.

Steps to resolve:
1. Obtain Telegram API credentials: https://my.telegram.org/apps
2. Generate a user session string using the Telegram SDK (or the connector's setup utility if one exists)
3. Navigate to the dashboard → Owner entity → Credentials
4. Set TELEGRAM_API_ID, TELEGRAM_API_HASH, TELEGRAM_USER_SESSION on the owner entity_info

Acceptance criteria:
1. Telegram user-client connector starts without RuntimeError
2. Connector polls Telegram and ingests messages as expected
notes: Coordinator note: This requires human action — obtaining Telegram API credentials from https://my.telegram.org/apps and configuring via dashboard. Cannot be automated.
title: Configure Telegram user-client credentials via dashboard

description: bu-g4r's acceptance criteria says 'Replace the refinery's full-suite pytest invocation with a scoped runner.' The scoped_runner.py module (plan_scoped_tests, build_pytest_command, run_scoped_tests) exists as a standalone library but nothing in the refinery patrol (mol-refinery-patrol) calls it. The refinery patrol uses its molecule variable test_command (default: 'go test ./...') directly. Wire run_scoped_tests() into the refinery's test execution step so that MR branches are tested with scoped selection instead of the raw test_command. This is the critical integration gap — without it, the entire scoped testing infrastructure is unused.
title: Integrate scoped test runner into refinery patrol test execution

close_reason: Butler-health spec updated to permit direct SQL for meal_history/nutrition_summary (direct-merge)
description:
Spec gap found in bu-axm.7 reconciliation audit.

The butler-health spec requires (spec.md line 79):
  'when meal_history is called to retrieve meal observations
   THEN it MUST internally call memory_search or memory_recall with scope=health and predicate filters'

Actual implementation in roster/health/tools/diet.py::meal_history() uses direct asyncpg SQL:
  pool.fetch('SELECT id, predicate, content, valid_at, created_at, metadata FROM facts WHERE predicate = ANY($1) AND validity = active AND scope = health ...')

Similarly nutrition_summary() uses direct pool.fetch SQL on facts.

Impact: Bypasses the memory module retrieval layer including:
- Reference counting (reference_count not incremented, last_referenced_at not updated)
- Composite scoring (relevance, importance, recency, confidence weighting)
- Semantic search (pgvector embeddings not used for meal_history)
- Decay-based confidence filtering (min_confidence threshold not applied)

This is partially intentional: the diet.py tools use direct SQL for precise predicate+date-range filtering that memory_search/recall do not support well. However the spec is clear on the contract.

Options to resolve:
1. Update butler-health spec to explicitly permit direct SQL queries as an alternative implementation (preferred - direct SQL is more appropriate for structured date-range queries)
2. Extend memory_search/recall to support predicate and date-range filters, then update diet.py to use them

Scope: Choose and implement one resolution.
title: meal_history uses direct SQL instead of memory_search/memory_recall

close_reason: Duplicate of bu-r55 (same unified ingestion policy epic)
description:
Replace the dual triage-rules + source-filters filtering systems with a single
unified ingestion_rules table and IngestionPolicyEvaluator. Full spec at
openspec/changes/unified-ingestion-policy/.

## Problem
Two independent filtering systems (triage rules: global post-ingest pre-LLM;
source filters: per-connector pre-ingest) create a confusing dual-model UX.
Both handle sender_domain/sender_address matching at different pipeline stages
with different APIs, UIs, and composition rules.

## Solution
Single `ingestion_rules` table with:
- `scope` field: `global` (post-ingest pre-LLM) or `connector:<type>:<identity>` (pre-ingest)
- `action`: block (connector only), skip, metadata_only, low_priority_queue, route_to:<butler>, pass_through
- First-match-wins evaluation everywhere (drop blacklist/whitelist composition)
- Single IngestionPolicyEvaluator class, single API, single frontend table

## Critical constraint: NO LEGACY CODE
All old code paths MUST be removed — no backward compatibility layer:
- Delete src/butlers/connectors/source_filter.py
- Delete roster/switchboard/tools/triage/{evaluator,cache,telemetry}.py
- Remove all /source-filters/*, /connectors/*/filters, /triage-rules/* API endpoints
- Remove ManageSourceFiltersPanel, ConnectorFiltersDialog, use-source-filters.ts, use-triage.ts
- Remove old Pydantic models (SourceFilter*, TriageRule*, ConnectorFilterAssignment*)
- Drop old tables (triage_rules, source_filters, connector_source_filters) after soak

## Key pipeline distinction
Connector-scoped rules (action=block) intervene BEFORE any LLM sees the data.
Global rules run post-ingest pre-LLM for routing/skip/metadata decisions.
Thread affinity preserved unchanged.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Single ingestion_rules table replaces triage_rules + source_filters + connector_source_filters
2. Zero data loss — all existing rules migrated
3. All 4 connectors (gmail, telegram-bot, telegram-user-client, discord) use IngestionPolicyEvaluator
4. Switchboard pipeline uses IngestionPolicyEvaluator for global rules
5. Frontend shows single unified rules table with scope column
6. No imports of removed modules remain anywhere in codebase
7. All tests pass with new evaluator and API
title: Unified ingestion policy — replace triage rules + source filters with single model

description:
Replace the SDK-based ClaudeCodeAdapter (claude_agent_sdk) with a subprocess-based
adapter that wraps the `claude` CLI binary, matching the pattern used by Codex,
Gemini, and OpenCode adapters. This eliminates the SDK dependency, gains
subprocess-level diagnostics (last_process_info), and unifies all four adapters
on one consistent invocation pattern.

OpenSpec change: openspec/changes/claude-binary-adapter/
- Proposal: openspec/changes/claude-binary-adapter/proposal.md
- Design: openspec/changes/claude-binary-adapter/design.md
- Spec delta: openspec/changes/claude-binary-adapter/specs/core-spawner/spec.md

Key design decisions (from design.md):
- D1: Use --output-format stream-json for tool call + usage visibility
- D2: Use --bare for minimal overhead (skip hooks/LSP/auto-memory)
- D3: Use --strict-mcp-config for MCP isolation
- D4: Pass system prompt via --system-prompt flag
- D7: Use --no-session-persistence for ephemeral sessions
- D8: Parse stream-json events for result text, tool calls, and token usage
- D9: Reuse existing build_config_file() for MCP config

Primary file: src/butlers/core/runtimes/claude_code.py
Test files: tests/adapters/test_claude_code_integration.py, tests/adapters/test_adapter_contract.py
title: Rewrite ClaudeCodeAdapter to wrap claude CLI binary via subprocess

close_reason: Gen-1 reconciliation complete, all gaps filled by bu-dtnk and bu-x92r, gen-2 (bu-v5ee) confirmed and synced spec
description:
Deep-dive review: compare the original spec/requirements (see epic description)
against the implementation delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

Workflow:
1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Audit the codebase changes delivered by each sibling bead.
3. Produce a checklist mapping every spec requirement to its implementing bead.
   Spec source: openspec/changes/claude-binary-adapter/specs/core-spawner/spec.md
4. For any requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
5. If gap beads were created in step 4, create a follow-up reconciliation bead
   (gen-2) that depends on ALL the new gap beads. The gen-2 bead re-runs this
   same workflow to catch anything still missing after the gaps are filled.
   - A gen-2 reconciliation bead may spawn one final gen-3 reconciliation bead
     using the same pattern. Gen-3 is the hard limit — it must NOT spawn further
     reconciliation beads.
6. Keep this reconciliation bead open (or blocked) until all newly created
   gap beads (and any follow-up reconciliation bead) are closed.
7. Re-run the requirement-to-bead checklist and close this bead only when all
   requirements show full coverage.
8. If requirements show full coverage and the epic bead is managed via a OpenSpec change,
   run /opsx:sync to synchronize deltas into the authoritative application specification.
notes: Gen-1 reconciliation complete. 2 gaps found (bu-dtnk, bu-x92r). Blocked on gen-2 (bu-v5ee).
title: Reconcile spec-to-code coverage for ClaudeCodeAdapter rewrite

description:
Introduce a dedicated connectors Postgres schema for connector-owned persistent state. Today, connector-side filtering decisions (skip, metadata_only, label exclusion, validation errors) are invisible -- they exist only in connector stdout logs. When a message fails to ingest due to a connector bug (e.g. IngestAttachment validation failure on PDF-bearing emails), there is no persistent record, no UI visibility, and no replay path.

This epic delivers: (1) connectors.filtered_events table (monthly partitioned) that persists every message a connector touches but does not submit to Switchboard, with full payload for replay. (2) Replay mechanism via status transition on filtered_events -- dashboard marks events for replay, connectors drain pending replays on each poll cycle. (3) Unified ingestion timeline at /butlers/ingestion?tab=timeline with Status column (ingested/filtered/error/replay_pending/replay_complete/replay_failed) and Action column (Replay button), backed by UNION query across shared.ingestion_events + connectors.filtered_events. (4) Batch flush write path -- connectors accumulate filtered events in memory during poll cycle, flush in single batch INSERT after cycle completes.

OpenSpec change: openspec/changes/add-connectors-schema/
title: Add connectors schema with filtered events, replay queue, and timeline UI

description:
Create Alembic migration that establishes the connectors Postgres schema and the filtered_events table. This is the foundation that all other beads in this epic depend on.

Work:
- CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS connectors
- Create connectors.filtered_events table with columns: id (UUID PK), received_at (timestamptz, partitioned), connector_type, endpoint_identity, external_message_id, source_channel, sender_identity, subject_or_preview, filter_reason, status (default 'filtered'), full_payload (JSONB), error_detail, replay_requested_at, replay_completed_at, created_at
- PARTITION BY RANGE (received_at) with monthly partitions
- Create partition management function (auto-create monthly partitions, same pattern as switchboard_message_inbox_ensure_partition)
- Create indexes: (connector_type, endpoint_identity, status, received_at DESC) for drain queries; (received_at DESC) for timeline UNION queries
- Grant connector DB role USAGE on connectors schema, SELECT on shared schema

Reference: openspec/changes/add-connectors-schema/specs/connector-filtered-events/spec.md

description:
Create the shared FilteredEventBuffer class that all connectors use to accumulate filtered/errored events in memory during a poll cycle and batch-flush them to connectors.filtered_events.

Work:
- Create src/butlers/connectors/filtered_event_buffer.py with FilteredEventBuffer class
- record(event) method accumulates events in memory list
- flush(pool) method does single batch INSERT via pool.executemany()
- full_payload serialization helper: builds envelope-shaped dict from connector message data (source, event, sender, payload, control -- same shape as ingest.v1 minus schema_version)
- filter_reason formatting helpers: label_exclude:<label>, global_rule:skip:<rule_type>, connector_rule:block:<rule_type>, validation_error, submission_error
- Buffer clears after successful flush; flush failure logs warning but does not raise
- Unit tests for buffer accumulation, flush SQL generation, reason formatting, and crash-safety semantics

description:
Deep-dive review: compare the original spec/requirements (see epic description and openspec/changes/add-connectors-schema/) against the implementation delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

Workflow:
1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Audit the codebase changes delivered by each sibling bead.
3. Produce a checklist mapping every spec requirement to its implementing bead.
4. For any requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
5. If gap beads were created in step 4, create a follow-up reconciliation bead (gen-2) that depends on ALL the new gap beads. The gen-2 bead re-runs this same workflow. Gen-3 is the hard limit.
6. Keep this reconciliation bead open until all newly created gap beads are closed.
7. Re-run the requirement-to-bead checklist and close this bead only when all requirements show full coverage.

Specs to verify against:
- specs/connector-filtered-events/spec.md (schema, table, batch flush, payload shape)
- specs/connector-replay-queue/spec.md (status transitions, drain loop, API endpoint, dedup safety)

- specs/dashboard-visibility/spec.md (Status column, Action column, filter, unified data source)
- specs/connector-base-spec/spec.md (flush obligation, drain loop, modified connector responsibilities)

description:
In _flush_chat_buffer, ingestion policy is currently evaluated after _fetch_conversation_history and _resolve_reply_tos (Telegram API calls). For policy-blocked chats, this wastes Telegram rate-limit quota on every flush cycle.

Reorder the pipeline: evaluate connector-scope and global ingestion policy immediately after the atomic buffer swap (before steps b+c), so blocked chats skip all Telegram API calls.

The IngestionEnvelope only requires source_channel and chat_id (already available), so the policy check can be moved before the try block's network calls.

Discovered during PR #632 review (bu-r2r1).
title: Move ingestion policy evaluation before history fetch in _flush_chat_buffer

description:
## What

`_extract_body_from_payload()` in `src/butlers/connectors/gmail.py` (line 1992) hard-codes UTF-8 decoding:

```python
return base64.urlsafe_b64decode(body_data).decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
```

This ignores the `charset` parameter from the part's Content-Type header (e.g., `text/plain; charset=iso-8859-1`). Non-UTF-8 emails are silently garbled — replacement characters (`\ufffd`) appear wherever the byte sequence is not valid UTF-8.

## Why

Non-UTF-8 charsets are common in:
- **CJK emails** (Shift-JIS, Big5, GB2312, EUC-KR) from Asian senders
- **European emails** (ISO-8859-1, Windows-1252) from older mail systems, government agencies, and legacy enterprise senders
- **Auto-generated emails** from older billing/ERP systems that default to the server locale encoding

The Gmail API preserves the original charset in the Content-Type header of each MIME part. The `headers` array on each part contains `{"name": "Content-Type", "value": "text/plain; charset=iso-8859-1"}`, but the connector never reads it.

## Context

- **File**: `src/butlers/connectors/gmail.py`, method `_extract_body_from_payload()` (line 1992)
- **Gmail API behavior**: Returns the raw bytes base64url-encoded. The charset is in the part's `Content-Type` header, not applied by Gmail.
- **Python stdlib**: `email.utils` or simple string parsing can extract the charset parameter. The `codecs` module handles all common charset aliases.
- **No tests exist** for non-UTF-8 charset handling.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. When a MIME part has a `charset` parameter in its Content-Type header, the body is decoded using that charset.
2. When charset is absent or unrecognized, UTF-8 with replacement is used as fallback (current behavior preserved).
3. Common charset aliases are handled: `iso-8859-1`, `windows-1252`, `us-ascii`, `shift_jis`, `big5`, `gb2312`, `euc-kr`, `utf-8`.
4. New test `test_extract_body_charset_iso8859` validates ISO-8859-1 body decoding.
5. New test `test_extract_body_charset_fallback` validates graceful fallback on unknown charset.
6. No new third-party dependencies — use Python stdlib `codecs` module.

## Design

1. In `_extract_body_from_payload()`, when a `text/plain` (or `text/html` after bu-nwn lands) part is found, parse the `charset` from the part's headers array.
2. Use `codecs.lookup()` to validate the charset name. If valid, decode with that charset and `errors="replace"`. If invalid, fall back to UTF-8.
3. Extract charset via: find `Content-Type` in `payload.get("headers", [])`, then parse with `email.utils` or a simple regex like `charset=([^\s;]+)`.

## Notes

- This fix has a soft dependency on bu-nwn (HTML-only body extraction) — the charset logic should apply to both text/plain and text/html extraction paths. Can be implemented independently but should be aware of the HTML fallback work.
- Gmail normalizes *most* modern email to UTF-8, but forwarded messages, older mailing lists, and government/enterprise senders frequently retain legacy charsets.
title: Fix Gmail body extraction to respect Content-Type charset parameter

description:
The dashboard's SSE health-check loop calls fan_out('SELECT count(*) FROM sessions') across all 9 registered butlers every ~30 seconds (src/butlers/api/db.py:30). When the butler daemons haven't yet run their core Alembic migrations (fresh DB, restart before daemons, or schema not yet provisioned), this produces a flood of warnings:

  UndefinedTableError: relation "sessions" does not exist
  fan_out query failed for butler {general,education,health,home,messenger,finance,relationship,travel,switchboard}

Observed in logs/20260307_153655/uvicorn/dashboard.log: this fires every ~30s continuously for all 9 butlers from startup.

The sessions table IS defined in core_001 Alembic migration and is created when each butler daemon first starts. Until daemons run, the table is absent.

Options to investigate:
1. Suppress/debounce the warning for UndefinedTableError (expected on uninitialized schema)
2. Track per-butler initialization state and exclude uninitialized butlers from fan_out
3. Return a structured 'not_ready' status rather than treating as query failure
4. Gate the SSE polling until at least one butler is healthy

Acceptance criteria:
1. Dashboard startup does not spam error logs when butler daemons haven't run migrations
2. Health check still accurately reports butler status once initialized
3. Log noise reduced to at most one warning per butler per initialization cycle
title: Dashboard SSE spams fan_out errors when butler schemas are uninitialized

description:
Add WhatsApp as a messaging channel to the Butlers framework, mirroring the
Telegram user-client pattern: standalone connector for passive readonly ingestion
of the user's personal WhatsApp inbox, a module for (gated) outbound tools on
the Messenger butler, and a Go sidecar binary wrapping whatsmeow for the
WhatsApp Web multidevice protocol.

Key design constraints:
- Readonly-first: send tools registered but functionally disabled (send_enabled=false) until ban risk is assessed
- Owner-only auto-approve for sends via existing gate.py pattern; external parties always require approval
- Only the Messenger butler mounts write tools (send_tools=true); all other butlers are read-only
- Go bridge compiled into Docker image via multi-stage build, managed as subprocess via new BridgeSubprocessManager
- Dashboard settings UX for QR pairing (modeled after Google OAuth account linking)
- QR pairing ceremony stores session to PostgreSQL for restart-safe operation

Full spec: openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/
- proposal.md — motivation and capabilities
- design.md — 11 technical decisions
- specs/ — 7 spec files (3 new capabilities, 3 deltas, 1 dashboard)
- tasks.md — 16 groups, 68 tasks

Prior research: docs/archive/whatsapp-draft.md
title: Add WhatsApp connector (readonly-first, whatsmeow Go bridge)

description:
Build the Go binary that wraps whatsmeow for WhatsApp Web multidevice protocol.
This is the core transport layer — the Python connector and module communicate
with WhatsApp exclusively through this bridge's HTTP API.

   - cmd/bridge/main.go with run/pair/status subcommands
   - PostgreSQL session store adapter (read/write whatsapp_sessions table)

2. QR Pairing:
   - CLI pair subcommand: terminal QR, auto-refresh, 120s timeout, exit codes
   - HTTP POST /pair/start: generate QR, return base64 PNG data URI + expiry
   - HTTP GET /pair/poll: return waiting/paired/expired for dashboard polling

3. HTTP API on Unix socket:

   - POST /send: relay message, support reply_to, return message ID

   - POST /disconnect: graceful disconnect preserving session keys

2. whatsapp-bridge run connects using stored session, streams events on /events SSE
3. POST /send relays a message and returns WhatsApp message ID
4. GET /status reports connected state with phone number
5. Session invalidation emits event and exits with code 2
6. POST /pair/start returns base64 QR data URI for dashboard consumption
7. All Go tests pass

description:
Create a new reusable utility for managing the whatsapp-bridge Go binary as a
persistent subprocess. This is a genuinely new pattern — no existing connector
manages an external binary as a subprocess (Telethon is a Python library,
the LLM CLI spawner handles ephemeral SDK calls).

Covers tasks 8.1-8.6 from openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/tasks.md.

Covers spec:
- openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/specs/module-whatsapp/spec.md → "Requirement: Go Bridge Sidecar Lifecycle"

Work:
1. Create src/butlers/connectors/bridge_manager.py with BridgeSubprocessManager class
2. Start bridge via asyncio.create_subprocess_exec()
3. Capture stdout/stderr and forward to Python logging
4. Health polling via bridge /status endpoint (configurable interval)
5. Restart with jittered exponential backoff (initial 5s, max 300s) on unexpected exit
6. Exit code interpretation:
   - 0 = clean shutdown (no restart)
   - 1 = pairing timeout (no restart)
   - 2 = session invalidated (no restart, log re-pair needed)
   - Other = unexpected crash (restart with backoff)
7. Graceful shutdown: POST /disconnect, wait 5s, SIGTERM fallback
8. Unit tests for lifecycle management, restart backoff logic, and exit code handling

Acceptance criteria:
1. BridgeSubprocessManager starts bridge and waits for /status connected
2. Unexpected exit triggers restart with jittered backoff
3. Exit code 2 does not trigger restart; logs session invalidation
4. on_shutdown sends /disconnect then SIGTERM fallback
5. All unit tests pass
title: Implement BridgeSubprocessManager for Go sidecar lifecycle

Build the standalone WhatsApp connector process for passive readonly ingestion
of the user's personal WhatsApp inbox. Follows the telegram_user_client pattern:
bridge event streaming, per-chat buffering, discretion filtering, checkpoint
durability, ingest.v1 normalization, and Switchboard MCP submission.

Covers tasks 10.1-10.15 from openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/tasks.md.

Covers spec:
- openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/specs/connector-whatsapp-user-client/spec.md (all requirements)

Work:
1. Connector module (src/butlers/connectors/whatsapp_user_client.py):
   - Single-file with if __name__ == "__main__": asyncio.run(...) entrypoint
   - Bridge management via BridgeSubprocessManager
   - SSE event consumer on bridge GET /events via async HTTP on Unix socket
   - ingest.v1 normalization: map bridge JSON → IngestEnvelopeV1
   - Message type normalization per spec (text, image, video, audio, etc.)

2. Buffering and discretion:
   - ChatBuffer per chat JID: flush_interval_s=600, buffer_max_messages=50
   - Time-based and size-based flush with Switchboard MCP submission
   - Shared DiscretionEvaluator per chat (discretion.py), ContactWeightResolver
     with type="whatsapp_jid", source name "wa:{chat_jid}"

3. Durability:
   - Checkpoint via cursor_store keyed by (whatsapp_user_client, whatsapp:<phone>)
   - Restart-safe resume from checkpoint
   - Bounded backfill via CONNECTOR_BACKFILL_WINDOW_H
   - Filtered event batch flush via FilteredEventBuffer

4. Observability:
   - Health endpoint on port 40082 via health_socket.py
   - ConnectorHeartbeat (120s interval)
   - ConnectorMetrics (Prometheus)

5. Unit tests for normalization, buffering, discretion, checkpoint, reconnection

Acceptance criteria:
1. Connector starts, bridge connects, events stream via SSE
2. Messages normalized to valid ingest.v1 envelopes
3. Per-chat buffering flushes on time and size thresholds
4. Discretion layer filters low-weight messages before Switchboard
5. Checkpoint persists and resume works after restart
6. Health endpoint responds on port 40082
7. All unit tests pass
title: Implement WhatsApp user client connector (readonly ingestion)

description:

against the implementation delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

Workflow:
1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Audit the codebase changes delivered by each sibling bead.
3. Produce a checklist mapping every spec requirement to its implementing bead.
   Spec sources:
   - openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/specs/module-whatsapp/spec.md
   - openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/specs/connector-whatsapp-user-client/spec.md
   - openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/specs/whatsapp-bridge/spec.md
   - openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/specs/contacts-identity/spec.md
   - openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/specs/butler-switchboard/spec.md
   - openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/specs/butler-messenger/spec.md
   - openspec/changes/whatsapp-connector/specs/dashboard-whatsapp-setup/spec.md
4. For any requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
5. If gap beads were created in step 4, create a follow-up reconciliation bead
   (gen-2) that depends on ALL the new gap beads. The gen-2 bead re-runs this
   same workflow to catch anything still missing after the gaps are filled.
   - A gen-2 reconciliation bead may spawn one final gen-3 reconciliation bead
     using the same pattern. Gen-3 is the hard limit — it must NOT spawn further
     reconciliation beads.
6. Keep this reconciliation bead open (or blocked) until all newly created
   gap beads (and any follow-up reconciliation bead) are closed.
7. Re-run the requirement-to-bead checklist and close this bead only when all
   requirements show full coverage.
8. If requirements show full coverage, run /opsx:sync to synchronize deltas
   into the authoritative application specification.
title: Reconcile spec-to-code coverage for WhatsApp connector (gen-1)

description:
## What

Consolidate module tests (excluding memory, which has its own bead) from ~48
files / 2,170 tests to ~400 behavioral tests through tool interfaces.

## Why

Module tests are the largest category overall (3,714 including memory). Non-memory
modules have heavy per-function testing of internal helpers that should be tested
through the MCP tool interface instead.

Top offenders:
- test_module_home_assistant.py: 190 tests, 287 asserts
- test_module_calendar.py: 127 tests, 339 asserts
- test_approval_rules.py: 95 tests, 176 asserts
- test_calendar_sync.py: 92 tests, 185 asserts
- Approvals spread across ~9 files
- Calendar spread across ~8 files

## Strategy

Per module, consolidate to ONE primary test file that:
1. Tests tool registration (Module ABC contract — RFC 0002)
2. Tests each registered MCP tool's happy path and error path
3. Tests module lifecycle (on_startup, on_shutdown)
4. Tests module-specific business logic only where non-trivial

### Calendar consolidation
8+ files → 1 file testing calendar MCP tools (create, update, delete, list, sync)
Keep autodiscovery and sync tests as they test real integration logic.

### Approvals consolidation
9 files → 1-2 files testing gate interception, rule evaluation, risk tiers.
Must cover: approval bypass rejection, sensitivity metadata matching, timeout
= denial semantics (RFC 0002, security.md).

### Insight Broker (RFC 0011)
Consolidate insight broker tests. Must cover:
- propose_insight_candidate() validation (priority range, dedup key format)
- Delivery cycle ordering (expire → cooldown → dedup → budget → top-B)
- Anti-spam guarantees (global budget cap, cooldown enforcement)
- Adaptive ratchet (never auto-increases, auto-off at 0% for 14 days)

### Other modules
- Home Assistant (190 tests in 1 file): → ~30 behavioral tests
- Contacts (4+ files): → 1 file testing contact MCP tools
- Spotify/Steam (78+81 tests): → 1 file each, ~15 tests

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Non-memory module tests reduced to ≤450 tests across ≤25 files
2. Each module has exactly 1 primary test file (+ 1 integration file if needed)
3. All tests exercise behavior through MCP tool interface
4. Module ABC contract (register_tools, migrations, lifecycle) tested per module
5. Insight broker tests cover RFC 0011 anti-spam contracts
6. Approval tests cover bypass rejection and timeout semantics
7. Full test suite still passes
title: Condense non-memory module tests from 2,170 to ~400

close_reason: All 4 PR #489 review issues fixed: domain filter bug, From-header dedup, comment typo, _warned_unknown_key_type made instance attribute
description:
PR #489 (SourceFilterEvaluator + GmailConnector) has 4 issues requiring fixes before merge:

1. CRITICAL: GmailConnector hardcodes 'sender_address' as the key_type when calling extract_gmail_filter_key, so domain filters silently receive the full email address (e.g. alice@example.com) instead of just the domain. Domain blacklists/whitelists are completely ineffective. Fix: pass the actual filter's source_key_type to extract_gmail_filter_key.

2. MEDIUM: From-header extraction is duplicated verbatim in both backfill and live-ingestion paths. Extract a _get_from_header() helper.

3. MEDIUM: Comment on line 414 in gmail.py says '# substring and chat_id: return verbatim' — chat_id is not a Gmail concept. Remove it.

4. NEW: _warned_unknown_key_type in source_filter.py is a module-level global set. In multi-account deployments with multiple SourceFilterEvaluator instances, a warning for filter_id X in one evaluator suppresses all subsequent warnings for that filter_id from all other evaluators. Make it an instance attribute instead of a module-level set.

See PR threads: https://github.com/Tzeusy/butlers/pull/489#discussion_r2900079805, r2900079808, r2900079812, r2900092099

Fixes must be applied to the existing PR branch agent/bu-qbq.4.
title: Fix review issues for PR #489 (SourceFilterEvaluator domain filtering bug)

description: PR #562 introduced a non-atomic replay flow: ingestion_event_replay_request does a SELECT to fetch current_status, then a separate UPDATE. Under concurrent replay requests, two callers can both see status=filtered and both succeed, violating exactly-once semantics. Fix: replace with a single UPDATE ... WHERE id=$1 AND status=ANY($2) RETURNING id, with a second SELECT only on the miss path to determine not_found vs conflict outcome. Thread: https://github.com/Tzeusy/butlers/pull/562#discussion_r2917349175
notes:
Coordinator: dispatching implementation worker
PR #568 opened for atomic replay request fix
title: Make ingestion_event_replay_request atomic (fix TOCTOU race)

The tick() docstring also incorrectly says 'Returns: The number of tasks successfully dispatched (cron pass only)'. Should add a deadlines_dispatched span attribute and update the docstring. description:
Implement the bulk transaction import backend: HTTP endpoint, MCP tool, Pydantic models, composite deduplication, and embedding bypass.

## What
- Add `BulkTransactionItem`, `BulkTransactionRequest`, `BulkTransactionResponse` Pydantic models to `roster/finance/api/models.py`
- Add `POST /api/finance/transactions/bulk` endpoint to `roster/finance/api/router.py` (max 500 items, per-row error handling, top-level `account_id` inheritance, `source` metadata tagging)
- Implement `bulk_record_transactions()` in `roster/finance/tools/facts.py` wrapping the same logic
- Implement composite dedup key with **canonicalization**: `sha256(canonical_posted_at|canonical_amount|merchant|canonical_account_id)` where posted_at is UTC ISO 8601 with Z suffix at second precision, amount is `str(Decimal(amount).quantize(Decimal("0.01")))`, merchant is case-sensitive as-is, account_id is lowercased or empty string
- **Skip embeddings**: the bulk path MUST NOT call `embedding_engine.embed()` per row. Store a zero vector or NULL placeholder. Compute `search_vector` (tsvector) only. This keeps 500-row batches under 10 seconds.
- Register `bulk_record_transactions` as an MCP tool in the finance butler's tool registration
- Response `error_details` MUST include `reason` field distinguishing `"duplicate"` skips from validation errors like `"invalid_date"`

## Covers
- `openspec/changes/transaction-csv-extraction/specs/bulk-transaction-ingestion/spec.md` — all requirements including canonicalization, embedding bypass, and re-run semantics
- `openspec/changes/transaction-csv-extraction/specs/butler-finance/spec.md` — tool inventory and composite dedup scenarios

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `POST /api/finance/transactions/bulk` accepts array of 1-500 normalized transaction objects and returns `{total, imported, skipped, errors, error_details}`
2. Re-POSTing the same batch returns all rows as `skipped` with reason `"duplicate"` (composite dedup works)
3. Composite dedup key uses canonicalized inputs (UTC Z-suffix, quantized amount, lowercased account_id)
4. Rows with `source_message_id` use existing email-based dedup, not composite key
5. Invalid rows counted in `errors` with `error_details` including `reason`; valid rows in same batch still persist
6. Batch >500 returns HTTP 422
7. Top-level `account_id` inherited by all rows; top-level `source` stored as `import_source` in fact metadata
8. `bulk_record_transactions` MCP tool produces identical results to HTTP endpoint
9. **Embeddings skipped**: bulk path stores zero vector or NULL, does NOT call embedding_engine.embed(). tsvector still computed.
10. 500-row batch completes within 10 seconds under normal conditions
11. Verify implementation matches behavior defined in bulk-transaction-ingestion/spec.md
notes:
## OpenSpec References
- `openspec/changes/transaction-csv-extraction/specs/bulk-transaction-ingestion/spec.md`
  - Requirement: Bulk transaction ingestion HTTP endpoint → Scenarios: Successful bulk import, Composite deduplication, Composite key canonicalization, Source message ID dedup, Per-row error reporting, Batch size limit, Account ID association, Source tagging

description:
The memory entities endpoint (src/butlers/api/routers/memory.py) queries shared.entities directly without checking whether the table exists. When the core_014 Alembic migration (core_014_entities_to_shared.py) has not yet been applied, every request crashes:

  UndefinedTableError: relation "shared.entities" does not exist
  Unhandled exception on GET /api/memory/entities

Observed in logs/20260307_153655/uvicorn/dashboard.log at ~07:38:07.

The shared.entities table is created by core_014 migration which runs when each butler daemon initializes. Before daemons run, the shared schema is empty.

Options to investigate:
1. Add a startup check in the dashboard API that verifies shared.entities exists before mounting the memory router
2. Return a 503 with a clear message ('memory entities not available: schema not initialized') instead of 500
3. Lazy initialization guard in the memory router

Acceptance criteria:
1. /api/memory/entities returns a clean error (503 or 404 with message) when shared.entities is absent
2. No unhandled 500 is thrown
3. Once migration is applied, endpoint works normally
title: GET /api/memory/entities returns 500 when shared.entities migration not applied

description:
Git worktree lifecycle management and the shared dispatch engine that both the
MCP module and spawner fallback use. The dispatcher runs the 10-gate check
sequence, creates worktrees, spawns healing agents, manages the timeout watchdog,
and orchestrates PR creation.

## Work Items (tasks.md §6, §7)
### Worktree (§6)
1. src/butlers/core/healing/worktree.py — create_healing_worktree(), raises
   WorktreeCreationError on failure
2. Worktree creation error handling: orphaned branch cleanup, branch collision, git lock
3. remove_healing_worktree() with --force fallback, conditional branch/remote cleanup
4. reap_stale_worktrees() — terminal+>24h, orphaned worktrees, orphaned hotfix/* branches
5. Add .healing-worktrees/ to .gitignore

### Dispatcher (§7)
6. src/butlers/core/healing/dispatch.py — unified dispatch_healing() accepting
   both FingerprintResult (module) and (exc, tb) (spawner fallback)
7. 10-gate sequence: no-recursion→opt-in→fingerprint→persistence→severity→
   novelty→cooldown→concurrency→circuit breaker→model resolution
8. Healing agent prompt construction (two variants: with/without agent context)
9. Agent spawning via Spawner.trigger() with complexity=self_healing,
   trigger_source=healing, CWD=worktree, empty MCP config
10. PR creation flow: git push→anonymize→validate→gh pr create with labels;
    handle push failure and anonymization failure (delete remote branch)
11. Timeout watchdog: asyncio.Task, cancel after timeout_minutes, transition
    to timeout, trigger worktree cleanup
12. Independent OTel trace span (healing.dispatch root span)
13. Tests for each gate, dual-path dispatch, prompt variants, timeout, PR flow

## Covers
- openspec/changes/butler-self-healing/specs/healing-worktree/spec.md — ALL requirements:
  → Worktree Creation, Worktree Creation Error Handling, Worktree Isolation,
    Worktree Cleanup on Completion, Stale Worktree Reaper, Worktree Function Signatures
- openspec/changes/butler-self-healing/specs/self-healing-dispatch/spec.md — ALL requirements:
  → Dual Entry Points, Dispatch Function Signature, Gate Ordering, No Recursive
    Healing, Opt-In Gate, Fingerprint Update, Severity/Novelty/Cooldown/Concurrency/
    Circuit Breaker/Model Resolution Gates, Healing Agent Spawning, Timeout Watchdog,
    PR Creation Flow, Semaphore Behavior, Dispatch Errors, Trace Isolation
- openspec/changes/butler-self-healing/design.md §2 (Dual-path dispatch), §5 (Worktree),
  §6 (Healing agent prompt), §8 (PR creation), §12 (Timeout watchdog), §14 (PR creation flow)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. create_healing_worktree() creates branch hotfix/<butler>/<fingerprint-short>-<epoch>
   and worktree at .healing-worktrees/; raises WorktreeCreationError on failure with cleanup
2. reap_stale_worktrees() removes terminal+>24h, orphan worktrees, orphan branches
3. dispatch_healing() accepts both FingerprintResult and (exc, tb) inputs
4. 10-gate sequence evaluated in strict order; opt-in checked before fingerprint computation
5. Healing sessions bypass per-butler semaphore, acquire global semaphore
6. Healing agent gets empty MCP config + GH_TOKEN only
7. PR flow: push→anonymize→validate→create; push failure→failed; anon failure→delete remote branch
8. Timeout watchdog cancels sessions exceeding timeout_minutes
9. Verify implementation matches all scenarios in healing-worktree/spec.md and
   self-healing-dispatch/spec.md
notes: PR reviewer found 3 bugs/gaps requiring fixes before merge: (1) cwd=worktree_path missing from spawner.trigger() call — CRITICAL, healing agent uses wrong CWD; (2) per-butler semaphore bypass not implemented — potential deadlock in module path; (3) OTel trace span not implemented. Also: PR body template missing First seen/Occurrences. Formatting auto-fixed by reviewer and pushed.
title: Implement healing worktree lifecycle and dispatch engine

description:
The batch envelope's normalized_text is currently a raw concatenation of '[sender_id]: text' lines with no framing context. The switchboard classifier receives this identically to a single message, making it impossible to:

1. Know it's a conversation log (vs. a single message)
2. Identify which participant is the owner
3. Understand the time window and chat context
4. Filter non-owner-centric content or segment for routing

**Change:** Enrich normalized_text in _build_batch_envelope() with conversation framing:
- Header: chat identity (chat_id, chat_title if available), time window (oldest→newest timestamp)
- Participant list with owner tagging (requires resolving owner contact_id against sender IDs)
- Use sender display names instead of raw IDs where available (from message.sender)
- Footer: message count, flush window summary
- Tag each line with sender name and whether they are the owner

Example format:
```
[Conversation log for chat 12345 ("Family Group") between 2026-03-14T10:00:00Z and 2026-03-14T10:09:30Z]
[Participants: Alice (owner), Bob, Charlie]

[Alice]: Hey, are we still on for Thursday?
[Bob]: Yes, Thursday works for me
[Charlie]: Same here, 7pm?
[Alice]: Perfect, I'll book the restaurant

[End of conversation log — 4 new messages]
```

This gives the switchboard's triage classifier enough context to make good routing decisions without any pipeline changes.

**Files:**
- src/butlers/connectors/telegram_user_client.py (_build_batch_envelope)
- tests/connectors/test_telegram_user_client.py

**Design considerations:**
- Chat title may not be available for all chats (DMs vs groups) — degrade gracefully
- Owner identity resolution: use self._config or shared.contacts to identify the owner's sender_id
- Sender display names: extract from message.sender (first_name, username) during envelope construction
- Keep conversation_history payload unchanged — only normalized_text gets the framing
notes: Cleanup: released orphaned in_progress claim — no commits, no remote branch, worktree removed. Last activity 9h ago.
title: Enrich batch envelope normalized_text with conversation framing for switchboard classification

description:
Actionable findings from the GPT-5.4-pro codebase review (docs/2026-03-16_gpt-5.4-pro_review.md).

Four concrete issues identified that affect deployability, security, CI coverage, and API coherence:

1. Secrets stored as plaintext in PostgreSQL despite README claiming encryption
2. Docker/compose files reference wrong paths (./butlers/ vs ./roster/, missing heartbeat)
3. Frontend (React/Vite/Vitest) entirely excluded from CI
4. Traces router implemented but not mounted in FastAPI app, causing frontend/backend contract drift

These are the items with clear code-level evidence — not the generic scalability/governance recommendations.
title: Address GPT-5.4-pro review: deployment, security, CI, and contract drift

close_reason: Simple change merged to main (direct FF merge)
description:
README.md line 73 claims: "Secured entries are stored encrypted and masked in
the dashboard." This is misleading — secret_value is plaintext TEXT in PostgreSQL.
Masking only happens at the API response layer (list_secrets() excludes raw values).

Since the platform is user-federated (each user runs their own instance),
encryption at rest adds minimal value — the user already controls the database.
The fix is to correct the documentation, not add encryption.

Changes needed:
1. Update README.md to accurately describe the security model: secrets are
   stored in PostgreSQL with API-level masking, not encryption at rest.
   Frame this as intentional for a user-federated architecture.
2. Review any other docs that reference "encrypted" secrets and correct them.

Files:
- README.md (line 73 — fix encryption claim)
- docs/ (grep for "encrypt" references to credential store)
notes: Documentation corrected: README.md line 73 now accurately describes plaintext credential storage with API-level masking, framed appropriately for user-federated architecture.
title: Correct README encryption claim to match plaintext credential store
