description:
## What

Replace field-by-field API response assertions with schema-level validation.
Reduce 78 files / 1,779 tests to ~200 tests across ~20 files.

## Why

API tests currently assert exact values of every response field. This is:
- Brittle: adding a field breaks tests even if behavior is unchanged
- Redundant: Pydantic models already validate response shapes
- Verbose: 1,779 tests for what is mostly CRUD serialization

## Strategy

Per API router, keep:
1. **Status code tests** — each endpoint returns correct 2xx/4xx/5xx codes
2. **Schema validation** — response deserializes into the Pydantic model
3. **Auth/permission tests** — unauthorized access rejected
4. **Business logic tests** — only for endpoints with non-trivial logic
   (filtering, aggregation, computed fields)

Delete:
- Tests asserting exact field values for simple CRUD responses
- Tests asserting error message text
- Tests duplicating Pydantic validation (model already enforces types/shapes)

## Key directories

- tests/api/ (78 files, 1,779 tests)
- Biggest: test_memory.py (82 tests), test_api_steam.py (169 asserts)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. API tests reduced to ≤250 tests across ≤25 files
2. Every API endpoint has status-code and schema-validation coverage
3. No tests assert exact error message strings
4. No tests duplicate Pydantic model validation
5. Full test suite still passes
title: Prune API tests from 1,779 to ~200

description:
## What

Replace ~25 migration test files (749 tests) with ~50 tests that validate schema
outcomes rather than SQL strings.

## Why

Current migration tests parse SQL statements and assert string contents. This is
brittle (breaks on formatting changes) and doesn't prove the migration actually
works. A migration test should: run the migration, then assert the resulting
schema has the expected columns, constraints, and indexes.

## Current State (749 tests across 3 areas)

- tests/config/test_*_migration*.py: 410 tests — SQL string assertions
- tests/migrations/: 279 tests — schema creation tests
- tests/modules/memory/test_*_migration*.py: 60 tests — memory-specific migrations

Biggest offenders:
- test_finance_002_migration_unit.py: 196 tests, 263 asserts
- test_finance_migration_unit.py: 76 tests

## Strategy

1. Per migration chain (core, switchboard, per-module), write ONE test that:
   a. Runs all migrations in the chain from base to head
   b. Asserts resulting schema: table existence, column types, constraints, indexes
   c. Optionally tests upgrade + downgrade roundtrip
2. Delete all SQL-string-assertion tests
3. Keep any tests that validate data migration logic (not schema, but data transform)
4. Ensure RFC 0010 cross-schema grants are tested (view creation, role grants)
5. Ensure RFC 0012 finance indexes (18 indexes, 3 UNIQUE partial indexes) are tested

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Migration tests reduced to ≤60 tests across ≤10 files
2. Tests validate schema state post-migration, not SQL strings
3. Each migration chain (core, per-module, per-butler) has at least one test
4. Upgrade/downgrade roundtrip tested for chains with downgrade support
5. RFC 0010 cross-schema grant/view creation verified

description:
Core healing package and deterministic error fingerprinting. Produces stable
fingerprints from exceptions so duplicate errors map to the same key. Supports
two input modes: raw Python exceptions (spawner fallback) and structured string
fields (MCP module report_error tool).

## Work Items (tasks.md §3)
1. Create src/butlers/core/healing/__init__.py package
2. Implement src/butlers/core/healing/fingerprint.py with FingerprintResult dataclass
   and dual-input functions:
   - compute_fingerprint(exc, tb) for spawner fallback path
   - compute_fingerprint_from_report(error_type, error_message, call_site,
     traceback_str, severity_hint) for module path
3. Message sanitization: UUIDs→<UUID>, timestamps→<TS>, numeric IDs→<ID>,
   empty→<empty>, truncate to 500 chars
4. Call site extraction: walk traceback frames, skip stdlib/third-party (use
   src/butlers/, roster/, tests/ as app code heuristic), extract relative_path:function_name
5. Severity scoring with agent hint tiebreaker: critical (DB/credential), high
   (runtime/config), medium (module), low (memory), info (cancellation)
6. Tests: same fingerprint regardless of input path, severity hint logic, empty
   messages, chained exceptions, cancellation exclusion

## Covers
- openspec/changes/butler-self-healing/specs/error-fingerprinting/spec.md — ALL requirements:
  → Fingerprint Computation, Exception Type Extraction, Call Site Extraction,
    Message Sanitization, Severity Scoring, Fingerprint Scope Boundary,
    Dual-Input Fingerprinting, FingerprintResult Type, Severity Hint from Agent,
    Legacy Function Signature
- openspec/changes/butler-self-healing/design.md §1 (Error Fingerprinting)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. compute_fingerprint(exc, tb) and compute_fingerprint_from_report(...) produce
   identical fingerprints for the same underlying error
2. Dynamic values (UUIDs, timestamps, numeric IDs) are collapsed into placeholders
3. Same root cause at same call site → same fingerprint; different call sites → different fingerprints
4. Severity scoring covers all spawner _run() exception types per spec
5. Agent severity_hint overrides default-only severity (not specific rules)
6. Empty messages produce stable fingerprints using <empty> placeholder
7. asyncio.CancelledError/KeyboardInterrupt scored as info (severity 4)
8. Verify implementation matches all scenarios in error-fingerprinting/spec.md
title: Implement error fingerprinting with dual-input support

description:
Add Ollama as a supported LLM provider in the Model Catalog. Ollama runs within the tailnet and exposes an OpenAI-compatible API at a configurable base URL.

This epic covers:
1. A shared provider configuration table for storing provider-level settings (base URL, etc.)
2. An HTTP-based OllamaAdapter that implements the RuntimeAdapter ABC using Ollama's /v1/chat/completions endpoint
3. Model discovery via Ollama's /api/tags endpoint with bulk import to the catalog
4. Frontend UI for configuring the Ollama provider, discovering models, and managing catalog entries

Key architecture decisions:
- Ollama is the first HTTP API-based adapter (vs existing CLI-based adapters which spawn subprocess binaries)
- Provider-level config (base URL) is stored in a new shared.provider_config table, not per-model extra_args
- Initial implementation supports simple prompt-to-response; full MCP tool loop is out of scope for v1
- Ollama server runs on a tailnet host (e.g., http://hostname.parrot-hen.ts.net:11434)

Design reference: OpenCode's Ollama provider config pattern uses a JSON config with baseURL pointing to the Ollama instance and a models dict mapping model IDs to display names.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Users can configure an Ollama provider with a tailnet base URL via the Settings UI
2. Available Ollama models are discoverable and importable into the model catalog
3. Ollama models can be tested, enabled/disabled, and assigned complexity tiers like any other catalog entry
4. Butler spawner can resolve and invoke Ollama models via the OllamaAdapter
5. Per-butler overrides work with Ollama models
title: Add Ollama provider support to Model Catalog

description:
Create a shared provider configuration table for storing provider-level settings shared across all models of a given provider type. Initially used for Ollama's base URL, but designed generically for future HTTP-based providers.

What needs to change:

1. New Alembic migration creating shared.provider_config table:
   - id UUID PK (DEFAULT gen_random_uuid())
   - provider_type TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL (e.g., 'ollama')
   - display_name TEXT NOT NULL (e.g., 'Ollama (tailnet)')
   - config JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}' (provider-specific settings)
   - enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT false
   - created_at / updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()

2. New API router at /api/settings/providers (file: src/butlers/api/routers/provider_settings.py):
   - GET '' — list all configured providers
   - POST '' — register a new provider
   - PUT '/{provider_type}' — update provider config
   - DELETE '/{provider_type}' — remove provider
   - POST '/{provider_type}/test-connectivity' — probe the provider's base URL

3. Pydantic request/response models (co-located in the router file)

4. Register the new router in the FastAPI app

5. Unit tests for all CRUD operations and connectivity test endpoint

Design notes:
- The config JSONB field is schema-free per provider type. For Ollama: {"base_url": "http://host:11434"}
- The connectivity test is provider-type-aware: for 'ollama' it GETs /api/version at the configured base URL
- Use httpx.AsyncClient for the connectivity probe (add httpx to dependencies if not already present)
- Follow the pattern established by model_settings.py for DB access via DatabaseManager

Files to create/modify:
- alembic/versions/core/core_026_provider_config.py (new migration)
- src/butlers/api/routers/provider_settings.py (new router)
- src/butlers/api/app.py (register router)
- tests/api/test_provider_settings.py (new tests)

Acceptance criteria:
1. Migration creates shared.provider_config with correct schema and indexes
2. All CRUD endpoints work and return proper HTTP status codes (201, 200, 404, 409)
3. Connectivity test endpoint probes the configured base URL and returns success/error with latency
4. provider_type column has UNIQUE constraint preventing duplicate providers
5. Tests cover happy path, duplicate detection, not-found, and connectivity test (mock HTTP)
title: Add provider configuration table and CRUD API

description:
Create a new RuntimeAdapter implementation that talks to Ollama's OpenAI-compatible HTTP API instead of spawning a CLI subprocess. This is the first HTTP-based adapter in the system.

What needs to change:

1. New file src/butlers/core/runtimes/ollama.py:
   - OllamaAdapter(RuntimeAdapter) class
   - binary_name property: return 'ollama' (for display only; not required on PATH)
   - invoke(): async HTTP POST to {base_url}/v1/chat/completions
     - Construct messages: [{role: 'system', content: system_prompt}, {role: 'user', content: prompt}]
     - Pass model_id as the 'model' field
     - Parse OpenAI-format response to extract (result_text, tool_calls, usage)
     - Use httpx.AsyncClient for async HTTP
   - build_config_file(): no-op — returns an empty JSON file (HTTP adapter doesn't need config files)
   - parse_system_prompt_file(): read AGENTS.md (same pattern as OpenCode/Gemini adapters)
   - Base URL resolution: query shared.provider_config WHERE provider_type='ollama' to get config.base_url
     - Accept runtime_args override ['--base-url', 'http://...'] for per-model URL overrides
     - Fall back to http://localhost:11434 if no provider config found

2. Register adapter in src/butlers/core/runtimes/__init__.py:
   - Import OllamaAdapter
   - Adapter auto-registers via register_adapter('ollama', OllamaAdapter) at module load

3. Add httpx to pyproject.toml dependencies (if not already present)

4. Unit tests with mocked HTTP (tests/adapters/test_ollama_adapter.py):
   - Test invoke() with successful chat completion response
   - Test invoke() with error response (non-200)
   - Test base URL resolution from provider_config
   - Test runtime_args base URL override
   - Test timeout handling
   - Test response parsing (text extraction, usage extraction)

Design notes:
- The existing test_catalog_entry endpoint will 'just work' for Ollama once the adapter is registered, since it calls get_adapter(runtime_type) and then adapter.invoke()
- For v1, invoke() does a single chat completion call (no multi-turn tool loop). MCP server tools are NOT passed as OpenAI function definitions. This means Ollama models handle direct prompt-to-response tasks only. Full tool loop support is a separate follow-up.
- Token usage parsing: Ollama's OpenAI-compatible endpoint returns usage in standard format {prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, total_tokens}
- The adapter needs an asyncpg pool reference to query provider_config. Pass it via constructor or resolve lazily.

Key reference files:
- src/butlers/core/runtimes/base.py — RuntimeAdapter ABC interface
- src/butlers/core/runtimes/opencode.py — most analogous existing adapter (subprocess-based, but good pattern reference)
- src/butlers/api/routers/model_settings.py:596-602 — test endpoint that instantiates adapters

Acceptance criteria:
1. OllamaAdapter registered as 'ollama' runtime type
2. invoke() successfully calls Ollama's /v1/chat/completions and returns (text, [], usage)
3. Base URL resolved from shared.provider_config with fallback to localhost:11434
4. Model test button works for Ollama catalog entries (via existing test endpoint)
5. Timeout and error handling produce clear error messages
6. All RuntimeAdapter ABC methods implemented
title: Implement OllamaAdapter runtime

description:
Add API endpoints for discovering models available on the configured Ollama instance and bulk-importing them into the model catalog. Also add default Ollama entries to the bootstrap TOML.

What needs to change:

1. New endpoints on the provider_settings router (src/butlers/api/routers/provider_settings.py):
   - GET /api/settings/providers/ollama/models — queries Ollama's GET /api/tags endpoint at the configured base URL, returns list of available models enriched with catalog presence
   - POST /api/settings/providers/ollama/import — accepts a list of model selections (name + complexity_tier), creates catalog entries for each with runtime_type='ollama'

2. Response model OllamaDiscoveredModel:
   - name: str (e.g., 'qwen2.5-coder:7b')
   - size: int (bytes)
   - modified_at: str (ISO timestamp)
   - parameter_size: str | None (e.g., '7B', from details.parameter_size)
   - quantization: str | None (e.g., 'Q4_K_M', from details.quantization_level)
   - already_in_catalog: bool (cross-check against existing catalog entries with runtime_type='ollama')

3. Request model OllamaImportRequest:
   - models: list of {name: str, alias: str | None, complexity_tier: str}
   - Alias defaults to the model name if not provided

4. Add default Ollama entries to model_catalog_defaults.toml (disabled by default since provider must be configured first):
   - qwen2.5-coder:7b → trivial tier
   - llama3.3:latest → medium tier
   - deepseek-r1:32b → high tier

5. Unit tests (tests/api/test_ollama_discovery.py):
   - Test discovery with mocked /api/tags response
   - Test already_in_catalog flag accuracy
   - Test import creates correct catalog entries
   - Test import with duplicate alias returns 409
   - Test discovery when Ollama is unreachable (proper error response, not 500)
   - Test empty model list

Design notes:
- Discovery endpoint reads base_url from shared.provider_config WHERE provider_type='ollama'. Returns 404 if no Ollama provider is configured, 503 if provider is disabled.
- Import endpoint uses INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (alias) to avoid duplicates, returns 409 with details on conflicts.
- The /api/tags response from Ollama returns: {models: [{name, model, modified_at, size, digest, details: {parameter_size, quantization_level, ...}}, ...]}

Acceptance criteria:
1. Discovery endpoint returns enriched model list from Ollama instance
2. already_in_catalog flag correctly reflects current catalog state
3. Import endpoint creates catalog entries with correct runtime_type, model_id, and complexity_tier
4. Graceful error handling when Ollama is unreachable or provider not configured
5. Default TOML entries added for common Ollama models (disabled)
6. Tests cover all edge cases with mocked HTTP
title: Add Ollama model discovery and catalog import API

description:
Add frontend UI for configuring the Ollama provider, discovering available models, and integrating with the existing model catalog card on the Settings page.

1. New ProviderConfigCard component (frontend/src/components/settings/ProviderConfigCard.tsx):

2. New OllamaDiscoveryDialog component:

description:
Deep-dive review: compare the original spec/requirements (see epic bu-fjsb description) against the implementation delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

Workflow:
1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Audit the codebase changes delivered by each sibling bead.
3. Produce a checklist mapping every spec requirement to its implementing bead.
4. For any requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
5. If gap beads were created in step 4, create a follow-up reconciliation bead (gen-2) that depends on ALL the new gap beads. The gen-2 bead re-runs this same workflow to catch anything still missing after the gaps are filled.
   - A gen-2 reconciliation bead may spawn one final gen-3 reconciliation bead using the same pattern. Gen-3 is the hard limit — it must NOT spawn further reconciliation beads.
   - Tag the title with generation: 'Reconcile spec-to-code (gen-N) for ...'
6. Keep this reconciliation bead open (or blocked) until all newly created gap beads (and any follow-up reconciliation bead) are closed.
7. Re-run the requirement-to-bead checklist and close this bead only when all requirements show full coverage.

description:
Support multiple concurrent Google accounts across all Google-consuming modules (Gmail connector, Calendar, Contacts) and the OAuth/dashboard flow.

Currently Butlers supports exactly one Google account — the refresh token is stored as a singleton on the owner entity via UNIQUE(entity_id, type) in shared.entity_info. This blocks users with multiple Google identities (personal, work, family).

Key architectural decisions:
- shared.google_accounts registry table with companion entities for credential anchoring
- Account-aware credential resolution (account param on all Google credential functions)
- Single multi-account Gmail connector process with DB-driven account discovery
- Per-account config overrides via google_accounts.metadata
- Dashboard UI for managing connected accounts (list, connect, disconnect, set primary)
- Zero-downtime migration: existing single-account deployments auto-promote to primary

Full spec: openspec/changes/multi-account-google/ (proposal, design, 8 delta/new specs, task breakdown)
title: Multi-account Google integration

description:
Refactor the Gmail connector from one-process-per-mailbox to a single process that manages all connected Google accounts with independent watch/poll loops per account.

Covers:
- specs/connector-gmail/spec.md → MODIFIED Requirement: Gmail Connector Identity and Authentication (multi-account discovery, per-account identity, scope validation, degraded startup)
- specs/connector-gmail/spec.md → MODIFIED Requirement: Multi-Account Connector Architecture (independent per-account loops, error isolation, per-account config via metadata, process-level defaults)
- specs/connector-gmail/spec.md → ADDED Requirement: Dynamic Account Discovery (periodic re-scan, MCP-triggered reload, graceful loop shutdown)
- specs/connector-gmail/spec.md → MODIFIED Requirement: Multiple Concurrent Connectors (per-account isolation across processes, uniqueness boundary)
- specs/connector-gmail/spec.md → ADDED Requirement: Aggregated Health Status (worst-case overall, per-account status array)
- specs/connector-gmail/spec.md → MODIFIED Requirement: Environment Variables (CONNECTOR_ENDPOINT_IDENTITY no longer required, GMAIL_ACCOUNT_RESCAN_INTERVAL_S added)
- specs/connector-gmail/spec.md → REMOVED Requirement: GMAIL_ACCOUNT environment variable
- design.md → D8: Single multi-account Gmail connector process (GmailConnectorManager/GmailAccountLoop architecture)

Architecture:
- GmailConnectorManager: top-level orchestrator. Queries shared.google_accounts for active accounts with gmail.modify/gmail.readonly scopes. Holds dict of GmailAccountLoop instances keyed by email.
- GmailAccountLoop: encapsulates per-account state (credentials, cursor, label filters, watch subscription, backfill). Runs as independent asyncio task.
- CONNECTOR_ENDPOINT_IDENTITY derived per-account as gmail:user:<email> (not process-level)
- Per-account config overrides via google_accounts.metadata.gmail (label_include, label_exclude, poll_interval_s, pubsub_enabled). Falls back to process-level env var defaults.
- Per-account error isolation: failed token refresh or API error in one loop does not affect others.
- Dynamic account discovery: periodic re-scan at GMAIL_ACCOUNT_RESCAN_INTERVAL_S (default 300). connector_reload_accounts MCP tool + SIGHUP for immediate re-scan.
- Aggregated health endpoint: worst-case overall status, per-account status array.

description:
attached_molecule: bu-wisp-w2yx
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-03-06T05:08:33Z
dispatched_by: mayor

The backfill engine (backfill.py) does not correctly write Telegram-specific contact_info types to shared.contact_info during sync. Three sub-issues:

1. **telegram_user_id never created**: When a Telegram contact is synced, no contact_info entry with type='telegram_user_id' is created. The user_id is stored in external_id and contacts_source_links but not as a contact_info entry — so reverse-lookup by telegram_user_id is impossible.

2. **telegram_username stored incorrectly**: Telegram usernames are stored as type='other' with label='telegram' (backfill.py:444-446) instead of type='telegram_username'. Additionally, the value includes the '@' prefix (telegram_provider.py:71) but the contacts-identity spec says it should be stored without '@'.

3. **telegram_chat_id entries never populated**: TelegramContactsProvider.enrich_chat_ids() exists (telegram_provider.py:239) to resolve private chat IDs from dialogs, but it is never called from the sync pipeline (on_startup, sync engine, or backfill engine). No code path creates telegram_chat_id contact_info entries for synced contacts.

**Impact**: Cross-provider identity resolution via telegram_user_id is broken. Switchboard reverse-lookup by telegram_chat_id for synced contacts doesn't work. Username search by type='telegram_username' returns no results.

**Fix approach**: 
- Add post-sync hook in ContactsModule.on_startup or sync engine callback to:
  a) Create telegram_user_id contact_info entries from CanonicalContact.external_id for telegram provider
  b) Store usernames as type='telegram_username' (not 'other') without '@' prefix
  c) Call enrich_chat_ids() after sync to populate telegram_chat_id entries
- Alternatively, make the backfill engine provider-aware for contact_info type mapping.

**Files**: src/butlers/modules/contacts/backfill.py, src/butlers/modules/contacts/telegram_provider.py, src/butlers/modules/contacts/__init__.py
title: Telegram contact_info types not populated by backfill engine

description:
Register a Claude provider in the CLI auth registry so it appears as a row in the dashboard Settings → CLI Runtime Authentication card.

Context: The migrate-to-claude-binary epic (bu-5rfk) rewrites ClaudeCodeAdapter to shell out to the claude CLI binary, but does not cover making Claude visible in the dashboard auth settings. The infrastructure is ready:
- cli_auth/registry.py supports device-code and API-key provider types
- The dashboard CLIAuthCard renders all registered providers automatically
- Claude CLI respects the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable

Approach:
1. Register a Claude provider in the CLI auth registry using API-key mode (same pattern as opencode-go)
2. Dashboard settings card lets user enter their Anthropic API key → stored in credential store (butler_secrets with key cli-auth/claude)
3. Implement a health probe that validates the key (e.g. lightweight API call or format check)
4. ClaudeCodeAdapter subprocess spawner reads the stored key and injects it as ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in the subprocess environment
5. Dashboard renders the Claude row with status badges automatically

This avoids any special 'external-auth' mode — it's the standard API-key provider flow. The claude CLI binary picks up ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from its environment, so no credential file manipulation is needed.
title: Add Claude provider to CLI Runtime Authentication settings

description:
Add inverse_of and is_symmetric columns to predicate_registry for bidirectional entity graph traversal.

**Schema:**
- Add inverse_of TEXT column (FK to predicate_registry name, nullable) — declares bidirectional pair
- Add is_symmetric BOOLEAN DEFAULT false — true means the predicate is its own inverse (e.g., knows, sibling_of)

**Seed inverse relationships:**
- parent_of ↔ child_of (create child_of if not exists)
- manages ↔ reports_to (create reports_to if not exists)
- works_at ↔ employs (create employs if not exists)
- knows: is_symmetric = true (no inverse needed)
- sibling_of: is_symmetric = true
- lives_with: is_symmetric = true

**Query-layer support (virtual inverse, not materialized):**
- When querying facts about entity X, also check facts where X is object_entity_id
- For those facts, present them using the inverse predicate label
- Example: fact parent_of(Alice, Bob) → when querying Bob's facts, show as child_of(Bob, Alice)
- This is a read-path concern — no duplicate facts stored

**API/dashboard impact:**
- Entity detail page should show both direct facts and inverse-resolved facts
- memory_recall should support inverse traversal when object_entity_id is queried

Spec: openspec/changes/predicate-registry-enforcement/ (new requirement needed)
Design note: Option B (virtual inverse at query time) — no storage duplication
title: Inverse and symmetric predicates for bidirectional traversal

description:
`roster/health/tests/test_tools.py` is not merge-gate-safe under pytest-xdist. The refinery scoped runner selected `roster/health/tests/` for MR `bu-wisp-x3g5u` and the run failed with widespread `RuntimeError: ... got Future ... attached to a different loop` followed by `asyncpg.exceptions._base.InterfaceError: cannot perform operation: another operation is in progress`.

Repro:
1. `uv run pytest roster/health/tests/test_tools.py::test_measurement_log_weight -n auto --maxfail=1`
2. Observe the asyncpg/different-loop failure on `main`

Expected:
- Health tests pass under the repository's default parallel gate mode (`-n auto`)

Actual:
- Even a single health test fails on `main` under xdist because the async fixture/pool lifecycle crosses event loops

Impact:
- Refinery cannot distinguish branch regressions from baseline failures for health MRs without extra manual triage
- Any scoped run that selects `roster/health/tests/` will fail in default parallel mode

Acceptance criteria:
1. `uv run pytest roster/health/tests/test_tools.py::test_measurement_log_weight -n auto --maxfail=1` passes on `main`
2. `uv run pytest roster/health/tests/ -n auto` is merge-gate-safe for refinery scoped runs
3. Fixture/loop-scope changes are documented if they rely on repo-wide pytest conventions
title: Fix health xdist asyncpg loop failure

description:
GAP from reconciliation bu-wjzb.7: save_voice_checkpoint() and load_voice_checkpoint() are implemented and tested in checkpoint.py but never called from connector.py.

Required changes:
1. In LiveListenerConnector.start() / _pipeline_once(): call load_voice_checkpoint() on startup to restore session state from the persisted checkpoint. Use the loaded VoiceCheckpoint to call session.restore() before beginning audio capture.
2. In LiveListenerConnector._process_segment(): after successful ingest submission (accepted or duplicate status), call save_voice_checkpoint() with the utterance timestamp, session ID, and session last timestamp.
3. Both calls require the db_pool (asyncpg pool) which is already a constructor parameter of LiveListenerConnector.

Spec references:
- Checkpoint Semantics scenarios 9.2 (Checkpoint timing) and 9.3 (No replay on restart)

Acceptance criteria:
- Checkpoint is saved after each successful ingest submission
- Checkpoint is loaded on connector startup and session state is restored
- Session continuity is maintained across connector restarts (within gap window)
- DB errors in checkpoint load/save are handled gracefully (fail-open)
title: Wire checkpoint save/load into connector lifecycle

description:
Add scope column to predicate_registry, aligning with the fact-level scope values (health, relationship, finance, home, global). This serves as namespace, UI grouping, and search filter in one.

**Schema:**
- Add scope TEXT DEFAULT 'global' to predicate_registry
- Scope values match fact-level scopes: global, health, relationship, finance, home
- Keep name as PK (not composite) — scope is metadata, not part of uniqueness
- Predicates with scope='global' are available to all butlers (e.g., knows, parent_of)
- Domain predicates use their butler scope (e.g., measurement_weight → scope='health')

**Migration to backfill scope:**
- Health predicates (measurement_*, symptom, took_dose, medication, condition, research) → scope='health'
- Relationship predicates (interaction, life_event, contact_note, activity, gift, loan, contact_task, reminder) → scope='relationship'
- Finance predicates (transaction_*, account, subscription, bill) → scope='finance'
- Home predicates (ha_state) → scope='home'
- Edge predicates (knows, parent_of, etc.) → scope='global'
- General predicates (goal, note, preference, etc.) → scope='global'

**Search integration:**
- memory_predicate_search scope parameter filters on this column directly (replaces the current expected_subject_type workaround)
- search_vector trigger includes scope in weight B for discoverability

**store_fact integration:**
- Registry lookup uses normalized predicate name (scope is not part of the lookup key)
- Scope on the registry is informational — fact-level scope on the stored fact is the authority

Spec: openspec/changes/predicate-registry-enforcement/ (new requirement needed)
title: Predicate scoping: namespace predicates by domain

description:
Implement the Spotify module: a shared MCP module providing 22 tools for bidirectional Spotify interaction — playback control, playlist management, library operations, catalog search, and discovery.

Spec: openspec/changes/spotify-module/specs/module-spotify/spec.md
Design: openspec/changes/spotify-module/design.md
Tasks: openspec/changes/spotify-module/tasks.md

Key decisions:
- Extends existing SpotifyClient (src/butlers/connectors/spotify_client.py) with PUT/POST/DELETE methods
- Module follows standard Module ABC pattern (config, register_tools, on_startup, on_shutdown)
- OAuth scopes expanded from 3 read-only to 10 (read+write), requiring re-authorization for existing users
- Tools split into Free tier (playlist, library, search, discovery) and Premium tier (playback control)
- Recommendations API has graceful degradation (may be restricted for newer Spotify apps)
- Primary home: Lifestyle butler (roster/lifestyle/butler.toml [modules.spotify])
- Depends on lifestyle-butler epic (bu-d657) for the butler to exist

Implements OpenSpec change: openspec/changes/spotify-module/
title: Add Spotify read/write module for butler ecosystem

description:
Add PUT/POST/DELETE request helpers and new API methods to the existing SpotifyClient at src/butlers/connectors/spotify_client.py.

New internal helpers (mirroring existing _get pattern with auth/retry/rate-limit):
- _put(path, body, params) 
- _post(path, body, params)
- _delete(path, body, params)

New public methods:
- search(query, type, limit) — GET /v1/search
- get_playback_state() — GET /v1/me/player (full player state)
- get_queue() — GET /v1/me/player/queue
- get_top_items(type, time_range, limit) — GET /v1/me/top/{type}
- get_recommendations(seed_artists, seed_tracks, seed_genres, limit) — GET /v1/recommendations (graceful 403/404)
- get_related_artists(artist_id) — GET /v1/artists/{id}/related-artists
- play(context_uri, uris, device_id) — PUT /v1/me/player/play
- pause(device_id) — PUT /v1/me/player/pause
- skip_next/skip_previous(device_id) — POST /v1/me/player/next|previous
- seek(position_ms, device_id) — PUT /v1/me/player/seek
- set_volume(volume_percent, device_id) — PUT /v1/me/player/volume
- add_to_queue(uri, device_id) — POST /v1/me/player/queue
- transfer_playback(device_id, play) — PUT /v1/me/player
- get_playlists(limit, offset) — GET /v1/me/playlists
- create_playlist(name, description, public) — POST /v1/users/{id}/playlists
- add_tracks_to_playlist(playlist_id, uris) — POST /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks
- remove_tracks_from_playlist(playlist_id, uris) — DELETE /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks
- get_playlist_tracks(playlist_id, limit, offset) — GET /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks
- get_saved_tracks(limit, offset) — GET /v1/me/tracks
- save_tracks(ids) — PUT /v1/me/tracks
- remove_saved_tracks(ids) — DELETE /v1/me/tracks

Write unit tests for all new methods (mock httpx responses).

Covers: spec.module-spotify.Catalog Search Tool, Playback State Tools, Playback Control Tools, Discovery Tools, Playlist Management Tools, Library Management Tools, Rate Limit and Error Handling

Acceptance criteria:
1. _put/_post/_delete follow identical auth/retry/rate-limit pattern as existing _get
2. All 20+ new methods have unit tests with mocked httpx responses
3. get_recommendations gracefully handles 403/404 with actionable error message
4. Existing _get/get_me/get_currently_playing/get_recently_played are unchanged
5. Verify implementation matches spec.module-spotify sections: all tool requirements
title: Extend SpotifyClient with write methods and discovery endpoints

description:
Create src/butlers/modules/spotify.py implementing the Module ABC. Registers 22 MCP tools as thin wrappers over the extended SpotifyClient.

Module structure:
- name = 'spotify', config_schema = SpotifyModuleConfig (playback_tools: bool = True)
- on_startup: resolve credentials from CredentialStore (spotify category), construct SpotifyClient, call get_me() to cache user profile (including product tier for Premium detection)
- on_shutdown: close SpotifyClient
- register_tools: register all 22 tools grouped by function (search, discovery, playback state, playback control, playlist, library)
- tool_metadata: read sensitivity for search/get tools, write sensitivity for mutation tools
- Missing credentials → actionable error on all tool calls
- Premium-required → actionable error on playback control tools (403 handling)

Tool groups:
1. Search: spotify_search
2. Discovery: spotify_get_recommendations, spotify_get_related_artists

description:

Workflow:
1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.

description: compute_deliver_at in src/butlers/core/temporal/delivery.py currently ignores prefs['timezone'] and schedules batch delivery in UTC only. The spec (time-aware-delivery/spec.md §Deferred Notification Storage) requires deliver_at to be computed in the user's configured timezone. title: temporal-intelligence: timezone-aware compute_deliver_at in delivery.py

description:
Spec requirement (dunbar-tier-scoring/spec.md, Requirement: Scoring eligibility — entity-contact linkage, Scenario: Archived contacts excluded from ranking): 'the contact MUST still report its last-computed dunbar_tier and dunbar_score if queried directly, but marked as stale'.

Current implementation: contact_get() calls get_contact_dunbar() which calls compute_tier_ranking(). compute_dunbar_scores() already excludes listed=false contacts (archived), so archived contacts will return tier=1500, score=0.0 always — which is NOT their 'last-computed' tier/score, and there is no 'stale' marker.

Gap: There is no persistence of last-computed Dunbar tier/score for archived contacts, and no 'stale' flag in the contact_get response.

This is a low-priority gap because the main use case (overdue list, urgency) correctly excludes archived contacts. It only matters for direct contact_get queries on archived contacts.

Possible fix: Cache last computed dunbar_tier/dunbar_score in a contacts column or fact, and add a dunbar_stale boolean to contact_get response for listed=false contacts.
title: Archived contacts should report stale dunbar_tier/score when queried directly

description:
Education butler stores memory facts with free-text subject strings (e.g. 'Cache coherence (MESI/MOESI)', 'cache coherency'). This creates fragmented unidentified entities in shared.entities — one per unique subject phrasing — when the transitory entity backfill runs. All variations should consolidate under a single canonical entity per mind map node.

Fix: auto-create a shared.entities entry when each mind_map_node is created, link it via a new entity_id column on education.mind_map_nodes, and update all education skills to pass entity_id to memory_store_fact(). This makes facts dedup correctly via (entity_id, scope, predicate) instead of (subject, predicate).

description:
The connector-filtered-events spec requires filter_reason for label-exclusion filters to be formatted as 'label_exclude:<label_name>' (e.g., 'label_exclude:CATEGORY_PROMOTIONS'). However, gmail_policy.py's LabelFilterPolicy.evaluate() returns 'label_excluded:<label_name>' (note 'd' suffix) which is used directly as filter_reason.

The FilteredEventBuffer.reason_label_exclude(label) helper was created per spec and produces the correct format, but it is NOT called for label-exclude events. Instead, policy_result.filter_reason is passed directly to record().

Gap to fix:
- In gmail.py _ingest_single_message(), when policy_result.should_ingest is False due to a label exclusion, extract the label name from policy_result.filter_reason and call FilteredEventBuffer.reason_label_exclude(label) instead of passing policy_result.filter_reason directly.
- OR update gmail_policy.py LabelFilterPolicy.evaluate() to return the spec-compliant 'label_exclude:' format.

Note: gmail_policy currently returns comma-separated labels if multiple are matched (e.g., 'label_excluded:SPAM,TRASH'). The spec only shows a single label in the example. Decide how to handle multiple matched labels.

Reference: openspec/changes/add-connectors-schema/specs/connector-filtered-events/spec.md (Filter reason format scenario)
title: Fix label-exclude filter_reason format mismatch in Gmail connector

description:
Add lifecycle management to predicate_registry following the universal pattern from Wikidata/Schema.org/OWL: never delete, always deprecate.

**Schema:**
- Add status TEXT DEFAULT 'active' — values: active, deprecated, proposed
- Add superseded_by TEXT (FK to predicate_registry name, nullable)
- Add deprecated_at TIMESTAMPTZ

**Write-time behavior:**
- If predicate has status='deprecated', store_fact() MUST still succeed (backward compat)
- BUT the response MUST include a 'warning' key with the superseded_by suggestion
- The LLM learns to use the canonical replacement on subsequent calls

**Migration for existing unused predicates:**
- Deprecate the 36 unused baseline predicates from migration 005 that were superseded by domain-specific versions in 009-011
- Example: baseline medication (005) → superseded_by health-domain medication (009)
- Example: medication_frequency, dosage, condition_status, symptom_pattern, symptom_trigger → superseded_by their domain counterparts

**Auto-registered predicate lifecycle:**
- Auto-registered predicates start with status='proposed'
- After usage_count > N (configurable threshold), promote to status='active'
- Predicates with usage_count=0 and age > 30 days are candidates for cleanup (dashboard/manual)

**Dashboard support:**
- Predicate list view shows status badges (active/deprecated/proposed)
- Deprecated predicates show superseded_by link
- Filter by status in predicate list

Spec: openspec/changes/predicate-registry-enforcement/ (new requirement needed)
title: Predicate lifecycle: status, deprecation, and supersedence

description:
attached_molecule: bu-wisp-a0mr
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-03-06T05:09:15Z
dispatched_by: mayor

The module-contacts spec defines multi-provider support but only single-provider is implemented.

**Spec requirements (not implemented)**:
1. ContactsConfig should accept a 'providers' list (e.g., [{type: "google"}, {type: "telegram"}]) with legacy single-provider compat
2. ContactsSyncRuntime should manage independent sync loops per provider
3. Provider failure isolation: one provider failing doesn't affect others
4. contacts_sync_now should accept provider filter (sync one or all)
5. contacts_sync_status should return per-provider sync state
6. contacts_source_list should list all configured provider accounts
7. Duplicate provider type validation at startup

**Current state**: ContactsConfig has provider: str (single provider). ContactsModule has _provider (singular) and _runtime (singular). MCP tools validate against a single configured provider.

**Impact**: Users cannot sync from both Google and Telegram simultaneously. They must choose one provider per butler.

**Fix approach**:
- ContactsConfig: Add 'providers' list field, keep 'provider' string as legacy compat
- ContactsModule: Replace _provider/_runtime with dicts keyed by provider name
- ContactsSyncRuntime: Create one runtime per provider in on_startup()
- MCP tools: Support per-provider and all-providers queries
- Error handling: Isolate failures per provider

**Files**: src/butlers/modules/contacts/__init__.py (ContactsConfig, ContactsModule)
title: Multi-provider contacts configuration and runtime

description:
Wire together the buffer infrastructure (child 1) and history fetching (child 2)
into the complete flush pipeline with batch envelope construction.

Covers spec sections:
- Requirement: Batch Envelope Format (all scenarios)
- Requirement: Policy and Discretion on Batch (all scenarios)
- Requirement: Checkpoint Advancement (all scenarios)

Work:
1. Implement _build_batch_envelope(chat_id, buffered_messages, context_messages) -> dict:
   - event.external_event_id = "batch:<chat_id>:<min_id>-<max_id>"
   - sender.identity = "multiple"
   - payload.normalized_text = concatenated NEW messages with sender prefixes
     (format: "[sender_id]: message text")
   - payload.conversation_history = ordered list of all context messages with
     fields: message_id, sender_id, text, timestamp (ISO 8601), is_new, reply_to
   - control.idempotency_key = "tg_batch:<chat_id>:<min_id>:<max_id>"
2. Implement _flush_chat_buffer(chat_id) — the full pipeline:
   a. Atomically swap buffer (take messages, reset list)
   b. Call _fetch_conversation_history()
   c. Call _resolve_reply_tos()
   d. Build batch envelope
   e. Evaluate ingestion policy (chat_id as raw_key)
   f. Evaluate discretion on concatenated normalized_text
   g. Submit via _submit_to_ingest()
   h. Advance checkpoint to max(msg.id for msg in buffered_messages)
   i. Record filtered events for policy/discretion rejections
3. Replace the stub flush from child 1 with this real implementation

description:
Implement the foundational buffering layer that replaces immediate per-message
submission with per-chat accumulation and timed flushing.

Covers spec sections:
- Requirement: Per-Chat Message Buffering (all scenarios)
- Requirement: Timed Flush Interval (all scenarios)
- Requirement: Configuration Environment Variables

Work:
1. Create ChatBuffer dataclass (messages list, last_flush_ts, asyncio.Lock)
2. Add _chat_buffers dict to TelegramUserClientConnector.__init__
3. Parse new env vars: TELEGRAM_USER_FLUSH_INTERVAL_S, TELEGRAM_USER_HISTORY_MAX_MESSAGES,
   TELEGRAM_USER_HISTORY_TIME_WINDOW_M, TELEGRAM_USER_BUFFER_MAX_MESSAGES
4. Implement _buffer_message(message): extract chat_id, create/append to ChatBuffer,
   force-flush on buffer cap (200 messages)
5. Replace the immediate _process_message submission path with _buffer_message
6. Implement _flush_scanner_loop(): async task running every 60s, iterates all
   chat buffers, flushes any where now - last_flush_ts >= flush_interval
7. Start flush scanner in start(), cancel in stop()
8. Force-flush all non-empty buffers in stop() for graceful shutdown

Note: The actual flush pipeline (_flush_chat_buffer) is implemented in child 3.
This bead provides the buffer infrastructure and timer; the flush method can be
a stub that logs + clears the buffer until the pipeline bead lands.

description:
Use /excalidraw-diagram to UPDATE docs/diagrams/01-system-architecture.excalidraw.

This is the top-level topology map of the entire Butlers system. Read the existing file first and evolve it.

## What to show

### Butlers (blue boxes)
- Switchboard (port 40100) — ingress router, message classification, pipeline
- General (port 40101) — freeform catch-all assistant
- Relationship (port 40102) — personal CRM
- Health (port 40103) — health tracking
- Messenger (port 40104) — outbound delivery plane (Telegram, Email)
- Finance (port 40105) — receipts, bills, subscriptions
- Travel (port 40106) — itineraries, trip planning
- Education (port 40107) — spaced repetition, mind maps, adaptive learning
- Home (port 40108) — smart home automation via Home Assistant

### Connectors (green boxes, standalone processes)
- telegram_bot.py — Telegram bot polling/webhooks
- telegram_user_client.py — Telegram MTProto user client (Telethon)
- gmail.py — Gmail watch/history delta ingestion
- discord_user.py — Discord Gateway WebSocket (DRAFT)
- Each connector has: health_socket.py (liveness probe), heartbeat.py (stats reporting to Switchboard), cursor_store.py (durable checkpoints)

### Database (orange)
- Single PostgreSQL instance with schema isolation: one schema per butler + shared schema
- shared schema: contacts, contact_info, model_catalog, ingestion_events, credentials

### LLM Runtimes (purple)
- Ephemeral subprocesses spawned by each butler's Spawner
- Model routing from shared.model_catalog with per-butler overrides
- Supported runtimes: Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI (via RuntimeAdapter ABC)

### Dashboard (teal)
- FastAPI gateway with 21+ core routers + 8 butler-specific routers (auto-discovered)
- SSE streaming endpoint for real-time updates
- OAuth 2.0 bootstrap flow

### External Channels (gray)
- Telegram, Gmail, Discord, Home Assistant

### Arrows
- Connectors → Switchboard (ingest.v1 envelopes)
- Switchboard → specialist butlers (routed messages via MCP)
- Butlers → Messenger (outbound delivery requests)
- Butlers → PostgreSQL (schema-isolated reads/writes)
- Dashboard → PostgreSQL (read-only queries)
- Dashboard → Butlers (MCP probes for live status)

### Color Legend
butlers=blue, connectors=green, DB=orange, LLM=purple, dashboard=teal, external channels=gray

Reference: roster/*/butler.toml, src/butlers/connectors/, src/butlers/api/routers/, src/butlers/core/model_routing.py

Home butler (port 40108) — smart home automation orchestrator. Read existing file first.

## What to show

### Enabled Modules
home_assistant, memory, contacts, approvals

### Scheduled Tasks
- weekly-energy-digest: Sun 9pm (0 21 * * 0)
- environment-report: Sun 9:05pm (5 21 * * 0)
- device-health-check: Sun 9:10pm (10 21 * * 0)
- memory_consolidation: every 6h
- memory_episode_cleanup: daily 4:05am
- memory_purge_superseded: daily 4:10am

### Primary User Flow (numbered sequence)
Actors: User, Telegram, Switchboard, Home Butler, Home Assistant Module, Approvals
1. User requests home action (e.g., "turn off lights", "set thermostat")
2. Switchboard routes to Home butler
3. LLM evaluates request via home_assistant module
4. If sensitive action → Approvals module gates execution (human-in-the-loop)
5. Home Assistant API called
6. Confirmation sent back

### Scheduled Flows
- Energy digest: aggregate energy usage data → weekly summary
- Environment report: temperature, humidity, air quality summary
- Device health: check device statuses, flag offline/unhealthy devices

### Skills
- weekly-energy-digest, environment-report, device-health-check, comfort, energy, scenes, troubleshooting

### Approvals Integration (NEW)
- Sensitivity detection for home actions
- Rule engine for auto-approve vs require approval
- Redaction of sensitive parameters

Messenger butler (port 40104) — outbound delivery execution plane. Read existing file first.

## What to show

### Enabled Modules
calendar, telegram, email, approvals, messenger (roster-specific)

### Scheduled Tasks
None — Messenger is purely reactive (triggered by other butlers)

### Architecture (this butler is unique)
- NOT a domain specialist — infrastructure-only delivery plane
- Centralizes all outbound communication (Telegram, Email)
- Other butlers call notify() → Switchboard routes to Messenger → Messenger delivers
- Consistent delivery outcomes, retry logic, delivery tracking

### Primary Flow (numbered sequence)
Actors: Source Butler, Switchboard, Messenger Butler, Telegram Module, Email Module, User
1. Source butler calls notify() with contact_id + intent
2. Switchboard routes notification to Messenger
3. Messenger resolves contact_id → shared.contact_info (channel + address)
4. Channel selection: Telegram chat ID or email address
5. Telegram module sends via Bot API OR Email module sends via SMTP
6. Delivery status recorded
7. If delivery fails → retry with backoff

### Approvals Integration
- Sensitive outbound messages may require approval
- Approval gates before delivery execution

### Channel Resolution
- shared.contacts → shared.contact_info lookup
- Multiple channels per contact (Telegram, Email)
- Channel preference / fallback logic

Reference: roster/messenger/butler.toml, roster/messenger/MANIFESTO.md, openspec/specs/butler-messenger/

description:
Use /excalidraw-diagram to UPDATE docs/diagrams/05-connector-design.excalidraw.

Connectors are standalone processes that normalize external events to ingest.v1 format. Read existing file first.

## What to show

### Connector as Standalone Process (NOT a butler)
- Transport-only: classification & routing are Switchboard responsibilities
- Each connector runs as its own process with health socket + heartbeat

### Lifecycle Loop
1. Start → initialize credentials + cursor store
2. Connect to external service (poll or webhook)
3. Receive raw event
4. Normalize to ingest.v1 envelope
5. Send to Switchboard via CachedMCPClient
6. Update cursor (checkpoint)
7. Loop to step 3

### ingest.v1 Envelope Exploded View
Fields: source (connector name), event (type + timestamp), sender (identity), payload (normalized message), control (dedup key, priority hints)

### Deduplication Decision Tree (3 tiers)
- Tier 1: Exact message ID dedup (connector-level)
- Tier 2: Content hash dedup (Switchboard-level)
- Tier 3: Semantic dedup (LLM-level, optional)

### Infrastructure Components
- cursor_store.py — Durable checkpoint storage (KV interface), restart-safe resume
- health_socket.py — NEW: Kubernetes-style liveness/readiness probes on configurable port
- heartbeat.py — NEW: Background asyncio task reporting liveness + operational stats to Switchboard
- mcp_client.py — CachedMCPClient (httpx-based, lazy connect, health-check + retry)
- filtered_event_buffer.py — NEW: In-memory buffer for poll-cycle filtered/errored events, batch INSERT on flush, lost on crash (intentional)
- metrics.py — NEW: Prometheus counters for tier assignment, ingest attempts, errors

### Implemented Connectors (green boxes)
- gmail.py — Gmail watch/history delta flow, durable historyId cursor, push notifications + bounded polling fallback
- gmail_policy.py — NEW: Three-layer tier assignment (label filtering → tier assignment → ingestion classification)
- telegram_bot.py — Bot polling (dev) or webhooks (prod), durable offset cursor
- telegram_user_client.py — MTProto user-account via Telethon, privacy-sensitive, requires consent
- discord_user.py — NEW (DRAFT): Discord Gateway WebSocket, guild/channel allowlist

### Crash-Safe Checkpoints
- cursor_store.py provides restart-safe resume
- On crash: connector restarts from last checkpoint, may re-process (dedup handles)

### Rate Limiting
- Per-connector configurable rate limits
- Backpressure via buffer sizing

Reference: src/butlers/connectors/, openspec/specs/connector-base-spec/, openspec/specs/connector-gmail/, openspec/specs/connector-telegram-bot/

description:
Use /excalidraw-diagram to UPDATE docs/diagrams/06a-spawner-runtime.excalidraw.

The Spawner is a core component that invokes ephemeral AI runtime instances. Read existing file first.

## What to show

### Spawner Class (src/butlers/core/spawner.py)
- trigger() method: entry point for spawning an LLM session
- Generates locked-down MCP config pointing only to the butler's own MCP server
- Serial dispatch: one session at a time per butler (configurable max concurrency)

### Model Routing Integration (NEW)
- model_routing.py: resolve_model() called before spawn
- Complexity enum: trivial, medium, high, extra_high
- Resolution: single-query LEFT JOIN on shared.model_catalog + per-butler overrides
- Butler TOML can override default model per complexity level

### Runtime Adapter Pattern (NEW)
- runtimes/base.py: RuntimeAdapter ABC
- binary_name property, invoke() method
- Factory: get_adapter() returns appropriate adapter
- Supported runtimes: Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI

### Spawn Sequence (numbered)
1. Trigger arrives (external MCP call or scheduler)
2. resolve_model() determines which model/runtime to use
3. get_adapter() selects RuntimeAdapter
4. Generate ephemeral MCP config (locked-down: only this butler's tools)
5. Spawn subprocess via RuntimeAdapter.invoke()
6. LLM calls butler's MCP tools, runs skills
7. Session completes → log to sessions table
8. Process stdout/stderr captured via session_process_logs.py

### Buffer Integration (NEW)
- buffer.py: DurableBuffer with 3 priority tiers
- Triggers queued in buffer when concurrency limit reached
- In-memory hot path + DB cold scanner for crash recovery
- Starvation guards prevent low-priority trigger starvation

### Metrics (NEW)
- OTel instruments: spawner_concurrency gauge, token_usage counter
- Session duration histogram

### Error Handling
- Timeout handling for long-running sessions
- Crash recovery: buffer scanner re-enqueues stale triggers

Reference: src/butlers/core/spawner.py, src/butlers/core/model_routing.py, src/butlers/core/runtimes/, src/butlers/core/buffer.py, src/butlers/core/sessions.py

description:
Use /excalidraw-diagram to UPDATE docs/diagrams/06b-scheduler.excalidraw.

Core scheduler drives cron-based task dispatch. Read existing file first.

## What to show

### Scheduler Component (src/butlers/core/scheduler.py)
- Cron-driven task dispatch from butler.toml [[butler.schedule]] entries
- TOML config sync: schedule entries synced to DB on startup
- Tick loop: evaluates cron expressions at each tick_interval_seconds
- Due task → triggers Spawner.trigger() with task context

### Tick Loop (numbered sequence)
1. Timer fires (every tick_interval_seconds)
2. Load schedule entries from DB
3. For each entry: evaluate cron expression against current time
4. If due: enqueue trigger in Buffer (or call Spawner.trigger() directly)
5. Mark task as dispatched (prevent re-fire within window)
6. Log dispatch metrics

### Schedule Entry Structure
From butler.toml:
- name: task identifier
- cron: standard 5-field cron expression
- description: human-readable purpose
- skill: optional skill to invoke

### TOML ↔ DB Sync
- On startup: read [[butler.schedule]] from TOML
- Upsert to DB (preserve manual overrides)
- _normalize_schedule_projection_fields() for field consistency

### Integration Points
- Spawner: scheduler dispatches to spawner
- Buffer: if spawner busy, trigger goes to buffer queue
- Metrics: scheduler_dispatch counter via OTel

### Example Schedule (from General butler)
- memory_consolidation: 0 */6 * * *
- memory_episode_cleanup: 0 4 * * *
- eod-tomorrow-prep: 0 15 * * *

Reference: src/butlers/core/scheduler.py, roster/general/butler.toml

description:
Use /excalidraw-diagram to UPDATE docs/diagrams/06c-state-store.excalidraw.

Core KV JSONB state store for butler persistence. Read existing file first.

## What to show

### State Store (src/butlers/core/state.py)
- Key-value JSONB store backed by PostgreSQL
- Per-butler schema isolation (each butler writes to its own schema)
- CAS (Compare-And-Swap) conflict detection via CASConflictError
- decode_jsonb() helper for JSONB → Python conversion

### Operations
- get(key) → value (JSONB)
- set(key, value) — upsert with JSONB encoding
- delete(key) — remove entry
- list(prefix) — list keys matching prefix
- cas_update(key, expected, new) — atomic CAS with conflict detection

### Schema Isolation
- Table per butler schema: {butler_name}.state
- Columns: key (text PK), value (jsonb), updated_at (timestamp)
- No cross-butler state access (enforced by schema)

### CAS Pattern
1. Read current value + version
2. Modify locally
3. cas_update with expected version
4. If version mismatch → CASConflictError → retry

### Integration Points
- Used by modules for persistent config/state
- Used by scheduler for task tracking
- Used by memory module for episodic state

Reference: src/butlers/core/state.py

description:
Use /excalidraw-diagram to UPDATE docs/diagrams/06d-startup-sequence.excalidraw.

Butler daemon startup and shutdown sequence. Read existing file first.

## What to show

### Startup Sequence (numbered, from src/butlers/daemon.py)
1. Load TOML config (roster/{name}/butler.toml)
2. Connect to PostgreSQL (asyncpg pool)
3. Run Alembic migrations (topologically sorted by module dependencies)
4. Initialize modules in dependency order (registry.py topological sort)
5. Call on_startup() for each module
6. Register all module MCP tools via register_tools()
7. Start FastMCP SSE server
8. Launch Switchboard heartbeat (non-switchboard butlers only — 300s TTL)
9. Start internal scheduler tick loop (_tick() every tick_interval_seconds)
10. Start liveness reporter (POST to Switchboard heartbeat endpoint)
11. NEW: Initialize telemetry (OTel TracerProvider, per-butler span attributes)
12. NEW: Initialize metrics instruments (spawner concurrency, token usage, buffer health)

### Graceful Shutdown Sequence (numbered)
1. Stop MCP server (stop accepting new connections)
2. Drain active sessions (configurable timeout)
3. Cancel heartbeat/scheduler/reporter background tasks
4. Shutdown modules in REVERSE topological order (on_shutdown())
5. Close DB connection pool

### Module Dependency Resolution
- registry.py scans built-in butlers.modules + roster/{butler}/modules/__init__.py
- Topological sort on dependencies property
- Startup: sorted order; Shutdown: reverse order
- Circular dependency detection

### Error Handling
- If any startup step fails → graceful shutdown of already-started components
- Module startup failure → skip module, log error (or fail-fast depending on config)

Reference: src/butlers/daemon.py, src/butlers/modules/registry.py

description:

PostgreSQL database schema with per-butler isolation. Read existing file first.

## What to show

### Schema Architecture
- Single PostgreSQL database: "butlers"
- Per-butler schemas: switchboard, general, relationship, health, messenger, finance, travel, education, home
- Shared schema: cross-butler identity and configuration tables

### shared Schema Tables
- shared.contacts — canonical contact registry, one row per person/actor, roles array (e.g. ["owner"]), optional entity_id FK
- shared.contact_info — per-channel identifiers (type, value), UNIQUE on (type, value), secured flag for credentials
- shared.model_catalog — NEW: model registry for dynamic model routing, model_id, provider, capabilities
- shared.ingestion_events — NEW: canonical ingest envelope registry, UUID7 PK, session fan-out tracking, replay capability
- shared.credentials — credential storage (encrypted)

### Per-Butler Schema Tables (representative)
- {butler}.state — KV JSONB store (key PK, value JSONB, updated_at)
- {butler}.sessions — append-only session log (trigger source, cost, lifecycle)
- {butler}.schedule — cron task definitions synced from TOML
- {butler}.route_inbox — NEW: durable work queue (lifecycle states)
- {butler}.buffer_queue — NEW: durable buffer overflow (priority, enqueued_at)

### Switchboard-Specific Tables
- switchboard.dashboard_audit_log — audit trail from core/audit.py
- switchboard.heartbeat_registry — connector/butler liveness records

### Module Migration Pattern
- Each module declares migration_revisions() → Alembic branch label
- Migrations run in topological dependency order
- Alembic versions in src/butlers/migrations/versions/

### Identity Resolution Flow
1. Inbound message arrives with channel-specific ID
2. Lookup shared.contact_info WHERE (type, value) matches
3. Join to shared.contacts for canonical identity
4. Route to appropriate butler schema

Reference: src/butlers/core/state.py, src/butlers/core/sessions.py, src/butlers/core/ingestion_events.py, src/butlers/core/route_inbox.py, src/butlers/core/model_routing.py, src/butlers/migrations/

8 butler routers: education, finance, general, health, home, relationship, switchboard, travel

### Router Discovery Mechanism (flowchart)
1. Scan roster/ directory
2. Check for {butler}/api/router.py
3. Load module dynamically via importlib
4. Validate "router" export exists
5. Wire DB dependencies
6. Mount under /api/butlers/{butler}/...

### Middleware Stack
- CORS middleware
- Authentication (OAuth + CLI auth)
- Error handling
- Request logging

Key data flows through the dashboard API. Read existing file first.

## What to show

### Flow 1: Session Viewer
Actors: Browser, Dashboard API, PostgreSQL
1. Browser requests GET /api/sessions (with filters: butler, date range, trigger)
2. sessions.py router queries cross-butler session tables
3. Returns paginated session list with cost metadata
4. Browser requests session detail → includes tool calls, process logs

### Flow 2: Memory Browser
Actors: Browser, Dashboard API, PostgreSQL
1. Browser requests GET /api/memory/episodes (with butler filter)
2. memory.py router queries butler's memory tables
3. Returns episodes with entities, facts, rules
4. Search endpoint: GET /api/memory/search with embedding similarity

### Flow 3: Approval Workflow
Actors: Browser, Dashboard API, PostgreSQL, Butler
1. Butler hits approval gate → pending action created
2. Browser polls GET /api/approvals/pending
3. User reviews and POST /api/approvals/approve or /reject
4. Butler resumes execution with decision

### Flow 4: Cost Tracking
Actors: Browser, Dashboard API, PostgreSQL
1. Browser requests GET /api/costs (date range, butler filter)
2. costs.py aggregates token usage from sessions
3. Returns per-butler, per-model cost breakdown

### Flow 5: Calendar Workspace
Actors: Browser, Dashboard API, Google Calendar
1. Browser requests GET /api/calendar/events
2. calendar_workspace.py queries across all butlers with calendar module
3. Aggregates events from multiple calendar providers

### Flow 6: SSE Streaming (NEW)
Actors: Browser, Dashboard API, Butler Events
1. Browser opens GET /api/sse (EventSource)
2. Server pushes real-time events: session starts/completions, approval requests, notifications
3. Browser updates UI reactively

### Flow 7: Ingestion Event Viewer (NEW)
Actors: Browser, Dashboard API, PostgreSQL
1. Browser requests GET /api/ingestion-events
2. ingestion_events.py queries shared.ingestion_events
3. Returns envelope list with session fan-out rollup
4. Drill-down: individual event with all spawned sessions

### Flow 8: Model Settings (NEW)
Actors: Browser, Dashboard API, PostgreSQL
1. Browser requests GET /api/models/catalog
2. model_settings.py queries shared.model_catalog
3. PUT /api/butlers/{name}/model-overrides for per-butler customization
4. Changes take effect on next spawn

description:

This diagram shows the two-layer butler design (core + modules). Read existing file first.

## What to show

### Core Ring (inner, always present)
Components with brief descriptions:
- state.py — KV JSONB store (PostgreSQL), CAS conflict detection
- scheduler.py — Cron-driven task dispatch, TOML config sync
- spawner.py — Ephemeral LLM CLI invocation, locked-down MCP config generation
- sessions.py — Append-only session log with lifecycle tracking
- buffer.py — NEW: Durable hybrid queue (in-memory hot path + DB cold scanner), 3 priority tiers (high_priority, interactive, default) with starvation guards
- model_routing.py — NEW: Dynamic model resolution from shared.model_catalog, Complexity enum (trivial/medium/high/extra_high), per-butler overrides via LEFT JOIN
- route_inbox.py — NEW: Durable work queue for async route dispatch, lifecycle states (accepted/processing/processed/errored), crash recovery
- ingestion_events.py — NEW: Query functions for shared.ingestion_events, UUID7 PK, session fan-out rollup, replay capability
- telemetry.py — NEW: OpenTelemetry initialization, span wrappers, trace context propagation, per-butler span attributes
- audit.py — NEW: Fire-and-forget audit logging to Switchboard dashboard_audit_log table
- metrics.py — NEW: OTel metrics instruments for spawner concurrency, token usage, buffer health, scheduler dispatch
- skills.py — System prompt loading with @include directives, AGENTS.md read/write, skill directory scanning
- logging.py — Structured logging (text/JSON), structlog with OTel trace context injection
- tool_call_capture.py — NEW: Tool call recording
- mcp_urls.py — NEW: MCP URL resolution
- session_process_logs.py — Process-level stdout/stderr capture
- utils.py — Shared utilities
- runtimes/ — RuntimeAdapter ABC with factory get_adapter(); supports Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI

### Modules Ring (outer, opt-in per butler)
Module interface (from base.py Module ABC):
- name property, config_schema (Pydantic), dependencies (list[str])
- register_tools(mcp, config, db), migration_revisions() -> str|None
- on_startup(config, db, credential_store), on_shutdown()
- tool_metadata() -> dict[str, ToolMeta] — NEW: per-tool sensitivity metadata for approval gates

Available modules:
- email.py — IMAP/SMTP tools
- calendar.py — Provider-agnostic (Google Calendar provider)
- telegram.py — Output-only send/reply tools
- pipeline.py — NEW: Message classification & routing pipeline
- approvals/ — NEW: Human-in-the-loop approval gates, rule engine, sensitivity detection, redaction
- contacts/ — NEW: Contact registry sync (Google People API + Telegram provider)
- mailbox/ — NEW: Local message queue for inter-butler/external comms (5 MCP tools)
- memory/ — NEW: Episodic memory with entities, facts, rules, search (18 MCP tools)
- metrics/ — NEW: Prometheus integration (counter/gauge/histogram + PromQL)
- registry.py — Module registry with topological dependency sort, dynamic roster module loading

### MCP SSE Transport
- FastMCP server, tool registration from modules
- Locked-down ephemeral MCP config for spawned LLM sessions

### Config Directory Tree
roster/{name}/ structure:
- MANIFESTO.md, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, butler.toml
- .agents/skills/ (Codex discovery)
- api/router.py + models.py (optional dashboard routes)

### DB Schema + Alembic
- Per-butler schema + shared schema in single PostgreSQL DB
- Module migration_revisions() for Alembic branch labels

Reference: src/butlers/core/, src/butlers/modules/base.py, src/butlers/modules/registry.py, roster/general/butler.toml

Switchboard (port 40100) is the intelligent front door routing incoming messages to specialist butlers. Read existing file first.

## What to show

### Switchboard MCP Tools
Tools registered by the Switchboard module (check roster/switchboard/ for current list). Include:
- Ingestion acceptance tools (accept ingest envelopes from connectors)
- Route dispatch tools (forward classified messages to target butlers)
- Heartbeat registration (connectors report liveness)
- Eligibility sweep (scheduled every 5 minutes — checks butler liveness TTLs)

### Pipeline Module Integration
- pipeline.py bridges input modules to Switchboard MCP
- Message classification flow: raw envelope → signal extraction → domain classification → target butler selection
- Multi-butler fan-out when message spans domains

### Ingestion Flow (numbered sequence, swim lanes)
Actors: Connector, Switchboard, Pipeline, Target Butler
1. Connector sends ingest.v1 envelope to Switchboard MCP
2. Switchboard writes to shared.ingestion_events (UUID7 PK)
3. Pipeline classifies message (signal extraction → domain matching)
4. Route inbox entry created (accepted state)
5. Route dispatched to target butler MCP (processing state)
6. Target butler processes and returns result (processed state)
7. Session fan-out recorded in ingestion_events

### Route Inbox (NEW)
- Durable work queue for async route.execute() calls
- Lifecycle states: accepted → processing → processed | errored
- Crash recovery via DB scanner polling stale entries

### Scheduled Tasks Sidebar
- eligibility_sweep: every 5 minutes — check butler liveness TTLs
- memory_consolidation: every 6 hours
- memory_episode_cleanup: daily at 4am

### Enabled Modules
calendar, telegram, memory, email, pipeline, switchboard

### Skills
- message-triage, signal-extraction, conversation-context, relationship-extractor, routed-message-safety

7. Pre-trip checklist generated

### Scheduled Flows
- Upcoming travel: scan trips departing within 7 days → send prep checklist
- Document expiry: check passport/visa dates → alert if expiring within 6 months

### Skills
- upcoming-travel-check, document-expiry-check, trip-planner, pre-trip-checklist, tool-reference

description:
## What

Consolidate core AND daemon tests from 1,869 tests (1,453 core + 416 daemon)
to ~350 focused behavioral tests. Separate contract tests (extracted to
tests/contracts/ in Phase 1) from implementation tests.

## Why

Core + daemon is the highest-value test area — it covers daemon lifecycle,
scheduler, spawner, ingestion events, state store, session management, and
the 17-phase startup sequence. But even here, many tests verify internal
helper functions rather than behavioral contracts.

## Scope

### tests/core/ (57 files, 1,453 tests)
Top offenders:
- test_temporal_intelligence.py: 113 tests — internal calculation helper
- test_core_spawner.py: 95 tests, 270 asserts — keep MCP config gen + session lifecycle
- test_core_scheduler.py: 81 tests, 196 asserts — keep cron eval + task dispatch
- test_ingestion_events.py: 91 tests — keep state machine transitions

Resolve duplicate pairs:
- test_context_bus.py + test_context_bus_unit.py → merge
- test_seasonal.py + test_seasonal_unit.py → merge

### tests/daemon/ (27 files, 416 tests) — NOW INCLUDED IN THIS BEAD
- Startup phase tests: keep behavioral tests for 17-phase sequence (RFC 0001)
- Shutdown tests: keep drain and reverse-order on_shutdown
- Trigger dispatch: keep behavioral tests
- Delete: internal helper tests, mock-wiring tests

### Session trace context (RFC 0001)
Ensure surviving tests cover:
- request_id UUIDv7 propagation end-to-end
- Tool call capture into session record
- Token usage tracking
- Session failure/success marking

## Strategy

After Phase 1 extracts contract tests:
1. For each core/daemon file, identify tests already covered by contract tests
2. Remove duplicates
3. Keep behavioral tests for: scheduler (cron evaluation, task dispatch),
   spawner (MCP config generation, session lifecycle), state store (KV ops),
   daemon (startup phases, shutdown, triggers)
4. Prune internal helper tests (temporal intelligence calculations, etc.)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Core + daemon tests reduced to ≤400 tests across ≤30 files
2. No duplicate test files (eliminate _unit.py pairs)
3. Contract-level tests live in tests/contracts/ (not duplicated here)
4. Behavioral tests cover: scheduler, spawner, state store, session mgmt, daemon lifecycle
5. Session trace context tests cover request_id propagation and tool call capture
6. Full test suite still passes
title: Consolidate core tests from 1,453 to ~300
