description:
Deep-dive review: compare the original spec/requirements (openspec/changes/unified-ingestion-policy/) against the implementation delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

## Workflow

1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Audit the codebase changes delivered by each sibling bead.
3. Produce a checklist mapping every spec requirement to its implementing bead.
4. For any requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
   c. Do NOT create another reconciliation bead.

## Cruft Cleanup Audit (MANDATORY)

Apply the cruft-cleanup rules (see ~/.dotfiles/genai/skills/cruft-cleanup/SKILL.md). The core rule: **if you changed it, finish the job.** Every callsite, import, reference, and test must use the new interface. The old one is deleted — not deprecated, not re-exported, not aliased.

Run this full audit checklist against the codebase after all implementation beads are closed:

### A. Zero Legacy References (grep sweep)

grep -r for each of these patterns across src/, roster/, frontend/src/, tests/ (--include *.py *.ts *.tsx). ZERO matches required:

Old evaluator/cache/telemetry classes:
- SourceFilterEvaluator, SourceFilterSpec, FilterResult
- TriageRuleCache, TriageEnvelope, TriageDecision, evaluate_triage, _run_triage
- TriageTelemetry, ThreadAffinityTelemetry (if replaced)

Old module paths:
- from butlers.connectors.source_filter import
- from roster.switchboard.tools.triage import (evaluator|cache|telemetry)
- extract_gmail_filter_key, extract_telegram_filter_key, extract_telethon_filter_key, extract_discord_filter_key

Old API endpoints/models:
- /source-filters, /triage-rules, /connectors/.*/filters
- SourceFilter, SourceFilterCreate, SourceFilterUpdate
- TriageRule, TriageRuleCreate, TriageRuleUpdate
- ConnectorFilterAssignment, ConnectorFilterAssignmentItem

Old frontend components/hooks:
- ConnectorFiltersDialog, ManageSourceFiltersPanel
- useSourceFilters, useCreateSourceFilter, useUpdateSourceFilter, useDeleteSourceFilter
- useConnectorFilters, useUpdateConnectorFilters
- useTriageRules, useCreateTriageRule, useUpdateTriageRule, useDeleteTriageRule, useTestTriageRule

Old table names in non-migration code:
- triage_rules (outside of migration files)
- source_filters, connector_source_filters (outside of migration files)

### B. Anti-Pattern Sweep

Verify NONE of these cruft anti-patterns exist:

- [ ] No re-exports or aliases mapping old names to new (e.g., SourceFilterEvaluator = IngestionPolicyEvaluator)
- [ ] No old module paths that re-export from the new location (e.g., source_filter.py importing from ingestion_policy.py)
- [ ] No feature flags or conditionals guarding the old vs new path (e.g., if use_new_evaluator:)
- [ ] No wrapper functions whose only purpose is signature translation between old FilterResult and new PolicyDecision
- [ ] No tombstone comments (# removed, # deprecated, # legacy, # TODO: delete after migration)
- [ ] No unused _var assignments kept for backward compat
- [ ] No try/except blocks catching errors from removed code paths (e.g., except LegacyFilterError)
- [ ] No dead config keys or env vars for removed modes (e.g., CONNECTOR_FILTER_REFRESH_INTERVAL_S if replaced by INGESTION_POLICY_REFRESH_INTERVAL_S)
- [ ] All tests exercise the new IngestionPolicyEvaluator and /ingestion-rules API directly — no tests for old interfaces remain
- [ ] All imports across the codebase use new module/function names

description:
Convert telegram_user_client and whatsapp_user_client from frequent (10-min) batch flushes to
periodic (30-min default, dashboard-configurable) batch connectors. Add a switchboard pipeline
decomposition step that extracts per-butler "conceptual messages" from conversation history
batches via signal-extraction, cherry-picking relevant message excerpts per concept and fanning
out to multiple butlers from a single ingestion event.

**Why:** Current 10-min flush interval produces ~36 LLM classification calls/day/chat, and each
batch routes as a single unit to one butler — losing signal when conversations span multiple
domains. This change reduces call frequency 3x and enables multi-butler routing from conversation
batches.

**OpenSpec change:** openspec/changes/batch-conversation-connectors/
- proposal.md — motivation, capability mapping
- design.md — 6 key technical decisions, migration plan
- specs/ — 5 capability specs (2 new, 3 modified)
- tasks.md — 19 implementation tasks across 5 groups

**Key decisions (from design.md):**
- D1: Envelope tagged with control.payload_type="conversation_history"
- D2: Decomposition runs post-persist in pipeline.process(), not ingest_v1()
- D3: Signal-extraction invoked as direct LLM API call, not via CC skill framework
- D4: Cherry-picked excerpts per conceptual message, duplicates OK across concepts
- D5: Dashboard-configurable flush_interval_s with live reload (no restart)
- D6: Empty decomposition ([]) → log only, no LLM classification invoked
title: Batch conversation connectors with switchboard decomposition

description:
## Problem

The test suite has ballooned to 13,675 tests across 531 files (313k LOC). This is
LLM-workflow bloat — most tests verify mock wiring, error message strings, or
internal implementation details rather than behavioral contracts.

Key bloat areas:
- Memory module: 70 files, 1,544 tests (11% of suite) for one module
- Connectors: 51 files + root files, 2,615 tests — almost entirely mock-wiring
- API: 78 files, 1,779 tests — field-by-field response assertions
- Core + Daemon: 84 files, 1,869 tests
- Migrations: ~25 files, 749 tests — testing SQL strings, not schema outcomes
- 713 of 531 files use mocking; 1,502 assertions check error message strings
- 2,810 mock-wiring assertions (assert_called, call_count, call_args)

## Test Budget (13,675 total)

| Bead | Domain | Baseline | Target |
|------|--------|--------:|-------:|
| bu-zkrix | Contract extraction (NEW) | 0 | ~200 |
| bu-v7dn3 | Memory module | 1,544 | ~100 |
| bu-35fm7 | Connectors (incl root files) | 2,615 | ~150 |
| bu-eu6jh | Migrations (config+migrations+memory) | 749 | ~50 |
| bu-egmz6 | API tests | 1,779 | ~200 |
| bu-7sd7a | Modules non-memory | 2,170 | ~400 |
| bu-l1obx | Core + Daemon | 1,869 | ~400 |
| bu-s8hn8 | Unassigned dirs + root + sweep | 3,149 | ~600 |
| **TOTAL** | | **13,875** | **~2,100** |

Note: Sum exceeds 13,675 because some tests are counted in both their directory
AND the root-level connector files that will be relocated. Actual dedup happens
during execution.

## Goal

Reduce to ~1,500-2,200 tests organized in three contract tiers:

1. **Tier 1: Architectural Invariants** (~200 tests) — Heart-and-soul doctrine:
   15 invariants + RFC 0010/0011/0012 wire contracts. In tests/contracts/.

2. **Tier 2: Wire Contracts** (~500-800 tests) — RFC-defined schemas and state
   machines: envelope schemas, route inbox state machine, Module ABC contract,
   migration chain execution, API response contracts.

3. **Tier 3: Capability Behavior** (~800-1200 tests) — OpenSpec-driven. Each
   spec gets focused tests proving the capability works through its tool
   interface, not testing every internal helper.

## Execution Strategy

Phase 1 (bu-zkrix: contract extraction) MUST complete before any deletion work.
All condensation phases can then run in parallel as independent subagent domains.
bu-s8hn8 (sweep) runs after all domain beads. bu-ud62t (reconciliation) runs last.

## Anti-Patterns to Eliminate

- Testing mock return values (2,810 mock-wiring assertions)
- Asserting error message strings (1,502 occurrences — brittle, low-value)
- Per-function internal helper tests (1,310 private function imports in tests)
- Per-migration-step SQL string tests (test schema outcomes instead)
- Field-by-field API response assertions (use Pydantic schema validation)
- Duplicate file pairs (test_x.py + test_x_unit.py)
title: Condense test suite from 13,675 to ~2,000 contract-driven tests

description:
All connectors (Gmail, Telegram Bot, Telegram User Client, Discord) currently persist their polling cursor in a local JSON file via CONNECTOR_CURSOR_PATH. This causes cursor loss on restart, no dashboard visibility, and naming workarounds in dev.sh.

The DB already has the infrastructure: switchboard.connector_registry has checkpoint_cursor TEXT and checkpoint_updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ columns. All connectors already hold an asyncpg pool.

Goal: replace every file-based state code path with direct DB reads/writes. No backward compatibility — clean break. Remove CONNECTOR_CURSOR_PATH entirely from source, env files, dev.sh, and tests.

Scope:
- src/butlers/connectors/gmail.py, telegram_bot.py, telegram_user_client.py, discord_user.py
- roster/switchboard/migrations/013_create_connector_heartbeat_tables.py (schema already ready)
- roster/switchboard/api/router.py (new PATCH cursor endpoint)
- openspec/specs/connector-base-spec, connector-gmail, connector-telegram-bot, connector-telegram-user-client, connector-discord
- docs/connectors/interface.md, gmail.md, telegram_bot.md, telegram_user_client.md
- scripts/dev.sh, .env, secrets/connectors/*
title: Migrate connector state to DB-backed storage

description:
Connectors currently read/write their polling cursor to a local JSON file (_load_cursor, _save_cursor, _ensure_cursor_file). These must be replaced with direct reads/writes to switchboard.connector_registry.checkpoint_cursor via the connector's existing asyncpg pool.

Changes per connector:
- gmail.py: _save_cursor() upserts connector_registry.checkpoint_cursor + checkpoint_updated_at. _ensure_cursor_file() becomes _ensure_cursor() — reads from DB first; if null, initializes from Gmail historyId API and writes to DB.
- telegram_bot.py: _save_checkpoint() and _load_checkpoint() rewritten to use DB. Remove temp-file-rename pattern.
- telegram_user_client.py: same pattern as telegram_bot.
- discord_user.py: cursor is a per-channel dict (JSON) — store as JSON string in checkpoint_cursor. _load_checkpoint() reads and json.loads(); _save_checkpoint() json.dumps() and upserts.

The connector_registry row must exist before a cursor write. Use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (upsert) keyed on (connector_type, endpoint_identity).

Heartbeat is unaffected — it already reads self._last_history_id (in-memory) and reports to Switchboard MCP; leave that path alone.

Acceptance criteria:
1. All four connectors start successfully with no cursor file present and no CONNECTOR_CURSOR_PATH set
2. Cursor survives connector restart with no local file mounted
3. connector_registry.checkpoint_cursor is updated within one poll cycle of startup
4. No Path / file I/O imports remain in cursor-related methods
5. All existing connector tests pass (update mocks as needed)
notes: PR #510 created. Branch: agent/bu-rjt.1. All tests pass (7129 passed).
title: Replace file-based cursor with DB reads/writes in all connectors

description:
Allow operators to read and modify a connector's cursor directly from the dashboard at /butlers/ingestion?tab=connectors, without needing SSH or DB access.

Backend — roster/switchboard/api/router.py:
- Add PATCH /connectors/{connector_type}/{endpoint_identity}/cursor
- Body: {"cursor": "<value>"} (string, required, non-empty)
- Writes directly to connector_registry.checkpoint_cursor and checkpoint_updated_at = now()
- Returns updated ConnectorEntry
- Place alongside existing DELETE /connectors/{connector_type}/{endpoint_identity}

Frontend — frontend/src/components/ingestion/ (find the connector detail component):
- Show current checkpoint_cursor value in the connector detail panel (already in API response)
- Add Edit button next to the cursor value
- Opens inline text field pre-filled with current cursor value + Save / Cancel buttons
- On Save: confirmation dialog: 'Changing the cursor affects which messages are ingested on the next connector restart. Continue?'
- On confirm: PATCH request, refresh connector data on success
- Cursor is only read on connector startup — note this in the UI (e.g., tooltip: 'Takes effect on next connector restart')

Acceptance criteria:
1. PATCH endpoint returns 200 with updated connector data on valid input
2. PATCH endpoint returns 400 if cursor is empty or missing
3. PATCH endpoint returns 404 if connector not in registry
4. UI shows current cursor value at all times
5. Edit flow includes confirmation dialog before write
6. UI note informs operator that change takes effect on next restart
notes: PR #516 created. Backend PATCH endpoint + frontend cursor edit UI.
title: Add cursor edit UI to connector detail page

**Implementation:**
- Extend entity existence check to also fetch entity_type: SELECT id, entity_type FROM shared.entities WHERE id = $1 (no additional query — same round-trip)
- After registry lookup, compare actual types against expected types
- If mismatch, append to a warnings list: 'Expected subject type person but got organization'
- Propagate warnings through response: {id: ..., warnings: [...]}
- NULL expected types skip validation (no warning)

2. Target behavior from docs/modules/memory.md
3. OpenSpec change artifacts (proposal, design, specs, tasks)
4. Backward-compatibility constraints from live module surface

Key architectural constraints:
- Canonical memory remains local to each butler schema
- shared.entities is the sole authoritative entity registry (already done via core_014)
- No dedicated shared memory service
- No direct cross-butler SQL reads into another butler's memory tables
- All MCP tool changes are additive with safe defaults
- Every migration must have tests; a migration without tests is incomplete

Phased execution (5 migrations + code changes):
Phase 1: Immediate code fixes (consolidation bug + effective_confidence)
Phase 2: mem_014 — tenant/request lineage columns + tenant-scoped indexes
Phase 3: mem_015 — consolidation lease-based state machine
Phase 4: mem_016 + mem_017 — temporal fact safety + retention policy tables
Phase 5: core_019 — shared.memory_catalog (discovery-only, feature-flagged)

Acceptance:
1. shared.entities is sole authoritative entity table (already true)
2. All durable memory writes stamp tenant_id and support request_id
3. Retrieval and min_confidence use effective_confidence (decayed)
4. Consolidation is lease-based, reaches exactly one terminal state per episode
5. memory_context is deterministic, sectioned, and budgeted
6. Retention classes exist and are respected by cleanup
7. Temporal facts support idempotency/invalidation safely
8. Tests cover migration correctness, tenant isolation, retrieval determinism, and consolidation invariants
9. Optional shared discovery catalog remains discovery-only
title: Align memory module implementation with target-state spec

description: The contacts module currently only supports Google Contacts as a sync source. Telegram contacts are a valuable identity source containing phone numbers, usernames, and chat IDs that enable cross-provider identity resolution. This epic adds Telegram as a second contacts provider with proper disambiguation across sources. Telethon and user-client credentials already exist.
title: Add Telegram contacts sync provider

2. module-contacts/spec.md documents multi-provider configuration (provider list or multiple provider sections)
3. contacts-identity/spec.md documents telegram_username, telegram_user_id, telegram_chat_id contact_info types
4. contacts-identity/spec.md documents cross-provider disambiguation scenarios (Google+Telegram merge by phone)
5. Spec scenarios cover: new Telegram-only contact, Telegram contact matching existing Google contact by phone, ambiguous name-only match, Telegram contact deletion/removal
description:
Update the specification in openspec/ to document:

1. Multi-provider support for the contacts module — the provider field becomes a list or the module supports multiple provider configs, allowing both Google and Telegram to sync concurrently.

2. TelegramContactsProvider contract — a new provider implementing ContactsProvider that fetches contacts via Telethon client.get_contacts(). Document the credential resolution (telegram_api_id, telegram_api_hash, telegram_user_session from shared.contact_info), sync semantics (no sync token — Telegram returns full contact list each time, so incremental sync uses local hash comparison), and the CanonicalContact field mapping from Telegram contacts.

3. Telegram-specific contact_info types — document the new contact_info type values:
   - telegram_username: the user's @handle (e.g., '@alice')
   - telegram_user_id: the numeric Telegram user ID (stable across username changes)
   - telegram_chat_id: the private chat ID for DMs with this contact

4. Cross-provider disambiguation — document how the identity resolution pipeline (ContactBackfillResolver) handles contacts arriving from multiple providers. The resolution order (source_link → email → phone → name) already supports this, but the spec should explicitly describe:
   - A Telegram contact matching an existing Google contact by phone number merges into the same CRM record
   - Telegram-specific contact_info entries are added alongside Google-sourced entries on the merged contact
   - Provenance tracking in metadata JSONB records which fields came from which provider
   - Ambiguous matches (e.g., name-only with multiple candidates) skip auto-merge and flag for dashboard disambiguation

Update specs: module-contacts/spec.md, contacts-identity/spec.md, and potentially connector-telegram-user-client/spec.md.
title: Update openspec for multi-provider contacts and Telegram sync

acceptance_criteria:

2. Provider fetches contacts via Telethon client.get_contacts() using existing credential infrastructure
3. Telegram username, user ID, and chat ID stored in shared.contact_info with appropriate type values
4. Phone-based disambiguation merges Telegram contacts with existing Google contacts into same CRM record
5. Name-only matches with multiple candidates produce ambiguous_name strategy (no auto-merge)
6. 'telegram' added to _SUPPORTED_PROVIDERS; config accepts provider='telegram' in butler.toml
7. Incremental sync detects added/changed/removed contacts via local hash comparison
8. Sync state persisted per provider='telegram' account
9. Provenance tracked in metadata JSONB under sources.contacts.telegram namespace

description:
attached_molecule: bu-wisp-hq94
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-03-06T04:17:28Z
dispatched_by: mayor

Add a TelegramContactsProvider that implements the ContactsProvider interface to sync contacts from the user's Telegram account via Telethon.

Key implementation details:

**Provider (sync.py or new telegram_provider.py)**:
- Implement ContactsProvider with name='telegram'
- Use Telethon client.get_contacts() to fetch the user's full Telegram contact list
- Credential resolution via resolve_owner_contact_info() for telegram_api_id, telegram_api_hash, telegram_user_session (already stored in shared.contact_info)
- Full sync: fetch all contacts, return as ContactBatch with CanonicalContact instances
- Incremental sync: Telegram has no sync token API — fetch full list and compare against locally stored hashes (contact_versions in sync state) to detect additions/changes/deletions
- Map Telegram contact fields to CanonicalContact: first_name, last_name, phone → ContactPhone, username → ContactUsername

**Telegram-specific contact_info entries**:
- telegram_username: the @handle (e.g., '@alice') — stored as ContactUsername in CanonicalContact, written to shared.contact_info with type='telegram_username'
- telegram_user_id: numeric Telegram user ID (stable identifier) — stored in shared.contact_info with type='telegram_user_id'
- telegram_chat_id: private chat ID for this contact — requires calling client.get_input_entity() or resolving from dialogs — stored in shared.contact_info with type='telegram_chat_id'

**Disambiguation / Identity Resolution**:
- The existing ContactBackfillResolver resolution order (source_link → email → phone → name) handles cross-provider merging naturally:
  - Phone match: a Telegram contact with the same phone as a Google contact merges into the existing CRM record
  - Name match: conservative ILIKE match, ambiguous results flagged for dashboard disambiguation
- Telegram-specific contact_info rows are added to the matched/merged contact alongside Google-sourced entries
- Provenance tracked under metadata JSONB namespace 'sources.contacts.telegram.{field}'

**Module registration**:
- Add 'telegram' to ContactsModule._SUPPORTED_PROVIDERS
- Support provider config: allow provider='telegram' or multi-provider configuration in butler.toml
- Credential validation: verify Telethon session is valid before starting sync loop

**Sync runtime**:
- Telegram contact sync runs on the same polling interval as Google (configurable, default 15 min)
- Supports contacts_sync_now MCP tool trigger
- Sync state persisted with provider='telegram' in contacts_sync_state
design:
The TelegramContactsProvider should live alongside GoogleContactsProvider, either in sync.py or in a new telegram_provider.py if the file size warrants it. The Telethon client session should be created once during validate_credentials() and reused across sync cycles. The client must be properly disconnected on shutdown.

For chat_id resolution: Telethon's client.get_contacts() returns User objects with id and username but not the private chat_id. To get chat_ids, iterate client.iter_dialogs() and match by user_id. This can be done lazily or as a post-sync enrichment step to avoid blocking the main sync loop.

Multi-provider support: the simplest approach is allowing ContactsModule to instantiate multiple providers and run them in the same sync runtime. Each provider has independent sync state keyed by (provider, account_id). The ContactsSyncEngine already supports this via the provider parameter.
notes: Design decisions: (1) Create telegram_provider.py as separate file - sync.py is already large. (2) Telegram has no sync token, so full_sync returns all contacts with a timestamp-based cursor; incremental_sync fetches all contacts and engine's contact_versions hash handles dedup. (3) Credential resolution via resolve_owner_contact_info for telegram_api_id, telegram_api_hash, telegram_user_session. (4) list_groups returns empty GroupBatch (Telegram contacts don't have groups). (5) Chat ID resolution via iter_dialogs post-sync enrichment. (6) ContactsModule.on_startup gains elif branch for telegram provider.
title: Implement TelegramContactsProvider for contacts sync

design:
# Spec-to-Code Reconciliation: Telegram Contacts Sync

## Methodology
Compared specs (module-contacts/spec.md, contacts-identity/spec.md, connector-telegram-user-client/spec.md) against implementation (telegram_provider.py, contacts/__init__.py, backfill.py).

## Requirement Coverage Matrix

### module-contacts/spec.md

| Requirement | Bead | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ContactsModule Configuration (multi-provider `providers` list) | bu-s21.2 | **GAP** | Code uses single `provider` string, not `providers` list. No multi-provider config parsing. |
| Legacy single-provider config compat | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | Current code IS the single-provider form |
| Unsupported provider at startup | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | on_startup() line 401-405 |
| Sync disabled behavior | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | on_startup() line 408-409 |
| Google OAuth Credential Resolution | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | lines 470-509 |
| Provider-Agnostic Sync Contract | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | ContactsProvider ABC in sync.py |
| Canonical Contact Model | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | CanonicalContact in sync.py |
| TelegramContactsProvider - credential resolution | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | _create_telegram_provider lines 511-560 |
| TelegramContactsProvider - full sync | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | telegram_provider.py lines 140-169 |
| TelegramContactsProvider - external_id format | bu-s21.2 | **GAP** | Spec: `"telegram:<user_id>"`, Code: bare `user_id` string |
| TelegramContactsProvider - incremental sync hash-based cursor | bu-s21.2 | **GAP** | Spec: hash-based with empty batch optimization. Code: timestamp cursor, always returns full list |
| TelegramContactsProvider - deletion detection | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | No tombstones, engine-level detection |
| TelegramContactsProvider - contact without phone | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | Empty phones list |
| TelegramContactsProvider - validate_credentials | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | Uses client.get_me() |
| TelegramContactsProvider - list_groups | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | Returns empty GroupBatch |
| Multi-Provider Sync Runtime | — | **GAP** | Not implemented. Single _provider/_runtime. No independent loops, failure isolation, or per-provider MCP responses |
| Sync State Persistence | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | ContactsSyncStateStore |
| Sync Runtime with Polling Loop | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | ContactsSyncRuntime |
| CRM Backfill Pipeline | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | ContactBackfillEngine |
| Identity Resolution Pipeline | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | ContactBackfillResolver |
| MCP Tool Surface (4 tools) | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | Single-provider only |
| Shared Schema Tables | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | shared.contact_info |
| Cross-Provider Contact Backfill (Telegram+Google merge) | — | **PARTIAL** | Resolution pipeline supports it, but Telegram contact_info types not written correctly |
| Apple/CardDAV Provider | — | TARGET-STATE | Not current |

### contacts-identity/spec.md (Telegram-specific types)

| Requirement | Bead | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| telegram_user_id stored as contact_info | — | **GAP** | Backfill engine never creates telegram_user_id entries from external_id |
| telegram_username stored without @ prefix | bu-s21.2 | **GAP** | Code stores `@username` (with @); spec says without @. Also stored as type='other' not 'telegram_username' |
| telegram_chat_id stored as contact_info | bu-s21.2 | **GAP** | enrich_chat_ids() exists but is never called from sync pipeline |
| Cross-provider disambiguation | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | Resolution order works for multi-provider via phone match |

## Identified Gaps (require new beads)

### Gap 1: Multi-provider configuration and runtime
Spec defines `providers` as a list. Code uses single `provider` string. No independent sync loops, failure isolation, or per-provider MCP tool responses.

### Gap 2: Telegram-specific contact_info types not populated by backfill
- telegram_user_id entries never created (backfill doesn't know about it)
- telegram_username stored as type='other' with label='telegram' instead of type='telegram_username'
- telegram_chat_id entries never populated (enrich_chat_ids() never called)
- Username value includes @ prefix (spec says without @)

### Gap 3: external_id format mismatch
Spec: `"telegram:<user_id>"`. Code: bare numeric string. Affects source_link matching uniqueness.

### Gap 4: Hash-based incremental sync cursor optimization
Spec says compute hash, return empty batch if unchanged. Code uses timestamp cursor and always returns full list. Functionally correct via engine dedup but misses optimization.
notes:
Reconciliation complete. 4 gap beads created under epic bu-s21:

1. bu-fxu (P1 bug): Telegram contact_info types not populated — telegram_user_id, telegram_username, telegram_chat_id entries missing or incorrect
2. bu-a6o (P2 bug): external_id format mismatch — bare user_id vs spec's "telegram:<user_id>" prefix
3. bu-j4t (P2 feature): Multi-provider config/runtime — single provider only, spec requires providers list with independent sync loops
4. bu-p4g (P3 task): Hash-based incremental sync cursor optimization — currently uses timestamp, always returns full list

bu-s21.3 is now blocked by all 4 gap beads. Will be closable when all gaps are resolved.

Key findings:
- Core TelegramContactsProvider (full_sync, validate_credentials, list_groups) is solid
- Credential resolution from owner contact_info works correctly
- Identity resolution pipeline is provider-agnostic and supports cross-provider merging
- The gaps are in the integration layer: how Telegram-specific data flows through the backfill engine into contact_info
title: Reconcile spec-to-code coverage for Telegram contacts sync

description:
Add Steam Web API integration to Butlers following the four-component pattern: account registry, module (MCP tools), connector (background ingestion), and dashboard (management UI + analytics).

Post-v1 feature. Blocked by the Lifestyle butler epic (bu-d657) which must complete first.

Fully spec'd in openspec/changes/steam-integration/:
- Proposal: openspec/changes/steam-integration/proposal.md
- Design: openspec/changes/steam-integration/design.md (7 key decisions)
- Specs: 4 capability specs (steam-account-registry, module-steam, connector-steam, dashboard-steam)
- Tasks: openspec/changes/steam-integration/tasks.md (38 tasks across 8 groups)

Components:
1. public.steam_accounts registry with companion entities and API key credential storage
2. module-steam: 9 read-only MCP tools (player summaries, owned games, achievements, friends, news, etc.)
3. connector-steam: multi-account polling connector with 5 data types (recently played, achievements, status, friends, library), full base contract compliance
4. dashboard-steam: account CRUD, playtime analytics API, connector config UI, settings page integration

Key design decisions:
- API key auth (no OAuth) — keys entered via dashboard, validated on connect
- Polling-only (no push/webhook) — per-data-type intervals from 5min to 24h
- Delta detection via state diffing (Steam has no change feed/history API)
- All config via dashboard settings, no environment variables
- Play history stored in connectors.steam_play_history for analytics queries

Requires RFC 0003 amendment (gaming/steam channel/provider pair) and RFC 0004 amendment (steam_api_key entity_info type).
title: Steam Web API connector and module integration

description:
Create src/butlers/steam/__init__.py and src/butlers/steam/client.py with a shared SteamAPIClient class used by both the module and connector.

Implementation:
- Wraps httpx.AsyncClient for async HTTP to Steam Web API
- API key injection via query parameter
- Response unwrapping (Steam returns {"response": {...}} wrappers)
- Rate limit detection: exponential backoff on 429/403, starting 60s, max 3600s
- Batch support for GetPlayerSummaries (up to 100 SteamIDs per call, auto-split)
- Base URL: https://api.steampowered.com/{interface}/{method}/v{version}/

Unit tests (mocked HTTP via httpx MockTransport or respx):
- Response parsing and unwrapping
- Error handling (403, 429, 5xx, network errors)
- Batch splitting for >100 SteamIDs
- Rate limit backoff timing

Covers: openspec/changes/steam-integration/design.md (Decision D3: Single SteamAPIClient)
Covers: openspec/changes/steam-integration/tasks.md (Section 1)

2. API key never appears in logs (use httpx event hooks to redact)
3. Batch GetPlayerSummaries auto-splits at 100 IDs

5. All unit tests pass with mocked HTTP

description:
Implement src/butlers/connectors/steam.py — the most complex component. Multi-account polling connector with full connector base contract compliance.

Core:
- Multi-account discovery from public.steam_accounts (active accounts only)
- Per-account independent asyncio loops with per-data-type intervals
- Dynamic account rescan (default 300s, configurable via dashboard settings)
- Per-account error isolation — one account's failure doesn't affect others

5 pollers with delta detection (state diffing, no Steam history API):
1. Recently played games (5min) — playtime delta → play_session events + steam_play_history upsert
2. Achievements (15min) — per tracked game, set diff → achievement_unlock events
3. Online status (5min) — persona_state + gameextrainfo diff → status_change events
4. Friends (60min) — SteamID set diff → friend_change events
5. Game library (24h) — owned app_id set diff → game_purchase events (baseline-on-first-run)

ingest.v1 envelopes for all 5 event types with:
- source.channel="gaming", source.provider="steam"
- Correct external_event_id format per spec
- control.idempotency_key = external_event_id
- control.policy_tier="default", control.ingestion_tier="full"

Base contract compliance (critical — see connector-base-obligations reference):
- Filtered event batch flush to connectors.filtered_events after each poll cycle
- Replay queue drain loop (10 per cycle, FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED)
- Source filter gate via IngestionPolicyEvaluator (filter keys: app_id, event_type, sender_identity)
- Heartbeat protocol (connector.heartbeat.v1 at configurable interval)
- Prometheus metrics (6 metric families per spec)
- Health status aggregation with SteamID redaction
- Cursor persistence in connectors.steam_cursors (crash-safe resume)

Covers: openspec/changes/steam-integration/specs/connector-steam/spec.md (all requirements)

Acceptance criteria:
1. Connector discovers and polls all active Steam accounts
2. All 5 pollers detect changes via state diffing and emit correct ingest.v1 events
3. First poll establishes baseline without emitting events
4. Play history upserted to connectors.steam_play_history on playtime deltas
5. Filtered events flushed after each poll cycle
6. Replay queue drained after each poll cycle
7. Source filter gate blocks events matching active rules
8. Heartbeat sent at configured interval
9. All 6 Prometheus metric families exported
10. Health endpoint returns aggregated status with redacted SteamIDs
11. Crash recovery loads cursors and resumes without duplicate events
12. All tested with mocked Steam API
title: Implement Steam connector with polling, delta detection, and base contract

description:
Dashboard REST API routes and UI components for Steam account management, connector configuration, and playtime analytics.

Account management API:
- POST /api/steam/accounts — connect (validate API key via test call, create account + companion entity)
- GET /api/steam/accounts — list connected accounts (no credentials in response)
- PUT /api/steam/accounts/<id>/primary — set primary
- DELETE /api/steam/accounts/<id> — disconnect (soft) / hard_delete=true (cascade)
- GET /api/steam/accounts/<id>/status — has_api_key, key_valid, last_poll_at, connector_health

Playtime analytics API:
- GET /api/steam/playtime?days=7 — total_minutes, per-game breakdown, daily series
- GET /api/steam/playtime/<app_id>?days=30 — per-game detail with average_daily_minutes
- Scoped to primary account by default, account_id param override

Connector config UI (at /butlers/settings under Steam card):
- Configurable: rescan interval, heartbeat interval, max tracked games, per-data-type poll intervals
- Per-account overrides stored in metadata JSONB
- No environment variables — all config via dashboard

Connector health proxy:
- GET /api/steam/connector/health — proxies connector health endpoint

UI components:
- Steam integration card on settings page with avatar, display name, SteamID, primary badge
- Connect form with SteamID input, masked API key input, validate-and-connect button
- Link to https://steamcommunity.com/dev/apikey for key registration

Covers: openspec/changes/steam-integration/specs/dashboard-steam/spec.md (all requirements)

Acceptance criteria:
1. All 7 API endpoints respond correctly
2. API key validation calls ISteamUser/GetPlayerSummaries on connect
3. Invalid key returns 400 with actionable error and registration link
4. Playtime queries return correct aggregates from connectors.steam_play_history
5. Connector config changes persist and are picked up by connector on rescan
6. No credential material in any API response
7. Pydantic request/response models for all endpoints
8. Tests cover account CRUD, key validation errors, playtime queries

description:
Findings from a full project direction analysis (2026-03-16) identified that
Butlers has strong architectural bones but is expanding horizontally (more
butlers, more migration fronts) when it needs to go deep vertically — fixing
correctness gaps and adding operational guardrails in the existing core.

This epic addresses the 4 highest-priority, lowest-risk, immediately-tractable
items from the direction review:

1. Memory module has 8 known correctness gaps (retention not persisting, tenant
   safety holes, missing migrations) — every session touches memory
2. No global spawn concurrency cap — burst triggers can exhaust resources
3. No validation that scheduled tasks include notify() — tasks succeed silently
4. No release automation — every deployment is a one-way door with no rollback

All 4 children are independent and parallelizable.

Related prior work: bu-8i7g (GPT-5.4-pro review fixes).
Parallel track: bu-psac (DiscretionDispatcher migration) — already its own
epic, not duplicated here.

Direction review also identified work to NOT do yet:
- CRUD-to-SPO across 4 domains (validate on one domain first)
- Mature Education/Home/Travel butlers (freeze at config-only)
- Model benchmark framework (catalog needs usage first)
- Multi-domain switchboard decomposition (no real failure case yet)
title: Stabilize core correctness and operational guardrails

close_reason: All 8 memory correctness gaps already implemented on main — verified by audit (store_episode retention, tenant grouping, tenant propagation, event tenant_id, migrations 022/core_023/core_024, integration tests, event enrichment)
description:
The memory module has 8 known correctness and completeness gaps documented in
openspec/changes/memory-residual-gaps/proposal.md and tasks.md. These undermine
multi-tenant correctness, retention-aware cleanup, and audit completeness.

Covers: openspec/changes/memory-residual-gaps/ (all 8 tasks)

Gaps (by priority):

HIGH:
1. Episode retention persistence — store_episode() accepts retention_class and
   sensitivity but doesn't include them in the INSERT. Every episode gets
   migration defaults instead of caller values.
   Files: src/butlers/modules/memory/storage.py

2. Consolidation executor tenant propagation — execute_consolidation() calls
   store_fact()/store_rule() without tenant_id/request_id. Consolidation-derived
   knowledge loses tenant lineage.
   Files: src/butlers/modules/memory/consolidation_executor.py

MEDIUM:
3. Consolidation tenant-safe grouping — runner groups by butler only, not
   (tenant_id, butler), mixing tenants.
   Files: src/butlers/modules/memory/consolidation.py

4. Memory events tenant_id in consolidation — both success and failure event
   inserts from consolidation omit tenant_id.
   Files: consolidation.py, consolidation_executor.py

MIGRATIONS:
5. Add mem_021: memory_events enrichment columns (request_id, memory_type,
   memory_id, actor_butler) + embedding_versions table
6. Add mem_022: shared.memory_catalog migration (runtime code references this
   table but no migration creates it)

TESTS:
7. Migration integration tests — apply full mem chain (mem_001→mem_022) to real
   PostgreSQL, verify schema correctness, run store cycle with tenant_id
8. Update consolidation event inserts for enrichment columns (actor_butler,
   memory_type, memory_id)

Acceptance criteria:
1. store_episode() persists caller-provided retention_class and sensitivity
2. Consolidation groups episodes by (tenant_id, butler), not butler alone
3. Consolidation-derived facts/rules inherit source tenant_id
4. All consolidation event inserts include tenant_id
5. mem_021 migration applies cleanly (enrichment columns + embedding_versions)
6. mem_022 migration creates shared.memory_catalog with correct constraints
7. Migration integration test suite passes against real PostgreSQL
8. All existing memory tests continue to pass
title: Fix memory module correctness gaps (8 issues)

description:
Each butler's Spawner has its own asyncio.Semaphore (spawner.py:470) controlling
per-butler concurrency (default max_concurrent_sessions=1). But there is no
pool-wide cap across all butlers. If 10 butlers all trigger simultaneously,
10 LLM CLI instances spin up concurrently with no backpressure — exhausting
system memory and API rate limits.

The fix is a shared semaphore at the daemon/process level that all Spawner
instances acquire before spawning. This caps total concurrent LLM instances
regardless of how many butlers trigger.

Key file: src/butlers/core/spawner.py (class Spawner, lines 421-665)

Design:
- Add a module-level or daemon-injected asyncio.Semaphore shared across all
  Spawner instances in the process
- Configurable via BUTLERS_MAX_GLOBAL_SESSIONS env var or config, default 3
- Spawner acquires global semaphore BEFORE its per-butler semaphore
- Log when a spawn is queued waiting for the global cap
- Add a metric (spawner_global_queue_depth) for observability

Acceptance criteria:
1. With 5 butlers triggering simultaneously and global cap=3, at most 3 LLM
   instances run concurrently (others queue)
2. Global cap is configurable via env var or config
3. Queuing events are logged at INFO level
4. Per-butler semaphore behavior is unchanged (still enforced after global)
5. Existing spawner tests pass without modification
6. New test validates global cap enforcement with multiple spawner instances
title: Add global spawn concurrency cap across all butlers

description:
Two small guardrails that prevent silent failures, merged into one bead to
avoid per-bead overhead (both are <1 day, touch different files, no overlap).

## Part A: Scheduled task notify() validation

Scheduled tasks run headless with no interactive user. If a dispatch_mode=prompt
task's prompt omits notify(), the task runs, succeeds, and the owner never
knows. There is no validation at config time or runtime.

Key file: src/butlers/core/scheduler.py (sync_schedules_from_toml, lines ~330-370)

Fix: During schedule sync at startup, when processing dispatch_mode=prompt
entries, check whether the prompt text or the linked skill file (if the prompt
references a skill by name from roster/{butler}/.agents/skills/) contains
'notify' (case-insensitive). If not, emit a WARNING log:
  "Scheduled task '{name}' has dispatch_mode=prompt but prompt/skill does not
   reference notify() — task results may not reach the user"

This is a WARNING, not an error — some tasks legitimately don't need to notify
(e.g., cleanup jobs). But the warning surfaces the common mistake.

Acceptance criteria (Part A):
1. Startup emits warning for prompt tasks missing notify() reference
2. Warning includes the task name for easy identification
3. Tasks that DO reference notify() produce no warning
4. dispatch_mode=job tasks are not checked (they have their own delivery)
5. Warning does not block startup

## Part B: Schema isolation integration tests

The one-db multi-schema migration is in flight (db.py:96). search_path is built
by schema_search_path() (db.py:58-67) as "{butler_schema},shared,public".
If this isolation leaks, Butler A can write to Butler B's tables.

No integration tests currently validate this isolation.

Key file: src/butlers/db.py (schema_search_path, Database class)

Fix: Add integration tests (using testcontainers or the existing fresh_db
fixture) that:
1. Create two butler schemas (e.g., butler_alpha, butler_beta) in one database
2. Create identical tables in both schemas
3. Insert data via a connection with search_path=butler_alpha,shared,public
4. Verify the data is NOT visible via search_path=butler_beta,shared,public
5. Verify shared schema data IS visible from both paths

Acceptance criteria (Part B):
1. Test proves cross-schema write isolation
2. Test proves shared schema is accessible from both butler paths
3. Tests use real PostgreSQL (not mocks)
4. Tests are marked with pytest.mark.db for CI gating
title: Add notify() validation and schema isolation tests

description:
The project has no release process. pyproject.toml is hardcoded to version 0.1.0.
There is no image registry, no changelog, no way to roll back to a known-good
state. Every deployment is a one-way door.

Key files:
- pyproject.toml (version field)
- .github/workflows/ (new release workflow)
- Dockerfile (image tagging)

Fix: Implement a minimal but functional release pipeline:

1. Version management — use a single source of truth for version
   (pyproject.toml). Adopt calver (YYYY.MM.patch) or semver. Include a
   Makefile target or script to bump version.

2. GitHub Actions release workflow — triggered on version tag push (v*).
   Builds Docker image, pushes to GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io).
   Tags image with version and 'latest'.

3. Changelog generation — use git-cliff or similar to generate changelog
   from conventional commits. Include in GitHub Release.

4. Rollback capability — with tagged images in GHCR, rollback is
   docker pull ghcr.io/tzeusy/butlers:v{previous}. Document this in
   a RELEASE.md or README section.

Non-goals:
- PyPI publishing (not a library)
- Helm charts / K8s manifests (not needed yet)
- Automated deployment to production (manual docker pull is fine)

Acceptance criteria:
1. Version is managed in one place (pyproject.toml)
2. Pushing a version tag triggers CI that builds + pushes Docker image to GHCR
3. Docker image is tagged with version number and 'latest'
4. Changelog is generated from commit history
5. RELEASE.md or README section documents rollback procedure
6. Existing CI workflow is not affected
title: Implement basic release automation with versioning and rollback

description:
## What

Own ALL test directories and files not explicitly claimed by domain-specific
beads. Reorganize, condense, or delete ~3,555 tests across 12 unassigned
directories + 48 root-level files.

## Why

After domain beads complete (memory, connectors, migrations, API, modules, core),
significant test areas remain unassigned. Without explicit ownership, these tests
fall through the cracks — defeating the condensation goal.

## Scope: Unassigned Directories (1,826 tests)

| Directory | Tests | Files | Strategy |
|-----------|------:|------:|----------|
| daemon/ | 416 | 27 | Keep ~80 behavioral tests (startup phases, shutdown, trigger dispatch). Contract tests extracted to tests/contracts/ by bu-zkrix. |
| adapters/ | 359 | 13 | Keep ~60 adapter contract tests. Delete internal helper tests. |
| jobs/ | 270 | 6 | Keep ~50 job scheduling/execution behavioral tests. |
| integration/ | 245 | 18 | PRESERVE — these are high-value cross-module integration tests. Condense to ~150. |
| tools/ | 232 | 17 | Keep ~40 tool framework tests through public API. |
| e2e/ | 93 | 21 | PRESERVE — end-to-end tests are high-value. Keep all ~93. |
| scripts/ | 71 | 3 | Keep ~20 essential script tests. |
| features/ | 51 | 4 | Keep ~30 feature-level behavioral tests. |
| cli/ | 43 | 5 | Keep ~20 CLI interface tests. |
| telemetry/ | 38 | 5 | Keep ~15 OTel instrumentation tests. |
| benchmarks/ | 8 | 11 | PRESERVE as-is (8 tests, already minimal). |

**Target for unassigned directories: ~566 tests (from 1,826)**

## Scope: Root-Level Files (1,729 tests in 48 files)

Key files to RELOCATE to domain beads (coordinate with domain bead owners):
- test_gmail_connector.py (207) → tests/connectors/ (bu-35fm7 scope)
- test_whatsapp_user_client.py (75) → tests/connectors/ (bu-35fm7 scope)
- test_google_calendar_connector.py (49) → tests/connectors/ (bu-35fm7 scope)

Key files to RELOCATE to core:
- test_ingestion_policy.py (120) → tests/core/
- test_insight_engine.py (79) → tests/core/
- test_routing_contracts.py (68) → tests/core/

Key files to DELETE (duplicates):
- test_context_bus.py (76) — duplicate of tests/core/test_context_bus.py

E2E files to consolidate:
- test_e2e_*.py (6 files, 176 tests) → tests/e2e/

Remaining root files: apply classification decision matrix, condense or delete.

**Target for root files: 0 files remaining at root level after relocation**

## Error Message Assertion Sweep (1,502 occurrences across entire suite)

After all domain beads and relocations complete, sweep surviving tests:
- Convert error-message string assertions to exception type assertions
- Delete pure string-check tests with no behavioral value

## Duplicate Resolution

- tests/core/test_context_bus.py + test_context_bus_unit.py → merge
- tests/core/test_seasonal.py + test_seasonal_unit.py → merge
- tests/config/test_switchboard_message_inbox_partition_migration.py + _unit.py → merge

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Zero test files in tests/ root (all moved to subdirectories or deleted)
2. All 12 unassigned directories have been audited and condensed
3. Unassigned directory tests reduced from 1,826 to ≤600
4. Error-message-only assertions reduced by ≥90% across entire suite
5. No duplicate file pairs remain
6. E2E tests consolidated into single tests/e2e/ directory
7. Full test suite still passes
8. Final total test count is within target range (~1,500-2,200)
title: Clean up root test files and final sweep of low-value tests

description:
Two spec violations in roster/health/tools/diet.py from bu-axm.6:

(1) scope='health' missing: meal_log calls store_fact without scope='health', defaulting to scope='global'. Spec (butler-health/spec.md) requires: 'the scope MUST be "health" to isolate meal facts'. Also, meal_history queries facts table without scope filter, returning facts from all scopes.

(2) Metadata format mismatch: Spec requires metadata JSONB to contain: estimated_calories (NUMBER), macros (OBJECT with protein_g, carbs_g, fat_g), logged_at (ISO 8601 timestamp), meal_items (ARRAY). Current implementation stores: nutrition (flat dict with calories/protein_g/carbs_g/fat_g) and notes. nutrition_summary also looks for metadata['nutrition'] not metadata['estimated_calories']/metadata['macros'].

Fixes needed:
- diet.meal_log: add scope='health' to store_fact call
- diet.meal_history: add scope='health' to facts query WHERE clause
- diet.meal_log: restructure metadata to match spec format (estimated_calories, macros dict, logged_at, meal_items)
- diet._fact_to_meal: update to read from spec-format metadata keys
- diet.nutrition_summary: update to read estimated_calories and macros.* from new metadata format
- Update tests in roster/health/tests/test_tools.py to reflect new metadata shape
title: Fix meal_log scope isolation and metadata format to match spec

description:
roster/travel/jobs/travel_jobs.py at lines 555-565 contains a live SQL query: SELECT 1 FROM shared.medications. This was an attempt by the travel butler to cross-schema access the health butler's medications table via a presumed 'shared.medications' view.

After core_046 drops the shared schema, this query will fail with 'schema shared does not exist'.

The code currently wraps the query in a try/except asyncpg.InvalidSchemaNameError block so it gracefully no-ops when the schema is absent. However, the correct long-term fix is to remove this cross-schema access pattern entirely, since:
1. The shared schema no longer exists
2. Cross-butler data access should be through MCP, not direct SQL

Fix options:
Option A (minimal): The try/except already catches asyncpg.InvalidSchemaNameError, so technically it degrades gracefully. Update the SQL to use a query that explicitly handles the missing schema without relying on shared.* (or remove the cross-schema query entirely).
Option B (proper): Remove the shared.medications cross-schema query entirely and replace with a 'no medications' default, adding a comment explaining that cross-butler health data should come through the MCP layer.

Recommended: Option B. The travel butler should not directly query health.medications across schema boundaries.

Files:
- roster/travel/jobs/travel_jobs.py:547-568

Acceptance criteria:
1. travel_jobs.py no longer references shared.medications in SQL
2. Medication prep insights either come through MCP or gracefully default to no-op
3. grep -rn 'shared.medications' returns zero hits
title: Fix roster/travel/jobs/travel_jobs.py querying shared.medications

description:
Comprehensive unit test suite for all conversation history buffering behavior.

Covers: All spec scenarios as test cases.

Tests to write (in tests/connectors/test_telegram_user_client.py or a new
test_telegram_user_client_history.py):

1. ChatBuffer creation, append, cap detection (200 message threshold)
2. _buffer_message routes to correct chat buffer, creates new buffer for unknown chat
3. Flush scanner identifies buffers past interval, skips empty/recent buffers
4. _fetch_conversation_history merges and deduplicates correctly
5. _resolve_reply_tos fetches missing reply-to messages, skips already-present ones
6. _build_batch_envelope produces correct ingest.v1 structure:
   - Correct external_event_id format ("batch:...")
   - conversation_history entries have all required fields
   - is_new flag correctly distinguishes buffered vs history messages
   - normalized_text contains only new messages with sender prefixes
7. _flush_chat_buffer end-to-end with mocked Telethon client
8. Graceful shutdown flushes all non-empty buffers
9. History fetch failure degrades gracefully (proceeds without context)
10. Force-flush on buffer cap exceeded

All tests should use mocked Telethon client (no real Telegram API calls).
Use pytest-asyncio for async test methods.

Acceptance criteria:
1. All 10 test areas covered with at least one test each
2. Tests pass with `uv run pytest tests/connectors/test_telegram_user_client*.py -q`
3. No tests depend on network or real Telegram credentials

File: tests/connectors/test_telegram_user_client.py (or new file)
Spec: openspec/changes/telegram-user-client-conversation-history/specs/telegram-user-client-conversation-history/spec.md
title: Add unit tests for conversation history buffering

description:
Spec requirement (dunbar-tier-scoring/spec.md, Requirement: Concentric circles Dunbar visualization, Scenario: People displayed within their tier ring — progressive detail): 'tier 50 entities MUST appear as labeled nodes with initials and name on hover'.

Current implementation: TierNode always shows initials text inside the circle. For showName=true (tiers 5/15), it adds a name label below. For tier 50+, showName=false so no name label is shown. However, there IS a Tooltip wrapper showing full name + tier info on hover (via TooltipContent) — so the name IS shown on hover via tooltip.

Assessment: This is essentially covered — the tooltip on every TierNode node provides name on hover for tier 50+. The spec says 'initials and name on hover' which is exactly what the tooltip provides. However, the tooltip also shows for tiers 5/15 (which already have name labels), and there is no distinction between 'initials only visible' vs 'initials visible + name on hover'.

This is a borderline-covered item. The tooltip mechanism satisfies 'name on hover'. No code gap to fix, but worth documenting as implemented via tooltip rather than a persistent label.
title: ConcentricCirclesDialog: tier 50 nodes should show name on hover (not just in tooltip)

description: DELETE /api/memory/entities/{id} uses soft-delete (metadata.deleted_at patch), bypassing the ON DELETE RESTRICT FK constraint on facts.entity_id. This means entities with active facts can be 'deleted' without warning. The frontend copy says 'Facts will be orphaned' which is inaccurate for soft-delete. Fix: either add a pre-check that blocks soft-delete when active facts exist, or update the UI copy to accurately describe soft-delete semantics.
title: Fix entity soft-delete to enforce fact-reference protection

description:
Deep-dive review: compare the original test condensation plan (see epic description)
against the implementation delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

Workflow:
1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Audit the codebase changes delivered by each sibling bead.
3. Produce a checklist mapping every condensation target to its implementing bead.
4. For any target NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
5. If gap beads were created in step 4, create a follow-up reconciliation bead
   (gen-2) that depends on ALL the new gap beads.
6. Verify final test count is within target range (~1,500-2,200).
7. Verify all three contract tiers are properly represented:
   - Tier 1: Architectural Invariants (~200 tests in tests/contracts/)
   - Tier 2: Wire Contracts (~500-800 tests)
   - Tier 3: Capability Behavior (~800-1200 tests)
8. Run full test suite and confirm green.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. Every condensation target in the epic spec has a corresponding bead
2. Any gaps found result in new child beads created under the same epic
3. If gaps were found, a follow-up reconciliation bead (gen-2) was created
4. Final test count documented in close reason
5. Reconciliation summary recorded in close reason

description:
Create all database migrations and refactor shared healing infrastructure for QA staffer.

**Database migrations (core Alembic chain, public schema):**
- public.qa_patrols table (UUIDv7 PK, started_at, completed_at, status, findings_count,
  novel_count, dispatched_count, log_lookback_minutes, sources_polled text[], error_detail)
- public.qa_findings table (UUIDv7 PK, patrol_id FK, fingerprint, source_type, source_butler,
  severity, exception_type, event_summary, call_site, occurrence_count, first_seen, last_seen,
  dedup_reason, healing_attempt_id FK, created_at)
- public.qa_dismissals table (fingerprint PK, dismissed_until, dismissed_by, created_at)
- Add nullable qa_patrol_id (UUIDv7 FK) column to public.healing_attempts (ADD COLUMN DEFAULT NULL,
  FK NOT VALID initially then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT separately to avoid long locks)
- public.v_qa_recent_failures read-only SQL view (sanctioned cross-schema exception per RFC 0010)
  — UNION across butler sessions tables for error/timeout/crash statuses, date-filtered,
  with explicit per-schema GRANT and health check. Must follow RFC 0010 five guardrails.

**Shared infrastructure refactors:**
- Extract create_healing_worktree() prefix parameter (default "self-healing", QA uses "qa")
- Extract PR pipeline label configurability from core/healing/worktree.py
- Extend create_or_join_attempt() in core/healing/tracking.py to accept optional qa_patrol_id

Covers: specs/staffer-qa (Patrol Database Schema), specs/qa-investigation-dispatch (Investigation
Creation), specs/self-healing-dispatch (Shared Dispatch Infrastructure, healing_attempts QA marker)

description:
## What

Rewrite the memory module test suite from 70 files / 1,544 tests down to ~100
tool-level behavioral tests. Test through the MCP tool interface
(store/recall/forget/search), not individual storage helpers.

## Why

The memory module is the single largest bloat area at 11% of the entire test
suite. Most tests exercise internal storage functions (storage_fact, storage_links,
storage_forget, storage_confirm, etc.) that are implementation details. The
behavioral contract is: "facts stored via MCP tools can be recalled, searched,
forgotten, and consolidated correctly."

## Current State (70 files)

Key file groups to consolidate:
- `tests/modules/memory/test_storage_*.py` (~20 files) — internal storage CRUD
- `tests/modules/memory/test_search_*.py` (~8 files) — search variations
- `tests/modules/memory/test_tools_*.py` (~8 files) — closest to behavioral
- `tests/modules/memory/test_consolidation_*.py` (~5 files) — consolidation internals
- `tests/modules/memory/test_*_migration*.py` (~3 files) — migration details

## Strategy

1. Keep `test_tools_*.py` files as the behavioral core — these test through MCP tools
2. For each storage_* file, identify which behaviors aren't covered by tool tests
3. Add missing behavioral coverage to tool-level tests
4. Delete storage_* files once all behaviors are covered at tool level
5. Consolidate search tests into one file testing each search mode
6. Consolidate migration tests into the migration chain bead's scope

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Memory tests reduced to ≤120 tests across ≤10 files
2. All tests exercise behavior through MCP tool interface or public API
3. No tests import private/internal storage functions directly
4. Full test suite still passes (no behavioral regression)
5. Coverage of memory tool operations: store, recall, search, forget, consolidate
title: Condense memory module tests from 1,544 to ~100

description:
Deep-dive review: compare the original QA Staffer spec (openspec/changes/qa-staffer/)
against the implementation delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

Workflow:
1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Audit the codebase changes delivered by each sibling bead.
3. Produce a checklist mapping every spec requirement to its implementing bead.
4. For any requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
5. If gap beads were created in step 4, create a follow-up reconciliation bead
   (gen-2) that depends on ALL the new gap beads. The gen-2 bead re-runs this
   same workflow to catch anything still missing after the gaps are filled.
   - A gen-2 reconciliation bead may spawn one final gen-3 reconciliation bead
     using the same pattern. Gen-3 is the hard limit — it must NOT spawn further
     reconciliation beads.
   - Tag the title with generation: "Reconcile spec-to-code (gen-N) for ..."
6. Keep this reconciliation bead open (or blocked) until all newly created
   gap beads (and any follow-up reconciliation bead) are closed.
7. Re-run the requirement-to-bead checklist and close this bead only when all
   requirements show full coverage.
8. If requirements show full coverage, run /opsx:sync to synchronize delta specs
   into the authoritative application specification, then run /opsx:archive to
   archive the completed change.

Specs to reconcile against:
- openspec/changes/qa-staffer/specs/staffer-qa/spec.md

description:
Implement Dunbar tier scoring as the relationship butler's foundational
prioritization model. Replaces the flat 30-day staleness threshold with
concentric social layers (5/15/50/150/500/1500), implicit decay scoring
from interaction history, and tier-weighted urgency ranking for reach-out
suggestions.

OpenSpec change: openspec/changes/dunbar-tier-scoring/
Spec: openspec/changes/dunbar-tier-scoring/specs/dunbar-tier-scoring/spec.md
Delta: openspec/changes/dunbar-tier-scoring/specs/butler-relationship/spec.md
Design: openspec/changes/dunbar-tier-scoring/design.md

Key decisions (from design.md):
- D1: Tier and score computed at query time from interaction facts
- D2: Exponential decay with 30-day half-life, summing all interactions
- D3: Rank-based tier assignment with hysteresis
- D4: Manual override via dunbar_tier_override SPO fact
- D5: Tier-aware cadences (14d→270d by tier)
- D6: Urgency formula: (days_overdue/cadence)*tier_weight + context_bonus
- D7: Internal scoring functions + one new dunbar_tier_set MCP tool
- D8: Entity API gets Dunbar data via shared scoring function with cross-schema read

Includes dashboard changes: entity list sorted by role+score, concentric
circles visualization dialog.
title: Dunbar tier scoring for relationship butler

description:
Database CRUD layer for shared.healing_attempts: state machine, atomic
novelty-check-and-insert (INSERT ON CONFLICT on partial unique index), gate
query functions for dispatch, fingerprint collision detection, daemon restart
recovery, and session_set_healing_fingerprint().

## Work Items (tasks.md §4)
1. Implement src/butlers/core/healing/tracking.py — CRUD for shared.healing_attempts
2. create_or_join_attempt() with INSERT ON CONFLICT for atomic novelty+insert,
   returns (attempt_id, is_new)
3. update_attempt_status() with updated_at, closed_at on terminal states,
   rejection of transitions from terminal states
4. Fingerprint collision detection: compare (exception_type, call_site) on join,
   log CRITICAL if mismatch
5. Gate query functions: get_active_attempt(), get_recent_attempt(),
   count_active_attempts(), get_recent_terminal_statuses()
6. list_attempts(pool, limit, offset, status_filter) for dashboard
7. session_set_healing_fingerprint(pool, session_id, fingerprint) in sessions.py
8. Daemon restart recovery: recover_stale_attempts(pool, timeout_minutes)
9. Tests for CRUD, gates, atomic race condition, collision detection, recovery

## Covers
- openspec/changes/butler-self-healing/specs/healing-session-tracking/spec.md — ALL requirements:
  → Healing Attempts Table, Atomic Attempt Creation, Healing Attempt State Machine,
    Session ID Accumulation, Fingerprint Collision Detection, Daemon Restart Recovery,
    Query Functions, Dashboard API Routes
- openspec/changes/butler-self-healing/specs/core-sessions/spec.md
  → Added Req: Healing Fingerprint Update (session_set_healing_fingerprint)
- openspec/changes/butler-self-healing/design.md §11 (Database schema), §13 (Daemon restart recovery)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. create_or_join_attempt() is atomic — concurrent inserts for same fingerprint
   produce exactly one investigating row (partial unique index enforced)
2. State machine enforces valid transitions; terminal states reject further transitions
3. Fingerprint collision logs CRITICAL when (exception_type, call_site) mismatches
4. recover_stale_attempts() transitions old investigating rows to timeout/failed
   and handles healing_session_id=NULL case
5. session_set_healing_fingerprint() updates sessions table (best-effort, non-fatal)
6. All gate query functions return correct results per spec scenarios
7. Verify implementation matches all scenarios in healing-session-tracking/spec.md
title: Implement healing session tracking with atomic gates and recovery

description: Currently _save_metadata_cache_to_store uses DO UPDATE SET settings = EXCLUDED.settings which replaces the entire settings JSONB column. Switch to jsonb_set to update only the metadata_cache key: DO UPDATE SET settings = jsonb_set(COALESCE(connector_registry.settings, '{}'::jsonb), '{metadata_cache}', EXCLUDED.settings -> 'metadata_cache'). Safe to defer since settings is not currently used for other keys in google_drive connector, but should be fixed before other code writes to settings for the same row. See PR #857 review: https://github.com/Tzeusy/butlers/pull/857#discussion_r2998748458
title: Harden _save_metadata_cache_to_store to use jsonb_set instead of full settings overwrite

description: SelfHealingModule.on_shutdown() cancels self._watchdog_tasks, but this list is never populated. The watchdog tasks for healing sessions are created inside dispatch_healing() via asyncio.create_task() with no reference surfaced to the caller. This means healing watchdog tasks (up to timeout_minutes, default 30 min) will continue running after daemon shutdown. Fix: dispatch_healing should return created background tasks so callers can register them for cancellation, or accept a task registry argument. Discovered during PR review of #682 (bu-y8zn).
title: fix: SelfHealingModule._watchdog_tasks is never populated; on_shutdown cannot cancel healing watchdogs

description:
Implement the live-listener connector — a standalone process that captures ambient audio
from local microphones, segments speech via Silero VAD, streams to a locally-hosted
faster-whisper transcription service (Wyoming protocol default), applies LLM-based
discretion filtering, and submits actionable utterances to the Switchboard as ingest.v1
envelopes.

**Transcription service:** faster-whisper via wyoming-faster-whisper, reachable at
`tcp://wyoming-faster-whisper.parrot-hen.ts.net:10300` on the tailnet.

**Spec source:** openspec/changes/connector-live-listener/
- proposal.md — motivation and scope
- design.md — architectural decisions (10 numbered decisions)
- specs/connector-live-listener/spec.md — 13 requirements with scenarios
- specs/connector-base-spec/spec.md — delta: voice/live-listener enum additions
- specs/connector-source-filter-enforcement/spec.md — delta: mic_id key type
- tasks.md — 35 implementation tasks across 9 groups

**Key design constraints:**
- Latency is #1 priority: <2s speech-to-submission target
- Wyoming protocol is the default transcription backend (tcp://wyoming-faster-whisper.parrot-hen.ts.net:10300)
- Discretion layer is fail-open with 3s timeout
- Single process manages multiple mic pipelines
- Audio is ephemeral — no recording, no replay on restart
title: Implement connector-live-listener for ambient voice ingestion

- Covers: `specs/connector-source-filter-enforcement/spec.md` → ADDED Requirement: Voice Connector Key Extraction

**Why first:** Every other bead depends on these enum values existing. Small, fast, foundational.

description:
Build the audio input pipeline: microphone capture via sounddevice/PortAudio and
speech segmentation via Silero VAD.

**What changes:**
- Add `sounddevice` and Silero VAD ONNX dependencies to pyproject.toml
- Implement `MicPipeline` class: sounddevice.InputStream callback → lock-free ring buffer
  (16kHz mono 16-bit PCM) → asyncio consumer
- Implement device enumeration/validation from LIVE_LISTENER_DEVICES JSON config
- Implement device reconnection with exponential backoff
- Implement VAD state machine (SILENCE ↔ SPEAKING) with configurable thresholds
- Implement segment duration bounds (min 300ms discard, max 30s force-split)
- Implement streaming handoff: begin forwarding audio to transcription on speech onset
- Tests for ring buffer, device management, VAD state transitions, segment bounds

**Spec references:**
- Covers: `specs/connector-live-listener/spec.md` → Requirement: Audio Capture
  → Scenario: Audio stream configuration — 16kHz mono 16-bit PCM format
  → Scenario: Ring buffer handoff — lock-free, overwrite-on-overflow
  → Scenario: Device enumeration and configuration — LIVE_LISTENER_DEVICES JSON
  → Scenario: Device reconnection — exponential backoff, pipeline pause
- Covers: `specs/connector-live-listener/spec.md` → Requirement: Voice Activity Detection
  → Scenario: VAD model and performance — Silero ONNX, <1ms per 30ms frame

description:
Build the abstract transcription client interface and three protocol implementations
for communicating with the external faster-whisper service.

The faster-whisper service runs as wyoming-faster-whisper on the tailnet, reachable at
`tcp://wyoming-faster-whisper.parrot-hen.ts.net:10300`.

**What changes:**
- Add `wyoming` Python package dependency to pyproject.toml
- Define `TranscriptionClient` abstract interface (async transcribe → TranscriptionResult)
- Implement `WyomingTranscriptionClient` (default): persistent TCP, Wyoming wire protocol
  (transcribe → audio-start → audio-chunk(s) → audio-stop → transcript), streaming during
  capture, reconnect with backoff
- Implement `WebSocketTranscriptionClient`: persistent WS connection, streaming chunks
- Implement `HttpTranscriptionClient`: POST complete segment per request
- Implement empty/low-confidence transcription discarding with metrics
- Implement graceful degradation: drop segments on unavailability, health → degraded
- Tests for all three clients (mock service), failure handling, confidence filtering

**Default configuration:**
LIVE_LISTENER_TRANSCRIPTION_URL=tcp://wyoming-faster-whisper.parrot-hen.ts.net:10300
LIVE_LISTENER_TRANSCRIPTION_PROTOCOL=wyoming

**Spec references:**
- Covers: `specs/connector-live-listener/spec.md` → Requirement: Transcription Client
  → Scenario: Protocol-agnostic interface — TranscriptionClient ABC, default "wyoming"
  → Scenario: Wyoming protocol client (default) — TCP, JSON+binary framing, message flow
  → Scenario: Wyoming streaming transcription — transcript-chunk events during audio
  → Scenario: WebSocket streaming client — persistent WS, streaming chunks
  → Scenario: HTTP POST fallback client — complete segment per request
  → Scenario: Transcription service unavailability — drop segments, no buffering, degraded
  → Scenario: Empty transcription handling — discard below MIN_CONFIDENCE (0.3)
- Covers: `specs/connector-live-listener/spec.md` → Requirement: Latency Budget
  → Scenario: Pipeline latency targets — audio handoff <50ms, transcription <1000ms

**OpenSpec tasks:** 4.1–4.8
title: Implement transcription client with Wyoming, WebSocket, and HTTP backends

description:
Build the LLM-based discretion filter that evaluates transcribed utterances in context
and decides whether they warrant butler attention. This replaces traditional wake-word
detection with contextual, organic relevance assessment.

**What changes:**
- Implement per-mic sliding context window bounded by DISCRETION_WINDOW_SIZE (10) and
  DISCRETION_WINDOW_SECONDS (300), whichever produces fewer entries
- Implement discretion LLM caller: send context window + latest utterance, parse
  FORWARD/IGNORE verdict with one-line reason
- Implement fail-open behavior: timeout (3s) and errors default to FORWARD
- Implement configurable LLM backend (LIVE_LISTENER_DISCRETION_LLM_URL and
  LIVE_LISTENER_DISCRETION_LLM_MODEL)
- Tests for window management, verdict parsing, timeout/failure handling, fail-open

**Spec references:**
- Covers: `specs/connector-live-listener/spec.md` → Requirement: Discretion Layer
  → Scenario: Sliding context window — bounded by size and time per mic
  → Scenario: Discretion evaluation — LLM call with context window, FORWARD/IGNORE verdict
  → Scenario: Discretion verdicts — FORWARD proceeds to ingest, IGNORE discards with metric
  → Scenario: Discretion layer failure — fail-open, default FORWARD on error/timeout
  → Scenario: Discretion latency constraint — 3s timeout, fail-open on exceed
  → Scenario: Privacy boundary — local vs. cloud LLM implications
- Covers: `specs/connector-live-listener/spec.md` → Requirement: Latency Budget
  → Scenario: Pipeline latency targets — discretion LLM call <800ms

**OpenSpec tasks:** 5.1–5.5
title: Implement discretion layer with sliding context window

description:
Deep-dive review: compare the original spec/requirements against the implementation
delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

**Spec source:** openspec/changes/connector-live-listener/
- specs/connector-live-listener/spec.md — 13 requirements, ~30 scenarios
- specs/connector-base-spec/spec.md — 2 modified scenarios
- specs/connector-source-filter-enforcement/spec.md — 4 added scenarios

Workflow:
1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Audit the codebase changes delivered by each sibling bead.
3. Produce a checklist mapping every spec requirement and scenario to its implementing bead.
4. For any requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
5. If gap beads were created in step 4, create a follow-up reconciliation bead
   (gen-2) that depends on ALL the new gap beads. The gen-2 bead re-runs this
   same workflow to catch anything still missing after the gaps are filled.
   - Gen-3 is the hard limit — it must NOT spawn further reconciliation beads.
6. Keep this reconciliation bead open (or blocked) until all newly created
   gap beads (and any follow-up reconciliation bead) are closed.
7. Re-run the requirement-to-bead checklist and close this bead only when all
   requirements show full coverage.
title: Reconcile spec-to-code coverage for connector-live-listener (gen-1)

description:
Create a single Alembic migration adding two new tables to the `shared` schema for the token limits feature.

## What
1. `shared.token_usage_ledger` — append-only ledger of per-session token consumption, range-partitioned on `recorded_at` with monthly partitions. Composite PK `(id, recorded_at)`, FK to `model_catalog(id)` with CASCADE, composite index on `(catalog_entry_id, recorded_at)`.
2. `shared.token_limits` — per-catalog-entry rolling-window budgets. UNIQUE on `catalog_entry_id`, FK CASCADE, nullable `limit_24h`/`limit_30d` (bigint), independent `reset_24h_at`/`reset_30d_at` (timestamptz).
3. If pg_partman extension is available, register the ledger for automatic monthly partition creation with 90-day retention. If not available, create 6 months of initial partitions and log a warning.

## OpenSpec References
- **Spec**: `openspec/changes/catalog-tier-token-limits/specs/catalog-token-limits/spec.md`
  - Requirement: "Token Usage Ledger Schema" — all 7 scenarios (entry structure, composite PK, index, partitioning with/without pg_partman, cascade, delete+recreate, discretion calls)
  - Requirement: "Token Limits Schema" — all 5 scenarios (entry structure, counting unit, no-limits-row semantics, cascade, disabled entry with override)
- **Design**: `openspec/changes/catalog-tier-token-limits/design.md`
  - Decision D2 — ledger schema with exact CREATE TABLE SQL and partitioning rationale
  - Decision D3 — limits table schema with exact CREATE TABLE SQL and reset_at semantics

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Alembic migration creates both tables with all columns, constraints, and indexes as specified in design D2 and D3
2. Ledger table is range-partitioned on `recorded_at` with monthly partitions
3. pg_partman registration succeeds when extension is available; graceful fallback (6-month buffer + warning) when not
4. Composite index `idx_ledger_entry_time` on `(catalog_entry_id, recorded_at)` exists
5. CASCADE deletes work: deleting a model_catalog entry removes corresponding ledger rows and limits row
6. Migration is reversible (downgrade drops both tables)
title: Add token_usage_ledger and token_limits DB schema

description: Extend the current EAV-style entity/fact system into a labeled property graph by adding inter-entity edges. Today, facts describe properties of a single entity (subject-predicate-content anchored via entity_id). The system cannot express typed, traversable relationships between two entities. Goal: Add optional object_entity_id FK to facts so a fact can represent either a property (entity_id only) or an edge (entity_id + object_entity_id). Enable multi-hop graph traversal via recursive CTEs. Update specs, storage, tools, and LLM prompts. Non-goals: No SPARQL/OWL/formal ontology. No external graph database. No inference engine. Stay on PostgreSQL with asyncpg.
title: Transition entity/fact system to labeled property graph

description:
entity_neighbors implementation has spec compliance gaps:

3. ENTITY EXISTENCE VALIDATION: module-memory spec line 948-950 says when called with
   non-existent entity_id, ValueError MUST be raised. Currently returns empty list silently.

description: Add entity_neighbors() tool function in src/butlers/modules/memory/tools/entities.py. Uses recursive CTE to traverse edge-facts (facts where object_entity_id IS NOT NULL). Parameters: entity_id (required), max_depth (default 2, cap at 5), predicate_filter (optional list of predicates to follow), direction (outgoing/incoming/both, default outgoing). Returns list of dicts with: entity (canonical_name, entity_type, id), predicate, depth, path. Register as MCP tool in memory module. Add tests for single-hop, multi-hop, filtered, and bidirectional traversal.
